title: microGPT, Pure Python
tagline: Karpathy's original microGPT, unmodified except for the dataset path. The complete algorithm in one file. Everything else is just efficiency.
code:
"""
The most atomic way to train and inference a GPT in pure, dependency-free Python.
This file is the complete algorithm.
Everything else is just efficiency.

@karpathy
"""

import os       # os.path.exists
import math     # math.log, math.exp
import random   # random.seed, random.choices, random.gauss, random.shuffle
random.seed(42) # Let there be order among chaos

# Let there be an input dataset `docs`: list[str] of documents (e.g. a dataset of names)
input_path = os.path.join(os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__)), '..', 'data', 'input.txt')
if not os.path.exists(input_path):
    import urllib.request
    names_url = 'https://raw.githubusercontent.com/karpathy/makemore/refs/heads/master/names.txt'
    urllib.request.urlretrieve(names_url, input_path)
docs = [l.strip() for l in open(input_path).read().strip().split('\n') if l.strip()] # list[str] of documents
random.shuffle(docs)
print(f"num docs: {len(docs)}")

# Let there be a Tokenizer to translate strings to discrete symbols and back
uchars = sorted(set(''.join(docs))) # unique characters in the dataset become token ids 0..n-1
BOS = len(uchars) # token id for the special Beginning of Sequence (BOS) token
vocab_size = len(uchars) + 1 # total number of unique tokens, +1 is for BOS
print(f"vocab size: {vocab_size}")

# Let there be Autograd, to recursively apply the chain rule through a computation graph
class Value:
    __slots__ = ('data', 'grad', '_children', '_local_grads') # Python optimization for memory usage

    def __init__(self, data, children=(), local_grads=()):
        self.data = data                # scalar value of this node calculated during forward pass
        self.grad = 0                   # derivative of the loss w.r.t. this node, calculated in backward pass
        self._children = children       # children of this node in the computation graph
        self._local_grads = local_grads # local derivative of this node w.r.t. its children

    def __add__(self, other):
        other = other if isinstance(other, Value) else Value(other)
        return Value(self.data + other.data, (self, other), (1, 1))

    def __mul__(self, other):
        other = other if isinstance(other, Value) else Value(other)
        return Value(self.data * other.data, (self, other), (other.data, self.data))

    def __pow__(self, other): return Value(self.data**other, (self,), (other * self.data**(other-1),))
    def log(self): return Value(math.log(self.data), (self,), (1/self.data,))
    def exp(self): return Value(math.exp(self.data), (self,), (math.exp(self.data),))
    def relu(self): return Value(max(0, self.data), (self,), (float(self.data > 0),))
    def __neg__(self): return self * -1
    def __radd__(self, other): return self + other
    def __sub__(self, other): return self + (-other)
    def __rsub__(self, other): return other + (-self)
    def __rmul__(self, other): return self * other
    def __truediv__(self, other): return self * other**-1
    def __rtruediv__(self, other): return other * self**-1

    def backward(self):
        topo = []
        visited = set()
        def build_topo(v):
            if v not in visited:
                visited.add(v)
                for child in v._children:
                    build_topo(child)
                topo.append(v)
        build_topo(self)
        self.grad = 1
        for v in reversed(topo):
            for child, local_grad in zip(v._children, v._local_grads):
                child.grad += local_grad * v.grad

# Initialize the parameters, to store the knowledge of the model.
n_embd = 16     # embedding dimension
n_head = 4      # number of attention heads
n_layer = 1     # number of layers
block_size = 16 # maximum sequence length
head_dim = n_embd // n_head # dimension of each head
matrix = lambda nout, nin, std=0.08: [[Value(random.gauss(0, std)) for _ in range(nin)] for _ in range(nout)]
state_dict = {'wte': matrix(vocab_size, n_embd), 'wpe': matrix(block_size, n_embd), 'lm_head': matrix(vocab_size, n_embd)}
for i in range(n_layer):
    state_dict[f'layer{i}.attn_wq'] = matrix(n_embd, n_embd)
    state_dict[f'layer{i}.attn_wk'] = matrix(n_embd, n_embd)
    state_dict[f'layer{i}.attn_wv'] = matrix(n_embd, n_embd)
    state_dict[f'layer{i}.attn_wo'] = matrix(n_embd, n_embd)
    state_dict[f'layer{i}.mlp_fc1'] = matrix(4 * n_embd, n_embd)
    state_dict[f'layer{i}.mlp_fc2'] = matrix(n_embd, 4 * n_embd)
params = [p for mat in state_dict.values() for row in mat for p in row] # flatten params into a single list[Value]
print(f"num params: {len(params)}")

# Define the model architecture: a stateless function mapping token sequence and parameters to logits over what comes next.
# Follow GPT-2, blessed among the GPTs, with minor differences: layernorm -> rmsnorm, no biases, GeLU -> ReLU
def linear(x, w):
    return [sum(wi * xi for wi, xi in zip(wo, x)) for wo in w]

def softmax(logits):
    max_val = max(val.data for val in logits)
    exps = [(val - max_val).exp() for val in logits]
    total = sum(exps)
    return [e / total for e in exps]

def rmsnorm(x):
    ms = sum(xi * xi for xi in x) / len(x)
    scale = (ms + 1e-5) ** -0.5
    return [xi * scale for xi in x]

def gpt(token_id, pos_id, keys, values):
    tok_emb = state_dict['wte'][token_id] # token embedding
    pos_emb = state_dict['wpe'][pos_id] # position embedding
    x = [t + p for t, p in zip(tok_emb, pos_emb)] # joint token and position embedding
    x = rmsnorm(x)

    for li in range(n_layer):
        # 1) Multi-head attention block
        x_residual = x
        x = rmsnorm(x)
        q = linear(x, state_dict[f'layer{li}.attn_wq'])
        k = linear(x, state_dict[f'layer{li}.attn_wk'])
        v = linear(x, state_dict[f'layer{li}.attn_wv'])
        keys[li].append(k)
        values[li].append(v)
        x_attn = []
        for h in range(n_head):
            hs = h * head_dim
            q_h = q[hs:hs+head_dim]
            k_h = [ki[hs:hs+head_dim] for ki in keys[li]]
            v_h = [vi[hs:hs+head_dim] for vi in values[li]]
            attn_logits = [sum(q_h[j] * k_h[t][j] for j in range(head_dim)) / head_dim**0.5 for t in range(len(k_h))]
            attn_weights = softmax(attn_logits)
            head_out = [sum(attn_weights[t] * v_h[t][j] for t in range(len(v_h))) for j in range(head_dim)]
            x_attn.extend(head_out)
        x = linear(x_attn, state_dict[f'layer{li}.attn_wo'])
        x = [a + b for a, b in zip(x, x_residual)]
        # 2) MLP block
        x_residual = x
        x = rmsnorm(x)
        x = linear(x, state_dict[f'layer{li}.mlp_fc1'])
        x = [xi.relu() for xi in x]
        x = linear(x, state_dict[f'layer{li}.mlp_fc2'])
        x = [a + b for a, b in zip(x, x_residual)]

    logits = linear(x, state_dict['lm_head'])
    return logits

# Let there be Adam, the blessed optimizer and its buffers
learning_rate, beta1, beta2, eps_adam = 0.01, 0.85, 0.99, 1e-8
m = [0.0] * len(params) # first moment buffer
v = [0.0] * len(params) # second moment buffer

# Repeat in sequence
num_steps = 1000 # number of training steps
for step in range(num_steps):

    # Take single document, tokenize it, surround it with BOS special token on both sides
    doc = docs[step % len(docs)]
    tokens = [BOS] + [uchars.index(ch) for ch in doc] + [BOS]
    n = min(block_size, len(tokens) - 1)

    # Forward the token sequence through the model, building up the computation graph all the way to the loss.
    keys, values = [[] for _ in range(n_layer)], [[] for _ in range(n_layer)]
    losses = []
    for pos_id in range(n):
        token_id, target_id = tokens[pos_id], tokens[pos_id + 1]
        logits = gpt(token_id, pos_id, keys, values)
        probs = softmax(logits)
        loss_t = -probs[target_id].log()
        losses.append(loss_t)
    loss = (1 / n) * sum(losses) # final average loss over the document sequence. May yours be low.

    # Backward the loss, calculating the gradients with respect to all model parameters.
    loss.backward()

    # Adam optimizer update: update the model parameters based on the corresponding gradients.
    lr_t = learning_rate * (1 - step / num_steps) # linear learning rate decay
    for i, p in enumerate(params):
        m[i] = beta1 * m[i] + (1 - beta1) * p.grad
        v[i] = beta2 * v[i] + (1 - beta2) * p.grad ** 2
        m_hat = m[i] / (1 - beta1 ** (step + 1))
        v_hat = v[i] / (1 - beta2 ** (step + 1))
        p.data -= lr_t * m_hat / (v_hat ** 0.5 + eps_adam)
        p.grad = 0

    print(f"step {step+1:4d} / {num_steps:4d} | loss {loss.data:.4f}")

# Inference: may the model babble back to us
temperature = 0.5 # in (0, 1], control the "creativity" of generated text, low to high
print("\n--- inference (new, hallucinated names) ---")
for sample_idx in range(20):
    keys, values = [[] for _ in range(n_layer)], [[] for _ in range(n_layer)]
    token_id = BOS
    sample = []
    for pos_id in range(block_size):
        logits = gpt(token_id, pos_id, keys, values)
        probs = softmax([l / temperature for l in logits])
        token_id = random.choices(range(vocab_size), weights=[p.data for p in probs])[0]
        if token_id == BOS:
            break
        sample.append(uchars[token_id])
    print(f"sample {sample_idx+1:2d}: {''.join(sample)}")

9.text: Standard library only, no NumPy, no PyTorch, no nothing. This is pure Python all the way down.

10.text: Just log and exp, the two math functions that make neural networks work.

11.text: Random numbers for weight initialization, sampling during generation, and shuffling data.

12.text: Fixed seed so every run is identical, essential for debugging when something goes wrong.

15.text: Builds the path to the dataset file relative to this script, one directory up in data/.

16.text: Auto-downloads the dataset if missing, the tutorial is self-contained.

17.text: Lazy import: only load urllib if we actually need to download.

18.text: Karpathy's "makemore" names dataset, ~32K human names, perfect for a tiny language model.

19.text: One HTTP call and we have our training data. No pip install, no API key.

20.text: Each document is one name. The model will learn the statistical patterns of how names are spelled.

21.text: Shuffling prevents the model from memorizing the alphabetical order of the dataset.

25.text: The vocabulary is just the unique characters across all names, typically a-z. Each character gets an integer ID.

26.text: A special token marking where names begin and end. The model learns to generate BOS when a name is "done."

27.text: Total vocabulary: 26 letters + 1 BOS token = 27 symbols. That's the entire language.

31.text: This is the heart of the autograd engine. Every Value wraps a single scalar number and knows how to compute its own gradient.

32.text: __slots__ saves memory by preventing Python from creating a __dict__ per instance, matters when you have thousands of Values.

34.text: Each Value stores four things: the number itself, its gradient, what produced it, and the local derivatives connecting it to its children.

35.text: The forward pass fills data; the backward pass fills grad.

36.text: Starts at zero because gradients accumulate, multiple paths through the graph can contribute to the same parameter's gradient.

37.text: The computation graph is built implicitly: every operation creates a new Value that remembers its inputs.

38.text: Local gradients are the partial derivatives of this node with respect to each child, the chain rule's building blocks.

40.text: Operator overloading is the trick that makes autograd invisible. Writing a + b secretly builds the computation graph.

41.text: If you add a plain number to a Value, wrap it first. This lets you write x + 1 naturally.

42.text: Addition: result is a + b, and both local gradients are 1 because d(a+b)/da = d(a+b)/db = 1.

44.text: Multiplication builds the graph and stores local gradients. The derivative of a*b w.r.t. a is b, and vice versa, the "swap and multiply" rule of calculus.

46.text: Product rule in action: d(a*b)/da = b and d(a*b)/db = a. The child values become each other's local gradients.

48.text: Power rule: d(x^n)/dx = n*x^(n-1). Used for division (x^-1) and square roots (x^0.5).

49.text: d(log x)/dx = 1/x. This appears in the cross-entropy loss computation.

50.text: d(exp x)/dx = exp(x), the only function that is its own derivative. Beautiful and essential for softmax.

51.text: ReLU: pass positive values through, kill negatives. The gradient is 1 or 0, a simple gate.

52.text: Negation reuses multiplication: -x is just x * -1. No new backward logic needed.

53.text: Right-add: handles 3 + value by flipping to value + 3. Python calls this when the left operand doesn't know how to add.

57.text: Division is multiplication by the reciprocal: a/b = a * b^(-1). The power rule handles the rest.

60.text: Backpropagation: walk the computation graph in reverse topological order, applying the chain rule at each node.

61.text: Topological sort ensures we process each node only after all nodes that depend on it. Gradients flow backward through the graph.

63.text: Recursive depth-first traversal to build the topological ordering, the classic algorithm.

69.text: Start the sort from the loss node, it's the root of the computation graph.

70.text: The loss's gradient w.r.t. itself is 1. This is the seed that starts the chain reaction of gradient computation.

71.text: Reversed topological order means we visit the loss first, then work backward toward the parameters. Each node distributes its gradient to its children.

72.text: For each node, multiply the incoming gradient by each local gradient and add to the child's gradient. This IS the chain rule.

73.text: The += is crucial, a parameter used in multiple operations receives gradients from all of them. This is why grad starts at 0.

76.text: 16 dimensions per token embedding. Tiny by modern standards (GPT-3 uses 12,288), but enough to learn name patterns.

77.text: 4 attention heads, each seeing a 4-dimensional slice. Multiple heads let the model attend to different types of relationships simultaneously.

78.text: Just 1 transformer layer. Real GPTs stack 12-96+ layers, but one layer already captures interesting patterns.

79.text: Names longer than 16 characters get truncated. Most names fit easily.

80.text: Each head gets n_embd / n_head = 4 dimensions. The heads work in parallel on different subspaces.

81.text: A helper to create a 2D list of random Values, this is how we initialize weight matrices without NumPy.

82.text: The three core weight matrices: token embeddings (what each character means), position embeddings (where it sits), and the output head (predicting the next character).

83.text: For each transformer layer, allocate the attention and MLP weight matrices.

84.text: Query weights: "what am I looking for?"

86.text: Value weights: "what information do I carry?"

87.text: Output projection: recombines the multi-head attention outputs back into a single vector.

88.text: MLP first layer expands to 4x the embedding dimension, a wider hidden layer for more expressive transformations.

89.text: MLP second layer projects back down to the embedding dimension.

90.text: Flatten all weight matrices into one list so the optimizer can iterate over every parameter uniformly.

95.text: A linear layer as a pure function: matrix-vector multiply using nested list comprehensions. No classes, no frameworks, just math.

96.text: Each output element is a dot product between one row of the weight matrix and the input vector. This is one row of Wx.

98.text: Softmax converts raw scores (logits) into a probability distribution that sums to 1.

99.text: Subtract the max for numerical stability, prevents exp() from overflowing. Doesn't change the output probabilities.

100.text: Exponentiate to make everything positive, then normalize. This is the standard softmax formula.

101.text: Sum of exponentials becomes the normalizing constant.

102.text: Each probability is its exponential divided by the total. Now they sum to 1 and represent a valid distribution.

104.text: RMSNorm: a simpler, faster alternative to LayerNorm. No mean subtraction, no learned parameters, just scale by the root mean square.

105.text: Mean of squared values, measures the "energy" of the vector.

106.text: Inverse square root scaling factor. The 1e-5 prevents division by zero when all values are near zero.

107.text: Scale each element so the vector has roughly unit RMS. This stabilizes training by preventing values from growing unboundedly.

109.text: The GPT forward pass. Takes one token and its position, plus KV caches for previous tokens. Returns prediction logits for the next token.

110.text: Look up this token's learned embedding, a dense vector capturing what the character "means" to the model.

111.text: Look up the position embedding, the model needs to know WHERE in the sequence this token sits.

112.text: Add token identity and position information. After this, the vector knows both what and where.

113.text: Normalize before entering the transformer layers, keeps activations stable from the start.

115.text: Process through each transformer layer. With n_layer=1, this loop runs once, but the code generalizes to deeper models.

117.text: Save the input for the residual connection, it will be added back after attention, creating a "skip road" for gradients.

118.text: Pre-norm architecture: normalize before attention, not after. This is the modern convention (GPT-2 onward) and helps training stability.

119.text: Query projection: transforms the normalized input into a "question" vector, what is this token looking for?

120.text: Key projection: transforms into a "label" vector, what does this token advertise about itself?

121.text: Value projection: transforms into the actual content that will be retrieved if attention deems this token relevant.

122.text: Append this token's keys to the cache. During generation, we accumulate keys from all previous positions so we don't recompute them.

123.text: Same for values, the KV cache is what makes autoregressive generation efficient.

125.text: Process each attention head independently. Each head can specialize in different types of token relationships.

126.text: Slice start index for this head's portion of the embedding.

127.text: Extract this head's slice of the query vector, just 4 dimensions out of 16.

128.text: Gather this head's key slices from ALL previous tokens (via the cache). This is the "memory" the current token attends to.

129.text: Same for values, the information to retrieve.

130.text: Scaled dot-product attention scores. Each score measures how much the query "matches" each key. Division by sqrt(head_dim) prevents the dot products from growing too large, which would make softmax too peaked.

131.text: Softmax converts attention scores into weights that sum to 1, a probability distribution over which past tokens to attend to.

132.text: Weighted sum of value vectors, using attention weights. Tokens that "match" the query contribute more to the output. This is the core of the attention mechanism.

133.text: Concatenate all heads' outputs. Multi-head attention is just independent attentions run in parallel, then merged.

134.text: Output projection: mixes the concatenated head outputs. Lets the model learn how to combine information from different heads.

135.text: Residual connection: add the attention output to the original input. This creates a direct gradient highway and lets the model learn incremental refinements.

137.text: Snapshot the representation before the MLP transforms it. The residual connection will add this back, ensuring the layer can only refine, not destroy, what came before.

138.text: Normalize before the feed-forward network. Like the pre-attention norm, this stabilizes training by keeping activations on a consistent scale before the MLP processes them.

139.text: MLP first layer: expand to 4x dimension. This wider hidden layer gives the network more capacity for complex transformations.

140.text: ReLU activation: zero out negatives, keep positives. Introduces non-linearity, without this, stacking linear layers would be equivalent to a single linear layer.

141.text: MLP second layer: project back to embedding dimension.

142.text: Second residual connection: add MLP output to its input. The pattern is always: normalize, transform, add back.

144.text: Final projection: map from the 16-dim embedding space to the 27-dim vocabulary. Each output is a score (logit) for how likely each character is to come next.

145.text: Return the logits, raw scores before softmax. The training loop will convert these to probabilities and compute the loss.

148.text: Adam optimizer hyperparameters. beta1=0.85 (momentum) is lower than the default 0.9, less smoothing of gradients. beta2=0.99 tracks the scale of recent gradients.

149.text: First moment (mean) estimates, initialized to zero. These smooth out noisy gradients, acting like momentum.

150.text: Second moment (variance) estimates, these adapt the learning rate per-parameter, giving larger updates to rarely-changed parameters.

153.text: 1000 training steps. Each step processes one name and updates all parameters.

154.text: The main training loop, each iteration is one gradient descent step.

157.text: Cycle through the dataset. After seeing all names, start over.

158.text: Tokenize: BOS + character IDs + BOS. The model learns to start generating after BOS and stop by predicting BOS.

159.text: Clip to the maximum context length. Most names are short, so this rarely activates.

162.text: Fresh KV caches for each training example. During training, we process the whole sequence at once (teacher forcing).

163.text: We'll accumulate the loss at each position and average them.

164.text: Process each position one at a time, the model predicts each next character given all previous characters.

165.text: Input token and the target it should predict. The model sees token_id and should output high probability for target_id.

166.text: Run the forward pass, building the entire computation graph. Every Value operation is recorded for backpropagation.

167.text: Convert logits to probabilities. The model's confidence distribution over all 27 possible next characters.

168.text: Negative log probability of the correct answer. If the model assigns probability 1.0 to the right token, loss is 0. Lower probability = higher loss.

170.text: Average loss across all positions in this name. This is the number we minimize, our measure of how wrong the model is.

173.text: One call triggers the entire backward pass through the computation graph. Every parameter's gradient gets computed via the chain rule we built in the Value class.

176.text: Linear warmdown: learning rate starts at 0.01 and linearly decays to 0. Smaller steps near the end help the model settle into a good minimum.

177.text: Iterate over every single parameter in the model for the Adam update.

178.text: Update the running mean of gradients (first moment). beta1 controls how much history to keep vs. how much to trust the current gradient.

179.text: Update the running mean of squared gradients (second moment). This tracks the scale/variance of each parameter's gradients.

180.text: Bias correction for the first moment, compensates for the zero initialization, especially important in early steps.

181.text: Bias correction for the second moment.

182.text: The Adam update: step in the direction of the smoothed gradient, scaled by the inverse of the gradient's historical magnitude. Parameters with large gradients get smaller steps.

183.text: Reset gradient to zero for the next training step. Without this, gradients would accumulate across steps.

188.text: Temperature controls randomness: 0.5 makes the distribution sharper (more conservative), closer to 1.0 gives more variety.

190.text: Generate 20 new names the model has never seen, these come purely from learned patterns.

191.text: Fresh KV caches for generation, no context from training.

192.text: Start with the BOS token, the universal "begin a name" signal.

194.text: Generate one character at a time, up to the max sequence length.

195.text: Feed the current token through the model to get predictions for the next one.

196.text: Apply temperature scaling before softmax. Lower temperature = more confident/predictable outputs.

197.text: Sample from the probability distribution. Unlike greedy decoding (always picking the most likely), sampling introduces variety so not every generation is identical.

198.text: If the model generates BOS, it's signaling "this name is complete."

200.text: Convert the token ID back to its character. The tokenizer goes both ways: chars to IDs for input, IDs to chars for output.

201.text: Print the generated name. These are entirely new names invented by the model, reflecting the statistical patterns it learned from 32K real names.

title: NumPy with Manual Backpropagation
tagline: Same architecture, but NumPy handles the math. Every gradient is derived by hand. You are the autograd.
code:
"""
microGPT — NumPy edition with manual backpropagation.

Same architecture as the pure-Python version, but using NumPy arrays for
vectorized math. Every gradient is derived and coded by hand — no autograd.
This is what PyTorch computes for you behind the scenes.
"""

import math
import os
import random

import numpy as np

random.seed(42)
rng = np.random.default_rng(42)

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Dataset & Tokenizer
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
input_path = os.path.join(os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__)), "..", "data", "input.txt")
if not os.path.exists(input_path):
    import urllib.request

    url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/karpathy/makemore/refs/heads/master/names.txt"
    urllib.request.urlretrieve(url, input_path)

docs = [l.strip() for l in open(input_path).read().strip().split("\n") if l.strip()]
random.shuffle(docs)
print(f"num docs: {len(docs)}")

uchars = sorted(set("".join(docs)))
BOS = len(uchars)
vocab_size = len(uchars) + 1
print(f"vocab size: {vocab_size}")

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Parameters — plain NumPy arrays + matching gradient arrays
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
n_embd = 16     # embedding dimension
n_head = 4      # number of attention heads
n_layer = 1     # number of layers
block_size = 16 # maximum sequence length
head_dim = n_embd // n_head # dimension of each head


def param(shape, std=0.08):
    return rng.standard_normal(shape) * std


# All parameters stored as a dict of arrays
P = {
    "wte": param((vocab_size, n_embd)),
    "wpe": param((block_size, n_embd)),
    "lm_head": param((vocab_size, n_embd)),
}
for i in range(n_layer):
    P[f"l{i}.wq"] = param((n_embd, n_embd))
    P[f"l{i}.wk"] = param((n_embd, n_embd))
    P[f"l{i}.wv"] = param((n_embd, n_embd))
    P[f"l{i}.wo"] = param((n_embd, n_embd))
    P[f"l{i}.fc1"] = param((n_embd, 4 * n_embd))
    P[f"l{i}.fc2"] = param((4 * n_embd, n_embd))

# Gradient buffers (same shapes, zeroed)
G = {k: np.zeros_like(v) for k, v in P.items()}

num_params = sum(v.size for v in P.values())
print(f"num params: {num_params}")


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Forward + Backward (manual)
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
def rmsnorm_fwd(x):
    """x: (T, D) -> out: (T, D), cache"""
    ms = np.mean(x**2, axis=-1, keepdims=True)  # (T, 1)
    scale = 1.0 / np.sqrt(ms + 1e-5)  # (T, 1)
    out = x * scale
    return out, (x, scale, ms)


def rmsnorm_bwd(dout, cache):
    x, scale, _ms = cache
    D = x.shape[-1]
    dx = dout * scale - x * scale**3 * np.sum(dout * x, axis=-1, keepdims=True) / D
    return dx


def softmax_fwd(logits):
    """logits: (T, V) or (nh, T, T2) -> probs same shape"""
    shifted = logits - logits.max(axis=-1, keepdims=True)
    exps = np.exp(shifted)
    return exps / exps.sum(axis=-1, keepdims=True)


def softmax_bwd(probs, dprobs):
    """Element-wise: dp - p * sum(dp * p)"""
    s = np.sum(dprobs * probs, axis=-1, keepdims=True)
    return probs * (dprobs - s)


def relu_fwd(x):
    mask = (x > 0).astype(x.dtype)
    relu_x = x * mask
    return relu_x, mask


def relu_bwd(dout, cache):
    mask = cache
    return dout * mask


def forward(token_ids):
    """
    token_ids: (n,) int array of token ids
    Returns: logits (n, vocab_size), cache dict (for backward)
    """
    n = len(token_ids)
    cache = {}

    # Embeddings
    tok_emb = P["wte"][token_ids]  # (n, D)
    pos_emb = P["wpe"][:n]  # (n, D)
    x = tok_emb + pos_emb

    # Initial RMSNorm
    x, cache["norm_in"] = rmsnorm_fwd(x)

    for li in range(n_layer):
        x_res = x.copy()

        # Pre-attention RMSNorm
        x_normed, cache[f"l{li}.norm1"] = rmsnorm_fwd(x)

        # QKV projections: (n, D) @ (D, D) -> (n, D)
        Q = x_normed @ P[f"l{li}.wq"]  # (n, D)
        K = x_normed @ P[f"l{li}.wk"]
        V = x_normed @ P[f"l{li}.wv"]

        # Reshape for multi-head: (n, nh, hd) -> (nh, n, hd)
        Q_h = Q.reshape(n, n_head, head_dim).transpose(1, 0, 2)  # (nh, n, hd)
        K_h = K.reshape(n, n_head, head_dim).transpose(1, 0, 2)
        V_h = V.reshape(n, n_head, head_dim).transpose(1, 0, 2)

        # Attention scores
        att = Q_h @ K_h.transpose(0, 2, 1) / math.sqrt(head_dim)  # (nh, n, n)
        # Causal mask
        causal_mask = np.triu(np.ones((n, n)), k=1).astype(bool)
        att = np.where(causal_mask, -1e9, att)
        att_probs = softmax_fwd(att)  # (nh, n, n)

        # Weighted sum of values
        attn_out = att_probs @ V_h  # (nh, n, hd)
        # Reshape back: (n, D)
        attn_out_cat = attn_out.transpose(1, 0, 2).reshape(n, n_embd)

        # Output projection
        attn_proj = attn_out_cat @ P[f"l{li}.wo"]  # (n, D)

        # Residual
        x = attn_proj + x_res

        cache[f"l{li}.attn"] = (x_res, x_normed, Q, K, V, Q_h, K_h, V_h, att_probs, attn_out, attn_out_cat)

        # Pre-MLP RMSNorm
        x_res2 = x.copy()
        x_normed2, cache[f"l{li}.norm2"] = rmsnorm_fwd(x)

        # MLP
        hidden = x_normed2 @ P[f"l{li}.fc1"]  # (n, 4D)
        act, cache[f"l{li}.act"] = relu_fwd(hidden)
        mlp_out = act @ P[f"l{li}.fc2"]  # (n, D)

        x = mlp_out + x_res2
        cache[f"l{li}.mlp"] = (x_res2, x_normed2, hidden, act)

    # LM head
    logits = x @ P["lm_head"].T  # (n, vocab_size)
    cache["final_x"] = x
    return logits, cache


def loss_and_cache(tokens_np):
    """Forward + cross-entropy loss for training. Returns: loss, cache for backward."""
    T = len(tokens_np)
    n = min(block_size, T - 1)
    input_ids = tokens_np[:n]
    targets = tokens_np[1 : n + 1]

    logits, cache = forward(input_ids)

    # Loss: cross-entropy
    probs = softmax_fwd(logits)  # (n, vocab_size)
    log_probs = -np.log(probs[np.arange(n), targets] + 1e-10)
    loss = log_probs.mean()

    cache["final"] = (cache["final_x"], logits, probs, targets, input_ids, n)
    return loss, cache


def backward(cache):
    """Compute gradients for all parameters, accumulate into G."""
    x, logits, probs, targets, input_ids, n = cache["final"]

    # d(loss)/d(logits) via softmax + cross-entropy shortcut
    dlogits = probs.copy()  # (n, V)
    dlogits[np.arange(n), targets] -= 1.0
    dlogits /= n

    # LM head: logits = x @ lm_head.T
    G["lm_head"] += dlogits.T @ x  # (V, D)
    dx = dlogits @ P["lm_head"]  # (n, D)

    for li in reversed(range(n_layer)):
        # --- MLP backward ---
        x_res2, x_normed2, hidden, act = cache[f"l{li}.mlp"]

        dx_res2 = dx.copy()
        # mlp_out = act @ fc2
        dact = dx @ P[f"l{li}.fc2"].T  # (n, 4D)
        G[f"l{li}.fc2"] += act.T @ dx  # (4D, D)

        dhidden = relu_bwd(dact, cache[f"l{li}.act"])

        # hidden = x_normed2 @ fc1
        dx_normed2 = dhidden @ P[f"l{li}.fc1"].T  # (n, D)
        G[f"l{li}.fc1"] += x_normed2.T @ dhidden  # (D, 4D)

        # RMSNorm backward
        dx_pre_mlp = rmsnorm_bwd(dx_normed2, cache[f"l{li}.norm2"])
        dx = dx_pre_mlp + dx_res2  # residual

        # --- Attention backward ---
        x_res, x_normed, Q, K, V, Q_h, K_h, V_h, att_probs, attn_out, attn_out_cat = cache[f"l{li}.attn"]

        dx_res = dx.copy()

        # attn_proj = attn_out_cat @ wo
        dattn_out_cat = dx @ P[f"l{li}.wo"].T  # (n, D)
        G[f"l{li}.wo"] += attn_out_cat.T @ dx  # (D, D)

        # Reshape back to multi-head: (n, D) -> (nh, n, hd)
        dattn_out = dattn_out_cat.reshape(n, n_head, head_dim).transpose(1, 0, 2)

        # attn_out = att_probs @ V_h
        datt_probs = dattn_out @ V_h.transpose(0, 2, 1)  # (nh, n, n)
        dV_h = att_probs.transpose(0, 2, 1) @ dattn_out  # (nh, n, hd)

        # Softmax backward on attention
        datt = softmax_bwd(att_probs, datt_probs)  # (nh, n, n)
        # Causal mask: zero out upper triangle grads (they were -inf)
        causal_mask = np.triu(np.ones((n, n)), k=1).astype(bool)
        datt = np.where(causal_mask, 0.0, datt)
        datt /= math.sqrt(head_dim)

        # att = Q_h @ K_h^T
        dQ_h = datt @ K_h  # (nh, n, hd)
        dK_h = datt.transpose(0, 2, 1) @ Q_h  # (nh, n, hd)

        # Reshape heads back to (n, D)
        dQ = dQ_h.transpose(1, 0, 2).reshape(n, n_embd)
        dK = dK_h.transpose(1, 0, 2).reshape(n, n_embd)
        dV = dV_h.transpose(1, 0, 2).reshape(n, n_embd)

        # QKV projections: Q = x_normed @ wq, etc.
        dx_normed = dQ @ P[f"l{li}.wq"].T + dK @ P[f"l{li}.wk"].T + dV @ P[f"l{li}.wv"].T
        G[f"l{li}.wq"] += x_normed.T @ dQ
        G[f"l{li}.wk"] += x_normed.T @ dK
        G[f"l{li}.wv"] += x_normed.T @ dV

        # RMSNorm backward
        dx_pre_attn = rmsnorm_bwd(dx_normed, cache[f"l{li}.norm1"])
        dx = dx_pre_attn + dx_res  # residual

    # Initial RMSNorm backward
    dx = rmsnorm_bwd(dx, cache["norm_in"])

    # Embedding gradients
    for i, tid in enumerate(input_ids):
        G["wte"][tid] += dx[i]
        G["wpe"][i] += dx[i]


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Adam optimizer (manual, operating on the P/G dicts)
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
learning_rate, beta1, beta2, eps_adam = 1e-2, 0.85, 0.99, 1e-8
M = {k: np.zeros_like(v) for k, v in P.items()}  # first moment
Vb = {k: np.zeros_like(v) for k, v in P.items()}  # second moment

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Training loop
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
num_steps = 1000
for step in range(num_steps):
    doc = docs[step % len(docs)]
    tokens = [BOS] + [uchars.index(ch) for ch in doc] + [BOS]
    tokens_np = np.array(tokens)

    # Zero gradients
    for k in G:
        G[k].fill(0.0)

    loss, cache = loss_and_cache(tokens_np)
    backward(cache)

    # Adam update with linear LR decay
    lr_t = learning_rate * (1 - step / num_steps)
    for k in P:
        M[k] = beta1 * M[k] + (1 - beta1) * G[k]
        Vb[k] = beta2 * Vb[k] + (1 - beta2) * G[k] ** 2
        m_hat = M[k] / (1 - beta1 ** (step + 1))
        v_hat = Vb[k] / (1 - beta2 ** (step + 1))
        P[k] -= lr_t * m_hat / (np.sqrt(v_hat) + eps_adam)

    if (step + 1) % 10 == 0 or step == 0:
        print(f"step {step + 1:4d} / {num_steps:4d} | loss {loss:.4f}")

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Inference
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
temperature = 0.5 # in (0, 1], control the "creativity" of generated text, low to high
print("\n--- inference (new, hallucinated names) ---")


for sample_idx in range(20):
    generated = [BOS]
    sample = []
    for _ in range(block_size):
        logits, _ = forward(np.array(generated))
        logits = logits[-1] / temperature
        probs = softmax_fwd(logits.reshape(1, -1))[0]
        token_id = rng.choice(vocab_size, p=probs)
        if token_id == BOS:
            break
        generated.append(token_id)
        sample.append(uchars[token_id])
    print(f"sample {sample_idx + 1:2d}: {''.join(sample)}")

9.text: Standard math library, just for sqrt in the attention scaling.

10.text: File path handling for the dataset.

11.text: Python's random for shuffling, keeps dataset handling identical to lab 01.

13.text: NumPy replaces the scalar-by-scalar math from lab 01. One matrix multiply here replaces thousands of individual Value multiplications.

15.text: Same seed as lab 01 for reproducible Python randomness.

16.text: NumPy's modern RNG, separate from Python's random module, used for weight initialization.

21.text: Same dataset path as lab 01, all labs share the same data.

22.text: Auto-download if missing, identical to lab 01.

25.text: Download URL for the names dataset.

28.text: Same tokenization as lab 01, each line becomes one training document.

33.text: BOS token ID, same convention across all labs.

40.text: Same tiny architecture as lab 01, this lab is about the computation method, not the model size.

42.text: A single transformer layer keeps the model minimal. Real GPTs stack dozens, but one is enough to learn interesting name patterns and demonstrate the full algorithm.

47.text: Helper to create a random NumPy array, replaces lab 01's nested list of Value objects. One line, same effect.

48.text: Standard normal scaled by 0.08, same initialization distribution as lab 01.

52.text: Parameters stored as a flat dict of NumPy arrays. Compare to lab 01's nested lists of Value objects, same data, vastly more efficient storage.

53.text: Token embeddings: each row is one token's learned representation.

54.text: Position embeddings: each row encodes a position in the sequence.

55.text: Output projection: maps embeddings back to vocabulary scores.

57.text: Allocate per-layer attention and MLP weights.

58.text: The query weight matrix. Multiplying the input by this produces the 'what am I looking for?' vectors for attention.

59.text: The key weight matrix. It produces 'what do I contain?' vectors that queries will be matched against to determine relevance.

60.text: The value weight matrix. It produces vectors carrying the actual content that attention will retrieve and blend together.

61.text: Output projection recombines multi-head attention outputs. After each head focuses on different relationships, this matrix merges their perspectives into a unified representation.

66.text: Every parameter gets a matching zero-filled gradient array. In lab 01, gradients lived inside each Value. Here, we manage them ourselves.

68.text: Count total parameters to verify we match lab 01.

75.text: RMSNorm forward, returns both the output AND a cache of intermediate values. The backward pass needs these cached values to compute gradients.

77.text: Mean of squares across the embedding dimension. NumPy handles this across entire matrices, not one scalar at a time.

78.text: Reciprocal square root, the scaling factor. Same formula as lab 01's (ms + 1e-5) ** -0.5.

79.text: Scale the input. Broadcasting handles the (T, 1) * (T, D) multiplication automatically.

80.text: Return the output AND the cache. This is the key pattern: forward stores everything backward will need.

83.text: RMSNorm backward, given the gradient of the output, compute the gradient of the input. This is the manual derivative you'd get from the chain rule on paper.

84.text: Unpack the cached values from the forward pass.

86.text: The hand-derived RMSNorm gradient. Two terms: direct path through the scale, and indirect path through the normalization. This is what autograd computes automatically.

90.text: Softmax forward, same subtract-max-then-exponentiate trick as lab 01, but vectorized over entire matrices.

92.text: Subtract max for numerical stability, prevents overflow in exp().

94.text: Normalize to get probabilities. Each row sums to 1.

97.text: Softmax backward. The Jacobian of softmax is dense (every output depends on every input), but this compact formula handles it.

99.text: Sum of element-wise product: this captures the coupling between all softmax outputs.

100.text: Elegant formula: p * (dp - sum(dp * p)). Derived from differentiating the softmax function, doing this by hand is a rite of passage.

103.text: ReLU forward: save a binary mask of which neurons fired. The backward pass just uses this mask as a gate.

104.text: The mask is 1 where input was positive, 0 elsewhere. This IS the ReLU derivative.

105.text: Element-wise multiply: zero out negative inputs.

109.text: ReLU backward: gradients pass through where the neuron was active, get blocked where it was dead. Multiply by the mask, that's it.

111.text: Dead simple: dout * mask. If the neuron fired in the forward pass, its gradient flows; if not, it's zero.

114.text: The forward pass processes an entire sequence at once as a matrix, no more position-by-position loop like lab 01.

119.text: How many tokens we're processing. The model will see all of these at once, unlike lab 01 which processed one position at a time.

120.text: The cache dictionary stores every intermediate result the backward pass will need.

123.text: Gather all token embeddings at once with fancy indexing. In lab 01, this was a lookup in a loop.

125.text: Broadcast addition fuses token and position information, same as lab 01 but for all positions simultaneously.

128.text: Normalize and cache. Every intermediate result stored here will be consumed during backpropagation.

131.text: Snapshot the representation before attention transforms it. The residual connection will add this back, ensuring the layer can only refine, not destroy, what came before.

134.text: Normalize and cache before attention. The cache stores the normalization statistics so the backward pass can compute exact gradients without re-doing the forward math.

137.text: Query projection: (n, D) @ (D, D). One matrix multiply replaces lab 01's nested list-comprehension linear layer.

139.text: Project into value vectors, which carry the actual information that attention will retrieve and blend. Same operation as Q and K, different learned weights.

142.text: Reshape and transpose to split into attention heads. The (n, D) matrix becomes (n_head, n, head_dim), each head gets its own slice.

143.text: Keys get the same multi-head split. Each head's keys will be matched against only that head's queries, so different heads learn different notions of relevance.

144.text: Values get the same multi-head split. Each head independently retrieves content from its own subspace, then the results are concatenated.

147.text: Batch matrix multiply: all heads compute attention scores simultaneously. One operation replaces lab 01's per-head loop.

149.text: Causal mask: upper triangle of ones. Prevents tokens from attending to future positions, the core constraint of autoregressive generation.

150.text: Fill future positions with -1e9 so softmax gives them ~zero probability.

151.text: Softmax over the last dimension, each row becomes a probability distribution over which past tokens to attend to.

154.text: Weighted sum of values using attention probabilities, the actual information retrieval step.

156.text: Reverse the reshape: merge heads back into a single (n, D) matrix.

159.text: Output projection: mix the multi-head outputs.

162.text: Residual connection: add attention output to original input.

164.text: Cache EVERYTHING the attention backward pass needs. Eleven values, this is the price of manual backprop.

167.text: Snapshot the representation before the MLP transforms it. The residual connection will add this back, letting gradients flow through an unobstructed shortcut path.

168.text: Normalize before the feed-forward network. Like the pre-attention norm, this keeps activations on a consistent scale so the MLP can learn stable transformations.

171.text: MLP expansion: project to 4x wider hidden layer.

172.text: ReLU activation with its binary mask cached for the backward pass. The mask records which neurons fired, which is all the backward pass needs to route gradients.

173.text: MLP contraction: project back to embedding dimension.

175.text: Add the MLP output back to its input via the residual connection. This is the second skip connection in the block, completing the norm-transform-add pattern.

176.text: Cache all MLP intermediates for backward.

179.text: Final linear projection to vocabulary logits. Note the transpose on lm_head, weight tying convention.

180.text: Cache the final hidden states for the backward pass.

184.text: Combines forward pass with loss computation, the complete training forward step.

186.text: Total sequence length before clipping. We need this to determine how many input-target pairs we can form from this document.

187.text: Clip to the maximum context window. Most names are short enough to fit, but this guard prevents index-out-of-bounds on unusually long entries.

189.text: Target tokens (all but first), shifted by one position.

191.text: Feed the tokens through the model to get logits and the cache of intermediates. The cache is what makes manual backprop possible without recomputing the forward pass.

194.text: Convert logits to probabilities for cross-entropy loss.

195.text: Gather the log-probabilities of the correct targets. The 1e-10 prevents log(0).

196.text: Mean negative log-probability, cross-entropy loss. Lower means the model assigns higher probability to the right answers.

198.text: Pack everything into the cache for the backward pass.

202.text: The backward pass: 80+ lines of hand-derived gradients. This is what loss.backward() does automatically in PyTorch.

207.text: Softmax + cross-entropy gradient shortcut: start with the probabilities, subtract 1 from the correct class. This elegant shorthand replaces two separate backward passes.

208.text: Subtract 1 at target positions, the combined softmax-CE gradient.

209.text: Average over the sequence, matches the mean in the loss computation.

212.text: Gradient for lm_head: dlogits.T @ x. Accumulate with += because gradients add up.

213.text: Gradient flowing back into the hidden states.

215.text: Walk backward through layers, reverse order of the forward pass.

217.text: Unpack the four intermediate values the MLP forward pass cached. Each one is needed to compute the exact gradient for the corresponding weight matrix.

219.text: Save residual gradient, it bypasses the MLP and adds directly later.

221.text: Gradient through fc2: the backward of act @ fc2 w.r.t. act.

222.text: Weight gradient for fc2: act.T @ dx. This is the outer product pattern: input.T @ output_grad.

224.text: ReLU backward: zero out gradients for dead neurons.

227.text: Gradient through fc1: same pattern as fc2.

228.text: Weight gradient for fc1: the outer product of the normalized input and the hidden gradient. This tells us how to adjust fc1 to reduce the loss.

231.text: RMSNorm backward, uses the cached normalization statistics.

232.text: Add the residual gradient. The residual connection means gradients take two paths, through the MLP and around it, and they add together.

235.text: Unpack the attention cache, all eleven intermediate values.

237.text: Save the residual gradient for the attention skip connection.

241.text: Weight gradient for the output projection.

244.text: Reshape gradient back into multi-head format.

247.text: Gradient through att_probs @ V_h w.r.t. attention probabilities.

248.text: Gradient flowing back into value vectors. The attention probabilities determine how much each value contributed to the output, so transposing them gives the correct routing.

251.text: Softmax backward on attention weights, uses the formula from softmax_bwd.

253.text: Zero out gradients for masked (future) positions, they were -inf in the forward pass.

255.text: Undo the sqrt(head_dim) scaling from the forward pass.

262.text: Reshape head gradients back to (n, D).

263.text: Key gradients get the same reshape back to full embedding dimension. The per-head gradients are simply concatenated since the heads operate on non-overlapping slices.

264.text: Value gradients reshaped identically. All three QKV gradients flow back through the same reshape-transpose pipeline that split them into heads during the forward pass.

267.text: Sum gradients from all three QKV projections. The same x_normed feeds into Q, K, and V, so its gradient is the sum of all three paths.

273.text: RMSNorm backward for the pre-attention norm.

274.text: Add residual gradient, completing the attention skip connection.

277.text: Initial RMSNorm backward, the very first normalization in the forward pass.

280.text: Embedding gradients: scatter the position-level gradients back to the embedding tables. Unlike weight matrices, embeddings use row indexing, so we accumulate manually.

281.text: Each token ID's embedding row gets the gradient from that position.

282.text: Each position's embedding row gets the same gradient.

289.text: First moment buffers, matching dict structure to parameters.

290.text: Second moment buffers. Named Vb to avoid shadowing Python's v loop variable.

296.text: The main training loop. Each iteration: pick a name, forward pass, manual backward pass, Adam update. One thousand steps through this cycle.

297.text: Cycle through the dataset with modular indexing. After seeing all names, we loop back to the beginning for another pass.

299.text: Convert to NumPy array for the forward pass.

302.text: Zero all gradients before each step. In lab 01, this was p.grad = 0 per parameter; here it's per array.

303.text: Fill with zeros, faster than creating new arrays.

306.text: Backward pass, our 80+ lines of hand-coded gradients. In lab 03, this will be one line.

309.text: Linear learning rate decay, same schedule as lab 01.

310.text: Iterate over every parameter array in the model to apply the Adam update. NumPy broadcasting handles entire weight matrices at once.

311.text: First moment update, exponential moving average of gradients.

312.text: Second moment update, exponential moving average of squared gradients.

315.text: Adam parameter update. Same formula as lab 01, but operating on entire arrays at once thanks to NumPy broadcasting.

317.text: Log every 10 steps (lab 01 logged every step, the NumPy version is fast enough that would be too verbose).

327.text: Generate 20 entirely new names by sampling from the model's learned distribution. These names never appeared in the training data.

328.text: Start with BOS token in the generated sequence.

331.text: Run the forward pass on the entire generated sequence so far. Unlike lab 01's KV-cache approach, we recompute from scratch each step.

332.text: Take only the last position's logits and apply temperature scaling.

333.text: Reshape for softmax (expects 2D input) and get probabilities.

334.text: Sample from the distribution using NumPy's RNG.

335.text: When the model outputs the BOS token during generation, it is signaling that this name is complete and we should stop adding characters.

337.text: Extend the sequence with the new token.

339.text: Print the finished name. These are entirely new names invented by the model, reflecting the statistical patterns it absorbed from 32K real names.

title: PyTorch
tagline: PyTorch handles tensors and gradients. See how much boilerplate disappears when a framework does the differentiation.
code:
"""
microGPT — PyTorch edition.

Same algorithm as microgpt.py (pure-Python scalar version), but using PyTorch
tensors for efficient computation. Every architectural choice is preserved:
  - Character-level tokenizer
  - RMSNorm (not LayerNorm)
  - ReLU (not GeLU)
  - No biases
  - Linear LR decay
  - Adam with beta1=0.85, beta2=0.99
"""

import math
import os
import random

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F

random.seed(42)
torch.manual_seed(42)

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Dataset & Tokenizer
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
input_path = os.path.join(os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__)), "..", "data", "input.txt")
if not os.path.exists(input_path):
    import urllib.request

    url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/karpathy/makemore/refs/heads/master/names.txt"
    urllib.request.urlretrieve(url, input_path)

docs = [l.strip() for l in open(input_path).read().strip().split("\n") if l.strip()]
random.shuffle(docs)
print(f"num docs: {len(docs)}")
uchars = sorted(set("".join(docs)))
BOS = len(uchars)
vocab_size = len(uchars) + 1
print(f"vocab size: {vocab_size}")

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Model
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
n_embd = 16     # embedding dimension
n_head = 4      # number of attention heads
n_layer = 1     # number of layers
block_size = 16 # maximum sequence length
head_dim = n_embd // n_head # dimension of each head


class RMSNorm(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, _dim, eps=1e-5):
        super().__init__()
        self.eps = eps

    def forward(self, x):
        return x * torch.rsqrt(x.pow(2).mean(-1, keepdim=True) + self.eps)


class CausalSelfAttention(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.wq = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)
        self.wk = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)
        self.wv = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)
        self.wo = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)

    def forward(self, x):
        B, T, C = x.shape
        q = self.wq(x).view(B, T, n_head, head_dim).transpose(1, 2)  # (B, nh, T, hd)
        k = self.wk(x).view(B, T, n_head, head_dim).transpose(1, 2)
        v = self.wv(x).view(B, T, n_head, head_dim).transpose(1, 2)

        # Scaled dot-product attention with causal mask
        att = (q @ k.transpose(-2, -1)) / math.sqrt(head_dim)
        mask = torch.triu(torch.ones(T, T, device=x.device), diagonal=1).bool()
        att = att.masked_fill(mask, float("-inf"))
        att = F.softmax(att, dim=-1)

        out = (att @ v).transpose(1, 2).reshape(B, T, C)
        return self.wo(out)


class MLP(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.fc1 = nn.Linear(n_embd, 4 * n_embd, bias=False)
        self.fc2 = nn.Linear(4 * n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)

    def forward(self, x):
        return self.fc2(F.relu(self.fc1(x)))


class Block(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.norm1 = RMSNorm(n_embd)
        self.attn = CausalSelfAttention()
        self.norm2 = RMSNorm(n_embd)
        self.mlp = MLP()

    def forward(self, x):
        x = x + self.attn(self.norm1(x))
        x = x + self.mlp(self.norm2(x))
        return x


class MicroGPT(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.wte = nn.Embedding(vocab_size, n_embd)
        self.wpe = nn.Embedding(block_size, n_embd)
        self.norm_in = RMSNorm(n_embd)
        self.layers = nn.ModuleList([Block() for _ in range(n_layer)])
        self.lm_head = nn.Linear(n_embd, vocab_size, bias=False)
        self.apply(self._init_weights)

    @staticmethod
    def _init_weights(module):
        if isinstance(module, (nn.Linear, nn.Embedding)):
            nn.init.normal_(module.weight, mean=0.0, std=0.08)

    def forward(self, idx):
        B, T = idx.shape
        tok_emb = self.wte(idx)
        pos_emb = self.wpe(torch.arange(T, device=idx.device))
        x = self.norm_in(tok_emb + pos_emb)
        for layer in self.layers:
            x = layer(x)
        return self.lm_head(x)


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Training
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
device = "cpu"
model = MicroGPT().to(device)
print(f"num params: {sum(p.numel() for p in model.parameters())}")

optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=1e-2, betas=(0.85, 0.99), eps=1e-8)
num_steps = 1000

for step in range(num_steps):
    doc = docs[step % len(docs)]
    tokens = [BOS] + [uchars.index(ch) for ch in doc] + [BOS]
    n = min(block_size, len(tokens) - 1)

    input_ids = torch.tensor([tokens[:n]], device=device)  # (1, n)
    targets = torch.tensor([tokens[1 : n + 1]], device=device)  # (1, n)

    logits = model(input_ids)  # (1, n, vocab_size)
    loss = F.cross_entropy(logits.view(-1, vocab_size), targets.view(-1))

    optimizer.zero_grad()
    loss.backward()
    lr_t = 1e-2 * (1 - step / num_steps)
    for pg in optimizer.param_groups:
        pg["lr"] = lr_t
    optimizer.step()

    if (step + 1) % 10 == 0 or step == 0:
        print(f"step {step + 1:4d} / {num_steps:4d} | loss {loss.item():.4f}")

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Inference
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
temperature = 0.5 # in (0, 1], control the "creativity" of generated text, low to high
print("\n--- inference (new, hallucinated names) ---")
model.eval()
with torch.no_grad():
    for sample_idx in range(20):
        tokens = [BOS]
        for _ in range(block_size):
            idx = torch.tensor([tokens[-block_size:]], device=device)
            logits = model(idx)
            logits = logits[0, -1] / temperature
            probs = F.softmax(logits, dim=-1)
            token_id = torch.multinomial(probs, 1).item()
            if token_id == BOS:
                break
            tokens.append(token_id)
        name = "".join(uchars[t] for t in tokens[1:])
        print(f"sample {sample_idx + 1:2d}: {name}")

14.text: Standard math library for the attention scaling factor.

15.text: Provides utilities for constructing file paths to locate the dataset on disk.

18.text: PyTorch enters the picture. Everything changes, tensors, autograd, GPU support, all in one package.

19.text: The nn module provides the building blocks: layers, modules, loss functions.

20.text: Functional API for operations like softmax and cross-entropy that don't have learned parameters.

22.text: Same Python seed for identical dataset ordering across all labs.

23.text: PyTorch has its own RNG, seed it separately for reproducible weight initialization.

28.text: Same dataset path, all labs share the data directory.

29.text: Auto-download the names dataset if needed.

32.text: Fetch the dataset from Karpathy's repo.

38.text: Extract all unique characters from the dataset and sort them to form a stable vocabulary. Each character becomes a token with a fixed integer ID.

39.text: BOS token gets the highest ID.

46.text: Same hyperparameters as labs 01-02, this lab changes the framework, not the architecture.

47.text: 4 heads, each attending to different relationships.

48.text: A single transformer layer. Real GPTs stack 12 to 96+ layers deep, but one already captures meaningful character-level patterns.

49.text: Names longer than 16 characters get truncated. Most names fit easily within this window.

50.text: Each of the 4 heads gets 4 dimensions of the 16-dimensional embedding, letting it focus on its own subspace of relationships.

53.text: RMSNorm as an nn.Module, PyTorch's standard way to define reusable layers. Compare to lab 01's bare function.

54.text: Constructor takes dimension (unused here since we have no learnable scale) and epsilon.

56.text: A tiny constant added before division to prevent numerical explosion when the input is near zero. Standard practice in every normalization layer.

58.text: The forward method is what gets called when you use the module. Same math as lab 01, but on tensors.

59.text: One line: compute RMS and scale. rsqrt is reciprocal square root, fused for speed.

62.text: Self-attention as a module. Four weight matrices, all managed by PyTorch.

65.text: nn.Linear wraps a weight matrix with proper initialization, gradient tracking, and GPU support. No bias, matching our architecture.

66.text: Key projection, same as lab 01's attn_wk, but PyTorch tracks gradients automatically.

67.text: Project the input into value vectors that carry the actual content to be aggregated by attention.

68.text: Project the concatenated multi-head output back into the embedding space.

70.text: Forward pass for attention. Notice: no cache management, no manual gradient logic. Just the math.

71.text: Unpack batch, sequence length, and channel dimensions. The B dimension is new, even though we train on one example, PyTorch expects batch format.

72.text: Project to Q, reshape into heads, and transpose so heads are a batch dimension. Same reshape as lab 02 but with PyTorch tensor ops.

73.text: Project into key vectors and reshape into separate attention heads. Keys advertise what each token contains so queries can find relevant matches.

74.text: Project into value vectors and reshape into heads. Values carry the actual content that will be retrieved and blended by the attention weights.

77.text: Scaled dot-product attention, one matrix multiply computes all attention scores. Same formula as lab 01's inner loop, fully vectorized.

78.text: Causal mask: upper triangle of True values. Positions can only attend to earlier positions.

79.text: Fill future positions with -inf so softmax gives them zero weight.

80.text: Softmax over the key dimension, each query gets a probability distribution over which keys to attend to.

82.text: Weighted value sum, transpose heads back, reshape to (B, T, C). The attention mechanism in three operations.

83.text: Output projection combines information from all heads.

86.text: MLP block, the "thinking" part of each transformer layer.

89.text: Expand to 4x dimension: the wider hidden layer provides capacity for more complex transformations.

92.text: Forward: expand, activate, contract. One line replaces lab 01's three-step MLP.

93.text: ReLU + two linear layers. The entire MLP in a single expression.

96.text: A transformer block combines attention and MLP with residual connections and normalization.

99.text: Normalize the input before the attention sub-layer. Pre-norm architecture stabilizes training by keeping activations on a consistent scale.

100.text: The self-attention sub-layer that lets each position gather information from all previous positions in the sequence.

101.text: Normalize before the feed-forward network. Same stabilizing role as the pre-attention norm, applied before a different transformation.

102.text: The position-wise feed-forward network that independently transforms each token's representation after attention has mixed information across positions.

104.text: The famous two-line transformer block: normalize-attend-add, normalize-MLP-add.

105.text: Attention with residual connection. Same as lab 01's manual x = [a + b for a, b in zip(x, x_residual)], but expressed as clean addition.

106.text: MLP with residual connection. The entire block is two residual sub-layers.

110.text: The complete GPT model. Four components: embeddings, normalization, transformer layers, and the output head.

113.text: nn.Embedding is a lookup table, same as indexing into lab 01's wte list, but with built-in gradient tracking.

114.text: Position embeddings, learned, not sinusoidal. Each position gets its own vector.

115.text: Initial RMSNorm before the transformer layers.

116.text: ModuleList registers all layers so PyTorch can find their parameters for optimization.

117.text: Output head: project from embedding space to vocabulary logits. No bias, matching our design.

118.text: Walk through every sub-module in the model and apply our custom N(0, 0.08) initialization. This ensures consistent starting conditions across all labs.

121.text: Static method, doesn't need self because initialization doesn't depend on the model instance.

122.text: Only initialize Linear and Embedding layers, skip RMSNorm (it has no weights).

125.text: The forward pass. Compare to lab 01: this is the same algorithm, but embedding lookups, matrix multiplies, normalization, and attention are all handled by PyTorch layers.

127.text: Look up each input token's learned embedding vector from the table. This converts discrete token IDs into continuous representations the model can process.

128.text: Position embedding: arange(T) generates [0, 1, ..., T-1] for position IDs.

129.text: Fuse embeddings, normalize, and enter the transformer.

130.text: Pass the representation through each transformer layer in sequence. With one layer this loops once, but the code scales to any depth.

131.text: Each layer applies attention + MLP with residual connections.

132.text: Project to vocabulary logits, the model's prediction for each position.

138.text: CPU training. For this tiny model, CPU is fine.

139.text: Instantiate and move to device. .to(device) would matter for GPU; here it's a no-op.

142.text: PyTorch's built-in Adam optimizer. One line replaces the manual Adam implementation from labs 01-02. Same hyperparameters: lr=0.01, betas=(0.85, 0.99).

145.text: Iterate over training steps, processing one name per step to update the model weights.

146.text: Cycle through the dataset with modular indexing. After processing all names, we loop back for another pass.

148.text: Clip to the maximum context window. Most names fit easily, but this prevents overflow on unusually long entries.

150.text: Create input tensor with batch dimension. The [...] wrapping adds the batch dimension, PyTorch always expects (B, T) shape.

151.text: Target tensor: same tokens shifted by one position.

153.text: Forward pass. All gradient tracking happens automatically, PyTorch builds the computation graph behind the scenes.

154.text: cross_entropy combines softmax + negative log likelihood in one numerically stable operation. Replaces lab 01's manual softmax + log + negation.

156.text: Zero gradients from previous step. Same as lab 01's p.grad = 0, but for all parameters at once.

157.text: THE magic line. loss.backward() computes all gradients automatically. This one call replaces lab 01's entire Value.backward() engine AND lab 02's 80+ lines of hand-derived gradients.

158.text: Linear learning rate decay, same schedule as previous labs.

159.text: PyTorch groups parameters; we update the learning rate for all groups.

160.text: Write the decayed learning rate into the optimizer. Unlike labs 01-02 where we computed lr manually each step, here we just update one config value.

161.text: Apply the Adam update. The optimizer reads the gradients computed by backward() and updates all parameters.

163.text: Print the loss periodically so we can monitor whether the model is learning.

164.text: .item() extracts the Python float from a 0-dim tensor, needed for printing.

169.text: Temperature 0.5 makes generation more conservative: the model favors its highest-confidence predictions, producing more typical-sounding names.

171.text: Switch to eval mode, disables dropout (if any). Good practice, though our model doesn't use dropout.

172.text: torch.no_grad() disables gradient tracking during inference, saves memory and computation. We're only generating, not training.

173.text: Generate 20 brand-new names by sampling from the model's learned distribution.

174.text: Begin each generated name with the beginning-of-sequence token as the initial prompt.

176.text: Feed the generated sequence (up to context length) as a batch of 1.

177.text: Forward pass, no gradient tracking needed.

178.text: Take the last position's logits, apply temperature.

179.text: Convert the temperature-scaled logits into a proper probability distribution that sums to one, ready for sampling.

180.text: multinomial samples one token from the distribution. PyTorch's equivalent of lab 01's random.choices.

181.text: When the model predicts BOS, it signals the name is complete, so we stop generating.

183.text: Append the newly sampled token to the growing sequence so it becomes part of the context for predicting the next character.

184.text: Convert token IDs to characters, skipping the initial BOS.

185.text: Print the finished generated name. These are entirely novel names the model invented from patterns it learned during training.

title: PyTorch Batched
tagline: Mini-batch training bridges the gap between educational toy and how real models are trained.
code:
"""
microGPT — PyTorch batched edition.

Same architecture as 03_pytorch, but with proper mini-batch training:
  - Batches of 32 sequences per step
  - Padding + attention masking
  - Scaled-up model (2 layers, 64-dim embeddings, context 16)
  - 1000 training steps

This is what "engineering for efficiency" looks like on top of the same algorithm.
"""

import math
import os
import random

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F

random.seed(42)
torch.manual_seed(42)

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Dataset & Tokenizer
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
input_path = os.path.join(os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__)), "..", "data", "input.txt")
if not os.path.exists(input_path):
    import urllib.request

    url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/karpathy/makemore/refs/heads/master/names.txt"
    urllib.request.urlretrieve(url, input_path)

docs = [l.strip() for l in open(input_path).read().strip().split("\n") if l.strip()]
random.shuffle(docs)
print(f"num docs: {len(docs)}")

uchars = sorted(set("".join(docs)))
BOS = len(uchars)
vocab_size = len(uchars) + 1
PAD = vocab_size  # padding token (not in vocab, ignored in loss)
vocab_size_with_pad = vocab_size + 1
print(f"vocab size: {vocab_size} (+1 pad = {vocab_size_with_pad})")

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Hyperparameters (scaled up from the single-sample version)
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
n_embd = 64     # embedding dimension
n_head = 4      # number of attention heads
n_layer = 2     # number of layers
block_size = 16 # maximum sequence length
head_dim = n_embd // n_head # dimension of each head
batch_size = 32
num_steps = 1000


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Model (same architecture, just bigger)
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
class RMSNorm(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, _dim, eps=1e-5):
        super().__init__()
        self.eps = eps

    def forward(self, x):
        return x * torch.rsqrt(x.pow(2).mean(-1, keepdim=True) + self.eps)


class CausalSelfAttention(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.wq = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)
        self.wk = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)
        self.wv = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)
        self.wo = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)

    def forward(self, x, pad_mask=None):
        B, T, C = x.shape
        q = self.wq(x).view(B, T, n_head, head_dim).transpose(1, 2)
        k = self.wk(x).view(B, T, n_head, head_dim).transpose(1, 2)
        v = self.wv(x).view(B, T, n_head, head_dim).transpose(1, 2)

        att = (q @ k.transpose(-2, -1)) / math.sqrt(head_dim)
        # Causal mask
        causal = torch.triu(torch.ones(T, T, device=x.device), diagonal=1).bool()
        att = att.masked_fill(causal, float("-inf"))
        # Padding mask: don't attend to PAD positions
        if pad_mask is not None:
            # pad_mask: (B, T), True where padded
            att = att.masked_fill(pad_mask[:, None, None, :], float("-inf"))
        att = F.softmax(att, dim=-1)
        att = torch.nan_to_num(att)  # handle all-masked rows

        out = (att @ v).transpose(1, 2).reshape(B, T, C)
        return self.wo(out)


class MLP(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.fc1 = nn.Linear(n_embd, 4 * n_embd, bias=False)
        self.fc2 = nn.Linear(4 * n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)

    def forward(self, x):
        return self.fc2(F.relu(self.fc1(x)))


class Block(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.norm1 = RMSNorm(n_embd)
        self.attn = CausalSelfAttention()
        self.norm2 = RMSNorm(n_embd)
        self.mlp = MLP()

    def forward(self, x, pad_mask=None):
        x = x + self.attn(self.norm1(x), pad_mask)
        x = x + self.mlp(self.norm2(x))
        return x


class MicroGPT(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.wte = nn.Embedding(vocab_size_with_pad, n_embd, padding_idx=PAD)
        self.wpe = nn.Embedding(block_size, n_embd)
        self.norm_in = RMSNorm(n_embd)
        self.layers = nn.ModuleList([Block() for _ in range(n_layer)])
        self.lm_head = nn.Linear(n_embd, vocab_size, bias=False)  # output over real vocab only
        self.apply(self._init_weights)

    @staticmethod
    def _init_weights(module):
        if isinstance(module, (nn.Linear, nn.Embedding)):
            nn.init.normal_(module.weight, mean=0.0, std=0.08)

    def forward(self, idx, pad_mask=None):
        B, T = idx.shape
        tok_emb = self.wte(idx)
        pos_emb = self.wpe(torch.arange(T, device=idx.device))
        x = self.norm_in(tok_emb + pos_emb)
        for layer in self.layers:
            x = layer(x, pad_mask)
        return self.lm_head(x)


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Batching utilities
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
def make_batch(docs, step, batch_size):
    """Create a padded batch of token sequences."""
    sequences = []
    for i in range(batch_size):
        doc = docs[(step * batch_size + i) % len(docs)]
        toks = [BOS] + [uchars.index(ch) for ch in doc] + [BOS]
        toks = toks[: block_size + 1]  # truncate to max length
        sequences.append(toks)

    max_len = max(len(s) for s in sequences)
    input_ids = []
    target_ids = []
    pad_masks = []
    for s in sequences:
        n = len(s) - 1
        inp = s[:n] + [PAD] * (max_len - 1 - n)
        tgt = s[1 : n + 1] + [-100] * (max_len - 1 - n)  # -100 = ignore in cross_entropy
        mask = [False] * n + [True] * (max_len - 1 - n)
        input_ids.append(inp)
        target_ids.append(tgt)
        pad_masks.append(mask)

    return (
        torch.tensor(input_ids),
        torch.tensor(target_ids),
        torch.tensor(pad_masks),
    )


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Training
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
device = "cpu"
model = MicroGPT().to(device)
print(f"num params: {sum(p.numel() for p in model.parameters())}")

optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=1e-2, betas=(0.85, 0.99), eps=1e-8)

for step in range(num_steps):
    input_ids, targets, pad_mask = make_batch(docs, step, batch_size)
    input_ids = input_ids.to(device)
    targets = targets.to(device)
    pad_mask = pad_mask.to(device)

    logits = model(input_ids, pad_mask)  # (B, T, V)
    loss = F.cross_entropy(logits.view(-1, vocab_size), targets.view(-1), ignore_index=-100)

    optimizer.zero_grad()
    loss.backward()
    lr_t = 1e-2 * (1 - step / num_steps)
    for pg in optimizer.param_groups:
        pg["lr"] = lr_t
    optimizer.step()

    if (step + 1) % 10 == 0 or step == 0:
        print(f"step {step + 1:4d} / {num_steps:4d} | loss {loss.item():.4f}")

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Inference
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
temperature = 0.5 # in (0, 1], control the "creativity" of generated text, low to high
print("\n--- inference (new, hallucinated names) ---")
model.eval()
with torch.no_grad():
    for sample_idx in range(20):
        tokens = [BOS]
        for _ in range(block_size):
            idx = torch.tensor([tokens[-block_size:]], device=device)
            logits = model(idx)
            logits = logits[0, -1] / temperature
            probs = F.softmax(logits, dim=-1)
            token_id = torch.multinomial(probs, 1).item()
            if token_id == BOS:
                break
            tokens.append(token_id)
        name = "".join(uchars[t] for t in tokens[1:])
        print(f"sample {sample_idx + 1:2d}: {name}")

15.text: Standard library for file path construction. Used to locate the shared dataset relative to the script.

16.text: Python's built-in random module, used here to shuffle the dataset into a random training order.

19.text: PyTorch's neural network building blocks: layers, modules, and everything needed to define model architecture.

20.text: Functional API for softmax, cross-entropy, etc.

22.text: Fix both Python and PyTorch random seeds so weight initialization and data ordering are identical across runs.

28.text: Dataset path, shared across all labs.

29.text: If the names dataset is not already on disk, download it automatically so the tutorial is self-contained.

32.text: URL pointing to the raw names dataset hosted in Karpathy's GitHub repository.

34.text: Read the text file and split it into individual name strings. Each line becomes one training document.

35.text: Shuffle the names so the model encounters them in random order rather than memorizing alphabetical patterns.

38.text: Build the character-level vocabulary: 26 lowercase letters, each mapped to a unique integer ID.

39.text: The beginning-of-sequence token doubles as the end-of-sequence marker for name boundaries.

40.text: The real vocabulary has 27 tokens: 26 letters plus the BOS/EOS marker. PAD is separate and never predicted.

41.text: PAD token for batch alignment. Sequences in a batch have different lengths, so shorter ones get padded to match the longest.

42.text: Expanded vocab includes PAD, the embedding table needs a row for it, even though it's never a real prediction target.

48.text: 4x larger embedding dimension than lab 03. Batching amortizes the overhead, so we can afford a bigger model.

49.text: Same 4 attention heads, but now each has 16 dimensions instead of 4.

50.text: 2 layers instead of 1, deeper model for richer representations.

51.text: Same 16-character context window as previous labs. Names longer than this get truncated.

52.text: Each head now has 16 dimensions (64 / 4), four times larger than in lab 03. More dimensions per head means richer attention patterns.

53.text: 32 sequences per training step, the model sees 32 names simultaneously and averages their gradients.

54.text: 1000 steps, same as previous labs. But each step processes 32x more data.

60.text: RMSNorm, identical to lab 03. The architecture doesn't change, just the scale.

69.text: Attention module, now with a pad_mask parameter. This is the key addition for batching.

72.text: Same Q, K, V projections as lab 03.

73.text: Project the normalized input into key vectors that other positions will compare against.

74.text: Project the input into value vectors that carry content to be aggregated by attention weights.

77.text: Attention now accepts a padding mask, the model needs to know which positions are real vs. padding.

80.text: Project into key vectors and reshape into separate attention heads. Keys let each position advertise its content for query matching.
