81.text: Project into value vectors and reshape into heads. Values carry the content that attention weights will blend together.

83.text: Compute attention scores by taking the dot product of queries and keys, scaled by the square root of head dimension to prevent saturation.

85.text: Causal mask: same as lab 03, prevents attending to future positions.

86.text: Set future positions to negative infinity so the model cannot cheat by looking ahead.

88.text: Padding mask: if provided, also prevent attending to PAD positions.

90.text: Expand pad_mask to (B, 1, 1, T) and fill PAD positions with -inf. Without this, the model would learn spurious patterns from padding tokens.

91.text: Turn raw attention scores into a probability distribution so weights sum to one across keys.

92.text: Safety net: when ALL keys are masked (a fully-padded row), softmax produces NaN. Replace with 0 so the model ignores those positions entirely.

95.text: Project the concatenated multi-head attention output back into the embedding space.

101.text: Widen the representation to 4x the embedding dimension for richer nonlinear transformations.

102.text: Compress the expanded hidden state back to the original embedding dimension.

104.text: Apply ReLU activation between two linear layers, forming the feed-forward sub-network.

105.text: The entire MLP compresses into a single chained expression in PyTorch.

108.text: Transformer block, now passes pad_mask through to attention.

111.text: Normalize the input before passing it to the attention sub-layer for stable training.

112.text: The attention sub-layer now receives the padding mask so it knows which positions hold real tokens versus filler.

113.text: Normalize the input before the MLP sub-layer, following the pre-norm transformer pattern.

114.text: The feed-forward network that processes each position independently after attention mixing.

117.text: Attention receives the mask so it can ignore padded positions.

118.text: MLP doesn't need the mask, it operates position-independently.

122.text: Complete model, embedding table is now larger to accommodate the PAD token.

125.text: padding_idx=PAD tells the embedding to output zeros for PAD tokens and exclude them from gradient updates. A clean way to handle variable-length sequences.

127.text: Normalize embeddings before entering the transformer stack. This initial normalization keeps activations stable from the start.

128.text: Register all transformer layers so PyTorch can discover and optimize their parameters.

129.text: Output head maps to real vocab only (27 tokens, not 28). PAD is never a valid prediction.

130.text: Walk through every sub-module and apply N(0, 0.08) initialization, ensuring consistent starting conditions across all labs.

138.text: Unpack batch size B and sequence length T from the input shape. These dimensions drive all the downstream tensor operations.

139.text: Token embeddings, PAD tokens get zero vectors thanks to padding_idx.

140.text: Position embeddings broadcast across the batch.

141.text: Combine token and position embeddings and normalize before entering the transformer stack.

142.text: Pass the representation through each transformer layer in sequence. With two layers now, the model builds progressively richer representations.

143.text: Each layer receives the padding mask.

144.text: Output logits over the real vocabulary.

150.text: Batch creation: the infrastructure needed for mini-batch training. This is where you see the engineering overhead of batching.

153.text: Gather batch_size documents, cycling through the dataset.

154.text: Select the document for this batch position.

159.text: Find the longest sequence in this batch, everything gets padded to this length.

160.text: Allocate lists that will hold the padded input tokens, shifted targets, and boolean masks for every sequence in the batch.

163.text: For each sequence, create aligned input, target, and mask.

164.text: Actual sequence length (minus 1 for the target shift).

166.text: Targets: shifted tokens + -100 fill. PyTorch's cross_entropy ignores positions marked -100.

167.text: Boolean mask: False for real tokens, True for padding.

173.text: The input tensor of shape (B, max_T) containing token IDs, with PAD filling shorter sequences to the batch's maximum length.

174.text: The target tensor of shape (B, max_T) with shifted tokens as labels. Padded positions use -100 so cross-entropy ignores them.

175.text: A boolean mask of shape (B, max_T) marking which positions are real tokens (False) and which are padding (True).

182.text: Run training on CPU, which is sufficient for this small model and dataset size.

183.text: Create the model with the scaled-up architecture: 64-dim embeddings, 4 heads, and 2 transformer layers.

184.text: Parameter count, much larger than lab 03 due to bigger dimensions and 2 layers.

186.text: Same Adam optimizer configuration as all previous labs: lr=0.01, betas=(0.85, 0.99). Only the model size and batch size changed.

188.text: Iterate over training steps, processing a batch of 32 names at each step.

189.text: Assemble a padded batch of 32 tokenized names, aligning them to the same length with PAD tokens and building the corresponding masks.

190.text: Transfer input token IDs to the compute device. On CPU this is a no-op, but the pattern generalizes to GPU training.

191.text: Transfer target labels to the compute device, matching where the model's output tensors will live.

192.text: Transfer the padding mask to the same device so it can be applied directly to the attention score matrix.

194.text: Forward pass with padding mask, the model knows which positions to ignore.

195.text: ignore_index=-100 means the loss is only computed on real tokens, not padding. This is crucial, otherwise shorter sequences would be penalized differently than longer ones.

197.text: Clear accumulated gradients from the previous step before computing new ones.

198.text: Backward pass, autograd computes gradients for all parameters through the batched forward pass.

199.text: Linearly reduce the learning rate toward zero over the course of training.

200.text: Update learning rate for all parameter groups.

201.text: Write the decayed learning rate into the optimizer's parameter group.

202.text: Use the Adam optimizer to update all model weights based on the computed gradients.

204.text: Print the loss periodically so we can monitor whether the model is learning.

210.text: Temperature 0.5 makes generation more conservative: the model favors high-confidence predictions, producing more typical-sounding names.

212.text: Switch the model to evaluation mode, which disables training-specific behaviors like dropout.

214.text: Generate 20 brand-new names by sampling from the model's learned distribution.

215.text: Begin each generated name with the beginning-of-sequence token as the initial prompt.

217.text: Feed generated sequence as a batch of 1. No padding needed for single-sequence inference.

218.text: Forward pass, no pad_mask needed for single sequences.

219.text: Grab the logits from the last generated position and divide by temperature. Lower temperature sharpens the distribution toward the top predictions.

220.text: Convert the temperature-scaled logits into a proper probability distribution that sums to one, ready for sampling.

221.text: Draw one token from the probability distribution to continue generating the name.

222.text: When the model outputs the BOS token during generation, it signals the name is complete and we stop adding characters.

224.text: Append the newly sampled token to the growing sequence so it becomes context for predicting the next character.

225.text: Map each token ID back to its character. Skip the leading BOS to get just the generated name text.

226.text: Display the completed generated name after all characters have been sampled.

title: JAX
tagline: Purely functional style. No classes, no hidden state, no mutation. Every function takes its inputs and returns its outputs.
code:
"""
microGPT — JAX edition.

Same architecture as the PyTorch version, but in JAX's functional style:
  - All parameters are explicit pytrees (no hidden state)
  - Forward pass is a pure function (no side effects)
  - Gradients via jax.grad (automatic, like PyTorch, but functional)
  - JIT compilation for speed
  - Explicit PRNG key threading

This shows how the same transformer looks in a purely functional framework.
"""

import math
import os
import random

import jax
import jax.numpy as jnp
from jax import grad, jit

random.seed(42)

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Dataset & Tokenizer
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
input_path = os.path.join(os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__)), "..", "data", "input.txt")
if not os.path.exists(input_path):
    import urllib.request

    url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/karpathy/makemore/refs/heads/master/names.txt"
    urllib.request.urlretrieve(url, input_path)

docs = [l.strip() for l in open(input_path).read().strip().split("\n") if l.strip()]
random.shuffle(docs)
print(f"num docs: {len(docs)}")

uchars = sorted(set("".join(docs)))
BOS = len(uchars)
vocab_size = len(uchars) + 1
print(f"vocab size: {vocab_size}")

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Parameters as a flat dict (pytree)
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
n_embd = 16     # embedding dimension
n_head = 4      # number of attention heads
n_layer = 1     # number of layers
block_size = 16 # maximum sequence length
head_dim = n_embd // n_head # dimension of each head

key = jax.random.PRNGKey(42)


def init_param(key, shape, std=0.08):
    return jax.random.normal(key, shape) * std


keys = jax.random.split(key, 20)
ki = iter(keys)

params = {
    "wte": init_param(next(ki), (vocab_size, n_embd)),
    "wpe": init_param(next(ki), (block_size, n_embd)),
    "lm_head": init_param(next(ki), (vocab_size, n_embd)),
}
for i in range(n_layer):
    params[f"l{i}.wq"] = init_param(next(ki), (n_embd, n_embd))
    params[f"l{i}.wk"] = init_param(next(ki), (n_embd, n_embd))
    params[f"l{i}.wv"] = init_param(next(ki), (n_embd, n_embd))
    params[f"l{i}.wo"] = init_param(next(ki), (n_embd, n_embd))
    params[f"l{i}.fc1"] = init_param(next(ki), (n_embd, 4 * n_embd))
    params[f"l{i}.fc2"] = init_param(next(ki), (4 * n_embd, n_embd))

num_params = sum(p.size for p in jax.tree.leaves(params))
print(f"num params: {num_params}")


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Pure-function forward pass
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
def rmsnorm(x):
    ms = jnp.mean(x**2, axis=-1, keepdims=True)
    return x / jnp.sqrt(ms + 1e-5)


def forward(params, input_ids):
    """
    params: dict of arrays
    input_ids: (n,) int array
    Returns: logits (n, vocab_size)
    """
    n = input_ids.shape[0]
    tok_emb = params["wte"][input_ids]  # (n, D)
    pos_emb = params["wpe"][jnp.arange(n)]  # (n, D)
    x = rmsnorm(tok_emb + pos_emb)

    for li in range(n_layer):
        x_res = x
        x_n = rmsnorm(x)

        Q = x_n @ params[f"l{li}.wq"]  # (n, D)
        K = x_n @ params[f"l{li}.wk"]
        V = x_n @ params[f"l{li}.wv"]

        # Multi-head reshape: (n, nh, hd) -> (nh, n, hd)
        Q_h = Q.reshape(n, n_head, head_dim).transpose(1, 0, 2)
        K_h = K.reshape(n, n_head, head_dim).transpose(1, 0, 2)
        V_h = V.reshape(n, n_head, head_dim).transpose(1, 0, 2)

        att = Q_h @ K_h.transpose(0, 2, 1) / math.sqrt(head_dim)
        causal_mask = jnp.triu(jnp.ones((n, n)), k=1).astype(bool)
        att = jnp.where(causal_mask, -1e9, att)
        att = jax.nn.softmax(att, axis=-1)

        attn_out = att @ V_h  # (nh, n, hd)
        attn_cat = attn_out.transpose(1, 0, 2).reshape(n, n_embd)
        x = attn_cat @ params[f"l{li}.wo"] + x_res

        # MLP
        x_res2 = x
        x_n2 = rmsnorm(x)
        h = x_n2 @ params[f"l{li}.fc1"]
        h = jax.nn.relu(h)
        x = h @ params[f"l{li}.fc2"] + x_res2

    logits = x @ params["lm_head"].T  # (n, V)
    return logits


def loss_fn(params, input_ids, targets):
    """Cross-entropy loss, pure function suitable for jax.grad."""
    logits = forward(params, input_ids)
    log_probs = jax.nn.log_softmax(logits, axis=-1)
    n = input_ids.shape[0]
    loss = -jnp.mean(log_probs[jnp.arange(n), targets])
    return loss


# JIT-compile the gradient function
grad_fn = jit(grad(loss_fn))
loss_fn_jit = jit(loss_fn)

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Adam optimizer (functional — no hidden state mutation)
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
learning_rate, beta1, beta2, eps_adam = 1e-2, 0.85, 0.99, 1e-8
m_state = jax.tree.map(jnp.zeros_like, params)
v_state = jax.tree.map(jnp.zeros_like, params)


def adam_update(params, grads, m_state, v_state, step, lr):
    new_params = {}
    new_m = {}
    new_v = {}
    for k in params:
        new_m[k] = beta1 * m_state[k] + (1 - beta1) * grads[k]
        new_v[k] = beta2 * v_state[k] + (1 - beta2) * grads[k] ** 2
        m_hat = new_m[k] / (1 - beta1 ** (step + 1))
        v_hat = new_v[k] / (1 - beta2 ** (step + 1))
        new_params[k] = params[k] - lr * m_hat / (jnp.sqrt(v_hat) + eps_adam)
    return new_params, new_m, new_v


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Training loop
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
num_steps = 1000
for step in range(num_steps):
    doc = docs[step % len(docs)]
    tokens = [BOS] + [uchars.index(ch) for ch in doc] + [BOS]
    n = min(block_size, len(tokens) - 1)

    input_ids = jnp.array(tokens[:n])
    targets = jnp.array(tokens[1 : n + 1])

    loss_val = loss_fn_jit(params, input_ids, targets)
    grads = grad_fn(params, input_ids, targets)

    lr_t = learning_rate * (1 - step / num_steps)
    params, m_state, v_state = adam_update(params, grads, m_state, v_state, step, lr_t)

    if (step + 1) % 10 == 0 or step == 0:
        print(f"step {step + 1:4d} / {num_steps:4d} | loss {loss_val:.4f}")

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Inference
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
temperature = 0.5 # in (0, 1], control the "creativity" of generated text, low to high
print("\n--- inference (new, hallucinated names) ---")
rng_key = jax.random.PRNGKey(0)
for sample_idx in range(20):
    tokens = [BOS]
    for _ in range(block_size):
        input_ids = jnp.array(tokens)
        logits = forward(params, input_ids)
        logits = logits[-1] / temperature
        rng_key, subkey = jax.random.split(rng_key)
        token_id = jax.random.categorical(subkey, logits).item()
        if token_id == BOS:
            break
        tokens.append(token_id)
    name = "".join(uchars[t] for t in tokens[1:])
    print(f"sample {sample_idx + 1:2d}: {name}")

14.text: Standard math library, used here for the square root in attention scaling. JAX handles everything else.

15.text: Provides utilities for constructing file paths to locate the dataset on disk.

16.text: Python's built-in random module for shuffling the dataset. JAX has its own separate RNG for tensor operations.

18.text: JAX: Google's functional autodiff framework. A completely different philosophy from PyTorch, no classes, no hidden state, pure functions everywhere.

19.text: jnp is JAX's NumPy replacement, same API, but arrays live on GPU/TPU and support automatic differentiation.

20.text: grad transforms a function into its gradient function. jit compiles Python into optimized machine code. These are JAX's core transformations.

29.text: Download the names dataset from the internet if it is not already present locally.

32.text: URL pointing to the raw names dataset hosted in Karpathy's repository.

34.text: Read the text file and split it into individual name strings, one per line.

35.text: Randomize the training order so the model does not memorize sequences of names.

38.text: The set of unique characters in the dataset forms our vocabulary. Each character gets a fixed integer ID for the model to work with.

39.text: The beginning-of-sequence token doubles as the end-of-sequence marker for name boundaries.

40.text: The vocabulary has 27 tokens: 26 lowercase letters plus the BOS/EOS marker.

47.text: Split attention into 4 parallel heads so each can focus on different character relationships.

48.text: A single transformer layer, keeping the model minimal for this small-scale experiment.

49.text: Names up to 16 characters long fit in the context window. Longer names get truncated, but most fit easily.

50.text: Each of the 4 heads gets 4 dimensions of the 16-dimensional embedding, letting heads specialize on different character relationships.

52.text: JAX's PRNG is explicit, you create a key and split it for each random operation. No hidden global state, unlike NumPy/PyTorch.

55.text: Parameter initializer, same N(0, 0.08) distribution, but using JAX's RNG.

56.text: JAX random requires an explicit key for every call. This makes randomness reproducible and parallelizable.

59.text: Pre-split 20 random keys for parameter initialization. JAX forces you to be explicit about where randomness enters the computation.

60.text: Iterator over keys, each parameter gets its own unique key.

62.text: Parameters are a plain Python dict, no nn.Module, no parameter registration. In JAX, parameters are just data you pass to functions.

63.text: Initialize the token embedding table that maps each character to a learned vector.

64.text: Position embeddings give the model a sense of order. Each slot in the sequence has its own learned vector encoding 'where' a token sits.

65.text: The output head weight matrix that maps from embedding space back to vocabulary logits for next-token prediction.

67.text: Per-layer weights, same structure as previous labs, just stored in a dict.

68.text: Weight matrix that projects the normalized input into query vectors for attention.

69.text: Weight matrix that produces key vectors for positions to be matched against queries.

70.text: Weight matrix that produces value vectors carrying content to be mixed by attention.

71.text: Project the concatenated multi-head output back into the embedding space.

72.text: First MLP weight matrix that expands the representation to 4x the embedding dimension.

73.text: Second MLP weight matrix that compresses back to the original embedding dimension.

75.text: jax.tree.leaves extracts all arrays from the nested dict. JAX treats dicts as "pytrees", a core abstraction for working with nested parameter structures.

82.text: RMSNorm as a bare function, no class, no state. JAX style: functions in, functions out.

83.text: Mean of squares, same math as every other lab.

84.text: Divide by the root mean square to normalize. This keeps all vectors at roughly unit scale, preventing activations from growing or shrinking as they flow through layers.

87.text: The forward pass is a PURE FUNCTION: same inputs always produce the same outputs, no side effects. This is what makes jax.grad and jax.jit work, they need mathematical functions, not stateful objects.

93.text: Sequence length from the input shape.

94.text: Token embedding lookup via array indexing, same operation as PyTorch's nn.Embedding.

95.text: Position embeddings tell the model where each token sits in the sequence. Without these, the model would treat 'ab' and 'ba' identically.

96.text: Combine token and position embeddings and normalize before entering the transformer.

98.text: Iterate through each transformer layer. With one layer this runs once, but the code generalizes to deeper stacks.

99.text: Save the current hidden state so we can add it back after attention as a residual connection.

100.text: Normalize before the attention sub-layer. Pre-norm keeps activations stable so the attention mechanism receives well-scaled inputs.

102.text: Matrix multiply for Q projection. Explicit @ with the parameter array, no hidden weight storage.

103.text: Project the normalized input into key vectors that other positions will query against.

104.text: Project the normalized input into value vectors carrying content to aggregate.

107.text: Multi-head reshape, same transpose pattern as PyTorch.

108.text: Reshape keys into separate attention heads, matching the same multi-head pattern as queries.

109.text: Reshape values into separate attention heads for parallel processing.

111.text: Scaled dot-product attention. Same math, different framework.

112.text: Causal mask using JAX's array ops.

113.text: Apply mask with jnp.where, JAX's equivalent of PyTorch's masked_fill.

116.text: Multiply attention weights by value vectors. Tokens deemed relevant by the query-key scores contribute their content proportionally to the output.

117.text: Transpose and reshape heads back to (n, D).

118.text: Output projection + residual in one line.

121.text: Save the hidden state before the MLP for the second residual connection in this block.

122.text: Normalize before the feed-forward network, following the pre-norm transformer pattern.

123.text: Multiply by the expansion weights to widen the representation for richer transformations.

124.text: Apply ReLU activation to introduce nonlinearity between the two MLP linear layers.

125.text: Contract the MLP back to embedding dimension and add the residual. This completes the second sub-layer of the transformer block.

131.text: Loss function, also a pure function. Takes params, inputs, and targets; returns a scalar loss. This is exactly what jax.grad expects.

133.text: Run the forward pass inside the loss function. grad will differentiate through this entire computation.

134.text: log_softmax is numerically more stable than separate softmax + log.

135.text: Get the number of tokens in the input sequence for indexing into log probabilities.

136.text: Gather target log-probs and negate their mean. Cross-entropy in one expression.

141.text: The JAX magic: grad(loss_fn) returns a NEW FUNCTION that computes gradients. jit compiles it. No backward pass code, no gradient tape, just function transformation.

142.text: JIT-compile the loss function too for fast evaluation.

147.text: Same hyperparameters as all previous labs.

148.text: jax.tree.map applies zeros_like to every array in the params dict. Functional alternative to manually creating matching zero buffers.

149.text: Second moment state, same pytree structure.

152.text: Adam update as a pure function, takes old state, returns new state. No mutation. This is the functional paradigm: transform data, don't change it.

153.text: Allocate fresh output dictionaries instead of mutating the old ones. In JAX's functional style, immutability makes transformations like jit and grad safe.

156.text: Iterate over each parameter name to apply the same Adam formula. The dict structure mirrors the model's parameter layout.

157.text: First moment update, same formula as all previous labs.

158.text: Update the exponential moving average of squared gradients. This tracks gradient magnitude per parameter, enabling adaptive learning rates.

159.text: Correct the first moment estimate for its bias toward zero in early training steps.

160.text: Correct the second moment estimate for its initialization bias in early steps.

161.text: Parameter update. Creates a NEW array; the old one is untouched.

162.text: Return the complete new state, params, first moments, second moments.

168.text: Train for 1000 steps, one document per step. Each iteration: forward, loss, grad, Adam update.

169.text: Iterate over training steps, processing one name per step to update model parameters.

170.text: Cycle through the dataset with modular indexing. After seeing all names, we loop back for another pass.

171.text: Convert the name into a sequence of integer token IDs with BOS markers at both ends.

172.text: Clip to the maximum context window. Most names fit easily, but this guard prevents overflow on unusually long entries.

174.text: Convert the Python token list to a JAX array so it can flow through the JIT-compiled forward pass.

175.text: Wrap the target tokens as a JAX array. These are the correct next characters the model should learn to predict.

177.text: Evaluate the cross-entropy loss using the JIT-compiled function. JIT compilation means this runs as optimized machine code, not interpreted Python.

178.text: Compute gradients using the JIT-compiled gradient function. One call, and JAX's autodiff handles everything, same as PyTorch's loss.backward() but functional.

180.text: Linearly reduce the learning rate toward zero over the course of training.

181.text: Functional Adam update. Returns entirely new params, new moments, nothing is mutated. The old state could be garbage collected.

183.text: Print the loss periodically so we can monitor whether the model is learning.

189.text: Temperature 0.5 makes generation more conservative: the model favors high-confidence predictions, producing more typical-sounding names.

191.text: JAX's PRNG needs explicit keys for every random operation during inference too.

192.text: Generate 20 brand-new names by sampling from the model's learned distribution.

193.text: Begin each generated name with the beginning-of-sequence token as the initial prompt.

194.text: Generate one character at a time, up to the maximum sequence length. Each step feeds all generated tokens back through the model.

195.text: Convert growing token list to JAX array.

196.text: Forward pass, the same pure function, just called without gradient tracking.

197.text: Grab the logits for the last generated position and divide by temperature. Lower values make the distribution sharper and more predictable.

198.text: jax.random.split produces two new keys from one, JAX's way of consuming randomness without hidden state. Every random call needs a unique key.

199.text: categorical samples from log-probabilities (unnormalized logits). JAX's equivalent of PyTorch's multinomial.

200.text: When the model outputs the BOS token during generation, it signals the name is complete and we stop adding characters.

202.text: Append the newly sampled token to the growing list so it becomes part of the context for predicting the next character.

203.text: Map each token ID back to its character, skipping the leading BOS, to assemble the generated name as a readable string.

204.text: Print the finished name. These are novel names the model invented by learning the statistical patterns of 32K real names.

title: JAX Batched
tagline: Use jax.vmap to automatically vectorize single-example code across a batch. No rewriting needed.
code:
"""
microGPT — JAX batched edition.

Same architecture as 05_jax, but with mini-batch training via jax.vmap:
  - Write the forward pass for a single example
  - vmap automatically vectorizes it across a batch
  - Padding at the data level, but the model code stays single-example
  - Scaled-up model (2 layers, 64-dim embeddings, context 16)
  - Batches of 32, 1000 training steps

This is JAX's signature trick: "write for one, run for many."
"""

import math
import os
import random

import jax
import jax.numpy as jnp
from jax import grad, jit, vmap

random.seed(42)

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Dataset & Tokenizer
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
input_path = os.path.join(os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__)), "..", "data", "input.txt")
if not os.path.exists(input_path):
    import urllib.request

    url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/karpathy/makemore/refs/heads/master/names.txt"
    urllib.request.urlretrieve(url, input_path)

docs = [l.strip() for l in open(input_path).read().strip().split("\n") if l.strip()]
random.shuffle(docs)
print(f"num docs: {len(docs)}")

uchars = sorted(set("".join(docs)))
BOS = len(uchars)
vocab_size = len(uchars) + 1
PAD = vocab_size
vocab_size_with_pad = vocab_size + 1
print(f"vocab size: {vocab_size} (+1 pad = {vocab_size_with_pad})")

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Hyperparameters (scaled up)
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
n_embd = 64     # embedding dimension
n_head = 4      # number of attention heads
n_layer = 2     # number of layers
block_size = 16 # maximum sequence length
head_dim = n_embd // n_head # dimension of each head
batch_size = 32
num_steps = 1000

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Parameters
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
key = jax.random.PRNGKey(42)


def init_param(key, shape, std=0.08):
    return jax.random.normal(key, shape) * std


keys = jax.random.split(key, 30)
ki = iter(keys)

params = {
    "wte": init_param(next(ki), (vocab_size_with_pad, n_embd)),
    "wpe": init_param(next(ki), (block_size, n_embd)),
    "lm_head": init_param(next(ki), (vocab_size, n_embd)),
}
for i in range(n_layer):
    params[f"l{i}.wq"] = init_param(next(ki), (n_embd, n_embd))
    params[f"l{i}.wk"] = init_param(next(ki), (n_embd, n_embd))
    params[f"l{i}.wv"] = init_param(next(ki), (n_embd, n_embd))
    params[f"l{i}.wo"] = init_param(next(ki), (n_embd, n_embd))
    params[f"l{i}.fc1"] = init_param(next(ki), (n_embd, 4 * n_embd))
    params[f"l{i}.fc2"] = init_param(next(ki), (4 * n_embd, n_embd))

num_params = sum(p.size for p in jax.tree.leaves(params))
print(f"num params: {num_params}")


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Forward pass (single example — vmap will handle the batch)
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
def rmsnorm(x):
    ms = jnp.mean(x**2, axis=-1, keepdims=True)
    return x / jnp.sqrt(ms + 1e-5)


def forward_single(params, input_ids, pad_mask):
    """
    Forward pass for ONE sequence.
    input_ids: (T,) int array
    pad_mask: (T,) bool array, True where padded
    Returns: logits (T, vocab_size)
    """
    T = input_ids.shape[0]
    tok_emb = params["wte"][input_ids]  # (T, D)
    pos_emb = params["wpe"][jnp.arange(T)]  # (T, D)
    x = rmsnorm(tok_emb + pos_emb)

    for li in range(n_layer):
        x_res = x
        x_n = rmsnorm(x)

        Q = x_n @ params[f"l{li}.wq"]
        K = x_n @ params[f"l{li}.wk"]
        V = x_n @ params[f"l{li}.wv"]

        Q_h = Q.reshape(T, n_head, head_dim).transpose(1, 0, 2)
        K_h = K.reshape(T, n_head, head_dim).transpose(1, 0, 2)
        V_h = V.reshape(T, n_head, head_dim).transpose(1, 0, 2)

        att = Q_h @ K_h.transpose(0, 2, 1) / math.sqrt(head_dim)
        # Causal mask
        causal = jnp.triu(jnp.ones((T, T)), k=1).astype(bool)
        att = jnp.where(causal, -1e9, att)
        # Padding mask: don't attend to PAD positions
        att = jnp.where(pad_mask[None, None, :], -1e9, att)
        att = jax.nn.softmax(att, axis=-1)
        att = jnp.nan_to_num(att)

        attn_out = att @ V_h
        attn_cat = attn_out.transpose(1, 0, 2).reshape(T, n_embd)
        x = attn_cat @ params[f"l{li}.wo"] + x_res

        # MLP
        x_res2 = x
        x_n2 = rmsnorm(x)
        h = x_n2 @ params[f"l{li}.fc1"]
        h = jax.nn.relu(h)
        x = h @ params[f"l{li}.fc2"] + x_res2

    logits = x @ params["lm_head"].T  # (T, V)
    return logits


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# vmap: the JAX way to batch
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# forward_single works on one sequence. vmap lifts it to work on a batch.
# in_axes=(None, 0, 0) means: shared params, batched input_ids, batched pad_mask.
forward_batch = vmap(forward_single, in_axes=(None, 0, 0))


def loss_fn(params, input_ids, targets, pad_mask, target_mask):
    """
    Batched cross-entropy loss.
    input_ids: (B, T), targets: (B, T), pad_mask: (B, T), target_mask: (B, T)
    """
    logits = forward_batch(params, input_ids, pad_mask)  # (B, T, V)
    log_probs = jax.nn.log_softmax(logits, axis=-1)
    B, T, V = log_probs.shape
    # Gather log-probs for target tokens
    target_log_probs = log_probs[jnp.arange(B)[:, None], jnp.arange(T)[None, :], targets]  # (B, T)
    # Mask out padding positions
    target_log_probs = target_log_probs * target_mask
    loss = -jnp.sum(target_log_probs) / jnp.sum(target_mask)
    return loss


grad_fn = jit(grad(loss_fn))
loss_fn_jit = jit(loss_fn)

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Adam optimizer (functional)
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
learning_rate, beta1, beta2, eps_adam = 1e-2, 0.85, 0.99, 1e-8
m_state = jax.tree.map(jnp.zeros_like, params)
v_state = jax.tree.map(jnp.zeros_like, params)


def adam_update(params, grads, m_state, v_state, step, lr):
    new_params, new_m, new_v = {}, {}, {}
    for k in params:
        new_m[k] = beta1 * m_state[k] + (1 - beta1) * grads[k]
        new_v[k] = beta2 * v_state[k] + (1 - beta2) * grads[k] ** 2
        m_hat = new_m[k] / (1 - beta1 ** (step + 1))
        v_hat = new_v[k] / (1 - beta2 ** (step + 1))
        new_params[k] = params[k] - lr * m_hat / (jnp.sqrt(v_hat) + eps_adam)
    return new_params, new_m, new_v


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Batching utility
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
def make_batch(docs, step, batch_size):
    """Create a padded batch of token sequences."""
    batch_docs = [docs[(step * batch_size + i) % len(docs)] for i in range(batch_size)]
    sequences = []
    for doc in batch_docs:
        toks = [BOS] + [uchars.index(ch) for ch in doc] + [BOS]
        toks = toks[: block_size + 1]
        sequences.append(toks)

    max_len = max(len(s) for s in sequences)
    input_ids, target_ids, pad_masks, target_masks = [], [], [], []
    for s in sequences:
        n = len(s) - 1
        inp = s[:n] + [PAD] * (max_len - 1 - n)
        tgt = s[1 : n + 1] + [0] * (max_len - 1 - n)  # 0 as dummy target for masked positions
        pmask = [False] * n + [True] * (max_len - 1 - n)
        tmask = [1.0] * n + [0.0] * (max_len - 1 - n)
        input_ids.append(inp)
        target_ids.append(tgt)
        pad_masks.append(pmask)
        target_masks.append(tmask)

    return (
        jnp.array(input_ids),
        jnp.array(target_ids),
        jnp.array(pad_masks),
        jnp.array(target_masks),
    )


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Training loop
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
for step in range(num_steps):
    input_ids, targets, pad_mask, target_mask = make_batch(docs, step, batch_size)

    loss_val = loss_fn_jit(params, input_ids, targets, pad_mask, target_mask)
    grads = grad_fn(params, input_ids, targets, pad_mask, target_mask)

    lr_t = learning_rate * (1 - step / num_steps)
    params, m_state, v_state = adam_update(params, grads, m_state, v_state, step, lr_t)

    if (step + 1) % 10 == 0 or step == 0:
        print(f"step {step + 1:4d} / {num_steps:4d} | loss {loss_val:.4f}")

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Inference (single-example, no vmap needed)
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
temperature = 0.5 # in (0, 1], control the "creativity" of generated text, low to high
print("\n--- inference (new, hallucinated names) ---")
rng_key = jax.random.PRNGKey(0)
for sample_idx in range(20):
    tokens = [BOS]
    for _ in range(block_size):
        input_ids = jnp.array(tokens)
        pad_mask = jnp.zeros(len(tokens), dtype=bool)
        logits = forward_single(params, input_ids, pad_mask)
        logits = logits[-1] / temperature
        rng_key, subkey = jax.random.split(rng_key)
        token_id = jax.random.categorical(subkey, logits).item()
        if token_id == BOS:
            break
        tokens.append(token_id)
    name = "".join(uchars[t] for t in tokens[1:])
    print(f"sample {sample_idx + 1:2d}: {name}")

14.text: Standard math library, used here for the square root in attention scaling. JAX handles everything else.

15.text: Provides utilities for constructing file paths to locate the dataset on disk.

16.text: Python's built-in random module for shuffling the dataset into a random training order.

19.text: JAX's drop-in NumPy replacement that runs on GPU/TPU and supports automatic differentiation.

20.text: vmap is the star of this lab, it automatically vectorizes a single-example function across a batch. Write once, batch for free.

22.text: Fix the Python random seed so dataset shuffling is identical across runs, making results reproducible.

28.text: Path to the shared dataset file used consistently across all labs.

29.text: Download the names dataset from the internet if it is not already present locally.

32.text: URL pointing to the raw names dataset hosted in Karpathy's repository.

34.text: Read the text file and split it into individual name strings, one per line.

35.text: Randomize the training order so the model does not memorize sequences of names.

38.text: The set of unique characters across all names forms our vocabulary. Each character gets a fixed integer ID.

39.text: The beginning-of-sequence token doubles as the end-of-sequence marker for name boundaries.

40.text: The real vocabulary has 27 tokens: 26 letters plus the BOS/EOS marker. PAD is tracked separately.

41.text: PAD token for aligning variable-length sequences in a batch.

42.text: The embedding table needs 28 rows: 27 real tokens plus one for PAD. But the output head only predicts over the 27 real tokens.

48.text: 4x larger embeddings, same scale-up as lab 04.

50.text: Two transformer layers give the model more depth to build progressively richer representations of character patterns.

51.text: Names up to 16 characters fit in the context window. Longer names get truncated, but most fit easily.

52.text: Each attention head operates on 16 dimensions, giving finer-grained representations than lab 05.

53.text: 32 sequences processed in parallel per training step. Batching amortizes overhead and gives the optimizer a smoother gradient signal.

54.text: Train for 1000 steps, same as all previous labs. But each step now processes 32 names at once, so the model sees 32x more data overall.

66.text: Pre-split enough random keys for all parameter initializations. JAX requires a fresh key for every random operation to stay deterministic.

67.text: Iterate through the pre-split random keys, giving each parameter its own unique source of randomness.

70.text: Token embeddings include a PAD row. Compare lab 04: vocab_size_with_pad for the embedding, vocab_size for the output head.

71.text: Position embeddings give the model a sense of order. Each position in the sequence has its own learned vector.

72.text: Output head, maps to real vocab only (27 tokens, no PAD).

75.text: Weight matrix that projects the input into query vectors for the attention mechanism.

76.text: Weight matrix that produces key vectors for positions to be matched against queries.

77.text: Weight matrix that produces value vectors carrying content to be mixed by attention.

78.text: Project the concatenated multi-head output back into the embedding space.

79.text: First MLP weight matrix that expands the representation to 4x the embedding dimension for richer nonlinear transformations.

80.text: Second MLP weight matrix that compresses back to the original embedding dimension.

82.text: Sum up all array sizes in the parameter tree to report the total trainable parameter count.

90.text: Compute the mean of squared values along the last axis for RMS normalization.

91.text: Divide by the root-mean-square to normalize the input, keeping direction but stabilizing scale.

94.text: Forward pass for a SINGLE sequence. This is the key insight: you write the simplest possible code for one example, and vmap handles batching. Compare lab 04 where the entire forward pass was rewritten for (B, T, C) shapes.

101.text: Sequence length from the single example.

102.text: Token embeddings, single sequence, no batch dimension.

103.text: Position embeddings tell the model where each token sits. Without these, the model could not distinguish token order.

104.text: Combine token and position embeddings and normalize before entering the transformer.

106.text: Iterate through each transformer layer. With two layers now, the model builds progressively richer representations.

107.text: Save the current hidden state for the residual connection after attention.

108.text: Normalize the input before passing it to the attention sub-layer for stable training.

110.text: Q projection, operates on (T, D), not (B, T, D). No batch handling needed in the model code.

111.text: Project the normalized input into key vectors that other positions will query against.

112.text: Project the normalized input into value vectors carrying content to aggregate.

114.text: Multi-head reshape, for a single sequence.

115.text: Reshape keys into separate attention heads, matching the same multi-head pattern as queries.

116.text: Reshape values into separate attention heads for parallel processing across heads.

118.text: Compute scaled dot-product attention scores between all query-key pairs.

120.text: Build an upper-triangular boolean mask that blocks attention to future positions.

121.text: Set future positions to a large negative value so softmax gives them zero weight.

123.text: Padding mask: block attention to PAD positions. Even though this is single-example code, we still need the mask because padded tokens exist within each sequence.

124.text: Turn raw attention scores into a probability distribution so weights sum to one.

127.text: Multiply attention weights by value vectors to produce the attention sub-layer output.

128.text: Transpose attention heads back and concatenate them into the full embedding dimension.

129.text: Project the concatenated multi-head output and add the residual connection in one expression. This completes the attention sub-layer.

132.text: Save the hidden state before the MLP for the second residual connection in this block.

133.text: Normalize before the feed-forward network, following the pre-norm transformer pattern.

134.text: Multiply by the expansion weights to widen the representation for richer transformations.

135.text: Apply ReLU activation to introduce nonlinearity between the two MLP linear layers.

136.text: Contract the MLP back to embedding dimension and add the residual. This completes the second sub-layer of the transformer block.

138.text: Final logits over the real vocabulary.

147.text: THE magic line. vmap(forward_single, in_axes=(None, 0, 0)) creates a batched version automatically. None means params are shared (not batched); 0 means batch along the first axis for inputs and masks. This one line replaces all of lab 04's manual batch reshaping.

150.text: Batched loss function. The forward pass is already batched thanks to vmap.

155.text: Call the vmap'd forward, returns (B, T, V) logits from (B, T) inputs.

156.text: Log-softmax is more numerically stable than computing softmax then taking the log separately, avoiding potential underflow issues.

159.text: Gather log-probs for target tokens using advanced indexing. Fancy indexing: for each batch and position, grab the log-prob of the correct target.

161.text: Zero out padding positions so they don't affect the loss.

162.text: Average only over real (non-padded) tokens. Dividing by sum(target_mask) ensures short sequences aren't penalized relative to long ones.

166.text: JIT-compile the gradient function. The grad + jit chain works the same as lab 05.

167.text: JIT-compile the loss function too, so we can cheaply evaluate the loss without computing gradients when we just need to monitor progress.

172.text: Same Adam hyperparameters as every other lab: lr=0.01, betas=(0.85, 0.99). Consistency makes it easy to compare across frameworks.

173.text: First moment state, zeros matching param shapes.

174.text: Second moment state tracks the magnitude of recent gradients per parameter, enabling Adam's adaptive learning rate behavior.

178.text: Allocate fresh output dictionaries rather than mutating old ones. Immutability is essential for JAX's functional transformations to work correctly.

180.text: Update the running mean of gradients (first moment). This smooths out noisy single-step gradients, acting like momentum.

181.text: Update the running mean of squared gradients (second moment). This tracks gradient scale so rarely-updated parameters get larger steps.

182.text: Correct the first moment estimate for its bias toward zero in early training steps.

183.text: Correct the second moment estimate for its initialization bias in early steps.

184.text: Compute the updated parameter value using the bias-corrected moments and learning rate.

191.text: Batch construction, same padding strategy as lab 04, but using JAX arrays instead of PyTorch tensors.

193.text: Gather batch_size documents from the dataset, cycling through with modular indexing to cover the full training set.

195.text: Convert each document's characters into integer token IDs that the model can process.

196.text: Wrap each name with beginning-of-sequence tokens to mark the start and end boundaries.

197.text: Truncate to the maximum context length. Most names are short enough, but this guard prevents overflow.

200.text: Pad to the longest sequence in the batch.

201.text: For each sequence, create aligned input tokens, target tokens, padding masks, and target masks, all padded to the same length.

203.text: The actual number of real tokens in this sequence (before padding). Used to know where real data ends and padding begins.

205.text: Targets: shifted tokens + 0 as dummy for masked positions.

207.text: Target mask: 1.0 for real positions, 0.0 for padding. Used to weight the loss.

213.text: Pack all input sequences into a single JAX array of shape (B, max_T) for efficient batched computation.

214.text: Pack target tokens into a matching JAX array. Padded positions use dummy values that the target mask will zero out.

215.text: Boolean mask that tells attention which positions are padding and should be ignored.

216.text: Float mask that weights the loss so padding positions contribute zero to the gradient.

224.text: Iterate over training steps, processing a batch of 32 names per step.

225.text: Assemble a padded batch of tokenized sequences with all necessary masks, ready to feed into the vmap'd forward pass.

227.text: Evaluate the cross-entropy loss on this batch using the JIT-compiled loss function.

228.text: Compute gradients for all parameters using the JIT-compiled gradient function.

230.text: Linearly reduce the learning rate toward zero over the course of training.

231.text: Functional Adam update, replace the entire state.

233.text: Print the loss periodically so we can monitor whether the model is learning.

239.text: Temperature 0.5 makes generation more conservative: the model favors high-confidence predictions, producing more typical-sounding names.

241.text: Initialize a PRNG key for sampling during generation. Each sampling step will split off a fresh sub-key to stay deterministic.

242.text: Generate 20 brand-new names by sampling from the model's learned distribution.

243.text: Begin each generated name with the beginning-of-sequence token as the initial prompt.

244.text: Generate one character at a time, up to the maximum sequence length. Each step feeds all generated tokens through the model.

246.text: No padding during single-sequence inference, all False mask.

247.text: Use forward_single directly, no need for vmap when generating one sequence at a time.

248.text: Grab the logits for the last generated position and divide by temperature. Lower temperature makes the distribution sharper.

249.text: Split PRNG key for this sampling step.

250.text: Draw one token from the probability distribution to continue generating the name.

251.text: When the model predicts BOS, it signals the name is complete, so we stop generating.

253.text: Append the newly sampled token to the growing sequence for the next forward pass.

254.text: Map each token ID back to its character, skipping the leading BOS, to produce the generated name as a readable string.

255.text: Print the finished name. These names were invented by the model from statistical patterns it learned during training.

title: MLX (Apple Silicon)
tagline: Running on Apple Silicon GPU via MLX. A NumPy-like API with automatic differentiation and unified memory.
code:
"""
microGPT — MLX edition.

Same architecture, running on Apple Silicon GPU via MLX.
MLX has a NumPy-like API with automatic differentiation and lazy evaluation.
Arrays live in unified memory — no CPU/GPU transfers needed.
"""

import math
import os
import random

import mlx.core as mx
import mlx.nn as nn
import mlx.optimizers as optim
import mlx.utils

random.seed(42)
mx.random.seed(42)

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Dataset & Tokenizer
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
input_path = os.path.join(os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__)), "..", "data", "input.txt")
if not os.path.exists(input_path):
    import urllib.request

    url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/karpathy/makemore/refs/heads/master/names.txt"
    urllib.request.urlretrieve(url, input_path)

docs = [l.strip() for l in open(input_path).read().strip().split("\n") if l.strip()]
random.shuffle(docs)
print(f"num docs: {len(docs)}")

uchars = sorted(set("".join(docs)))
BOS = len(uchars)
vocab_size = len(uchars) + 1
print(f"vocab size: {vocab_size}")

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Model
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
n_embd = 16     # embedding dimension
n_head = 4      # number of attention heads
n_layer = 1     # number of layers
block_size = 16 # maximum sequence length
head_dim = n_embd // n_head # dimension of each head


class RMSNorm(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, _dim):
        super().__init__()
        self.eps = 1e-5

    def __call__(self, x):
        ms = mx.mean(x * x, axis=-1, keepdims=True)
        return x * mx.rsqrt(ms + self.eps)


class CausalSelfAttention(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.wq = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)
        self.wk = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)
        self.wv = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)
        self.wo = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)

    def __call__(self, x):
        n = x.shape[0]
        q = self.wq(x).reshape(n, n_head, head_dim).transpose(1, 0, 2)
        k = self.wk(x).reshape(n, n_head, head_dim).transpose(1, 0, 2)
        v = self.wv(x).reshape(n, n_head, head_dim).transpose(1, 0, 2)

        att = (q @ k.transpose(0, 2, 1)) / math.sqrt(head_dim)
        mask = mx.triu(mx.ones((n, n)), k=1).astype(mx.bool_)
        att = mx.where(mask, mx.array(-1e9), att)
        att = mx.softmax(att, axis=-1)

        out = (att @ v).transpose(1, 0, 2).reshape(n, n_embd)
        return self.wo(out)


class MLP(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.fc1 = nn.Linear(n_embd, 4 * n_embd, bias=False)
        self.fc2 = nn.Linear(4 * n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)

    def __call__(self, x):
        return self.fc2(mx.maximum(self.fc1(x), 0))


class Block(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.norm1 = RMSNorm(n_embd)
        self.attn = CausalSelfAttention()
        self.norm2 = RMSNorm(n_embd)
        self.mlp = MLP()

    def __call__(self, x):
        x = x + self.attn(self.norm1(x))
        x = x + self.mlp(self.norm2(x))
        return x


class MicroGPT(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.wte = nn.Embedding(vocab_size, n_embd)
        self.wpe = nn.Embedding(block_size, n_embd)
        self.norm_in = RMSNorm(n_embd)
        self.layers = [Block() for _ in range(n_layer)]
        self.lm_head = nn.Linear(n_embd, vocab_size, bias=False)

    def __call__(self, idx):
        n = idx.shape[0]
        tok_emb = self.wte(idx)
        pos_emb = self.wpe(mx.arange(n))
        x = self.norm_in(tok_emb + pos_emb)
        for layer in self.layers:
            x = layer(x)
        return self.lm_head(x)


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Training
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
model = MicroGPT()
# Match original init: N(0, 0.08) for all weights
weights = mlx.utils.tree_flatten(model.parameters())
model.load_weights([(k, mx.random.normal(v.shape) * 0.08) for k, v in weights])
num_params = sum(p.size for _, p in mlx.utils.tree_flatten(model.parameters()))
print(f"num params: {num_params}")

learning_rate, beta1, beta2, eps_adam = 1e-2, 0.85, 0.99, 1e-8


def loss_fn(model, input_ids, targets):
    logits = model(input_ids)  # (n, V)
    # Cross-entropy
    log_probs = logits - mx.logsumexp(logits, axis=-1, keepdims=True)
    n = input_ids.shape[0]
    loss = -mx.mean(log_probs[mx.arange(n), targets])
    return loss


loss_and_grad = nn.value_and_grad(model, loss_fn)
optimizer = optim.Adam(learning_rate=learning_rate, betas=[beta1, beta2], eps=eps_adam)

num_steps = 1000
for step in range(num_steps):
    doc = docs[step % len(docs)]
    tokens = [BOS] + [uchars.index(ch) for ch in doc] + [BOS]
    n = min(block_size, len(tokens) - 1)

    input_ids = mx.array(tokens[:n])
    targets = mx.array(tokens[1 : n + 1])

    loss_val, grads = loss_and_grad(model, input_ids, targets)

    # Linear LR decay
    lr_t = learning_rate * (1 - step / num_steps)
    optimizer.learning_rate = mx.array(lr_t)
    optimizer.update(model, grads)
    mx.eval(model.parameters(), optimizer.state)

    if (step + 1) % 10 == 0 or step == 0:
        print(f"step {step + 1:4d} / {num_steps:4d} | loss {loss_val.item():.4f}")

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Inference
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
temperature = 0.5 # in (0, 1], control the "creativity" of generated text, low to high
print("\n--- inference (new, hallucinated names) ---")
for sample_idx in range(20):
    tokens = [BOS]
    for _ in range(block_size):
        input_ids = mx.array(tokens)
        logits = model(input_ids)
        logits = logits[-1] / temperature
        token_id = mx.random.categorical(logits).item()
        if token_id == BOS:
            break
        tokens.append(token_id)
    name = "".join(uchars[t] for t in tokens[1:])
    print(f"sample {sample_idx + 1:2d}: {name}")

9.text: Standard math library, used here for the square root in attention scaling. MLX handles all tensor math.

10.text: Provides utilities for constructing file paths to locate the dataset on disk.

11.text: Python's built-in random module for shuffling the dataset into a random training order.

13.text: mlx.core is MLX's tensor library, similar to NumPy but runs on Apple Silicon GPU. Arrays live in unified memory shared between CPU and GPU.

14.text: mlx.nn provides neural network modules, feels like PyTorch's nn module.

15.text: MLX ships with built-in optimizers like Adam, so we don't need to implement the update logic manually as we did in JAX.

16.text: Utility functions for working with MLX's parameter trees.

19.text: MLX has its own RNG, seeded separately.

24.text: Dataset path, shared across all labs.

25.text: If the names dataset is not already on disk, download it automatically so the tutorial is self-contained.

28.text: URL pointing to the raw names dataset hosted in Karpathy's repository.

31.text: Read the text file and split it into individual name strings. Each line becomes one training document.

32.text: Randomize the training order so the model does not memorize sequences of names.

35.text: The set of unique characters across all names forms our vocabulary. Each character gets a fixed integer ID.

36.text: The beginning-of-sequence token doubles as the end-of-sequence marker for name boundaries.

37.text: The vocabulary has 27 tokens: 26 lowercase letters plus the BOS/EOS marker.

44.text: Split attention into 4 parallel heads so each can focus on different character relationships.

45.text: A single transformer layer keeps things minimal. One layer is enough to learn meaningful character-level patterns for names.

46.text: Names up to 16 characters fit in the context window. Most names are well within this limit.

47.text: Each attention head works with 4 dimensions, matching the small model scale.

50.text: RMSNorm module. MLX uses __call__ instead of PyTorch's forward, Python convention for callable objects.

53.text: A tiny constant added before division to prevent numerical explosion when activations are near zero. Standard in every norm layer.

55.text: MLX uses __call__ for the forward pass, not forward. A stylistic choice, both work.

56.text: Element-wise square, then mean. MLX arrays support the same operations as NumPy.

57.text: Scale by inverse RMS. mx.rsqrt is a fused reciprocal square root.

60.text: Self-attention module, structurally identical to PyTorch's version in lab 03.

63.text: nn.Linear in MLX works just like PyTorch's, stores a weight matrix, applies matrix multiply.

64.text: Project the normalized input into key vectors that other positions will compare against.

65.text: Project the input into value vectors that carry content to be aggregated by attention.

66.text: Project the concatenated multi-head output back into the embedding space.

68.text: Forward pass. No batch dimension here, MLX's single-example version operates on (T, C) tensors.

69.text: Extract the sequence length from the input tensor to set up attention masks.

70.text: Reshape into heads: (T, D) -> (T, nh, hd) -> (nh, T, hd). Same pattern across every lab.

71.text: Keys get the same multi-head reshape as queries. Each head's keys will be compared against only that head's queries.

72.text: Values get the same multi-head reshape. Each head independently retrieves content from its own subspace of the embedding.

74.text: Compute attention scores by taking the dot product of queries and keys, scaled by the square root of head dimension to prevent saturation.

75.text: Causal mask. MLX's triu works identically to NumPy/PyTorch.

76.text: Apply the causal mask using element-wise selection. Future positions get a large negative value so softmax assigns them zero weight.

77.text: Turn raw attention scores into a probability distribution so weights sum to one.

79.text: Attention output: weighted sum, transpose back, reshape to (T, D).

80.text: Project the combined multi-head output through the final linear layer.

86.text: Expand the representation to 4x the embedding dimension. This wider hidden layer gives the MLP more capacity for nonlinear transformations.

87.text: Compress the expanded hidden state back to the original embedding dimension.

89.text: Run the input through the MLP sub-layer combining expansion, activation, and contraction.

90.text: mx.maximum(x, 0) is MLX's ReLU. No separate F.relu function, just use the element-wise maximum.

93.text: Transformer block, same norm-attend-add, norm-MLP-add pattern.

96.text: Normalize the input before passing it to the attention sub-layer for stable training.

97.text: The self-attention sub-layer that mixes information across positions in the sequence.

98.text: Normalize before the feed-forward network, following the pre-norm transformer pattern.

99.text: The feed-forward network that processes each position independently after attention mixing.

101.text: Forward: same two-line transformer block as PyTorch.

102.text: Apply attention with a residual connection: normalize, attend to other positions, and add the result back to the original signal.

103.text: Apply the MLP with a residual connection that preserves the original signal.

107.text: The complete GPT model combining embeddings, normalization, transformer layers, and output head.

110.text: The token embedding lookup table. Each of the 27 tokens maps to a learned 16-dimensional vector capturing what that character 'means' to the model.

111.text: The position embedding table. Each of the 16 positions maps to a learned vector that tells the model where a token sits in the sequence.

112.text: Normalize embeddings before entering the transformer stack. This initial normalization keeps activations stable from the very start.

113.text: Transformer layers. MLX uses a plain Python list (not ModuleList), the framework still discovers parameters through its tree-walking utilities.

114.text: The output head projects from 16-dim embedding space to 27-dim vocabulary logits. Each output score indicates how likely a character is to come next.

116.text: Run the full model on the current input to produce next-token predictions.

117.text: Extract the sequence length from the input to generate matching position indices.

118.text: Look up a learned embedding vector for each input token in the sequence.

119.text: Generate position indices [0, 1, ..., n-1] and look up their learned embeddings. These give the model a sense of token order.

120.text: Combine token and position embeddings and normalize before entering the transformer.

121.text: Pass the representation through each transformer layer in sequence. The code scales to any depth even though we use just one layer here.

122.text: Each layer transforms the hidden state.

123.text: Map from embedding space to vocabulary logits. Each output value is a raw score for how likely each of the 27 tokens is to come next.

129.text: Instantiate the model. No .to(device) needed, MLX's unified memory means arrays are already accessible by both CPU and GPU.

131.text: Flatten the parameter tree for re-initialization.

132.text: Replace all weights with N(0, 0.08) random values. MLX doesn't have PyTorch's apply method, so we reinitialize through load_weights.

133.text: Sum up all parameter sizes to report the total number of trainable weights.

136.text: Adam hyperparameters, same across all labs.

139.text: Loss function takes the MODEL as its first argument, this is how MLX's value_and_grad knows which parameters to differentiate.

140.text: Run the full model on the current input to get next-token prediction logits.

142.text: Manual log-softmax: logits - logsumexp(logits). More numerically stable than log(softmax(x)).

143.text: Get the number of tokens in the input for indexing into log probabilities.

144.text: Gather target log-probs and negate their mean, cross-entropy loss.

148.text: nn.value_and_grad creates a function that returns both the loss value and gradients in one call. MLX's equivalent of PyTorch's forward + backward.

149.text: MLX's built-in Adam optimizer. Unlike JAX where we wrote Adam manually, MLX provides a stateful optimizer like PyTorch.

151.text: Train for 1000 steps, one document per step. Each iteration: forward, loss, gradients, Adam update.

152.text: Iterate over training steps, processing one name per step to update model weights.

153.text: Cycle through the dataset with modular indexing. After processing all names, loop back for another pass.

154.text: Convert the name into a sequence of integer token IDs with BOS markers at both ends.

155.text: Clip to the maximum context window. Most names fit easily, but this guard prevents overflow on unusually long entries.

157.text: Convert the Python token list to an MLX array so it can flow through the computation graph for forward and backward passes.

158.text: Wrap the target tokens as an MLX array. These are the correct next characters the model should learn to predict.

160.text: Compute loss and gradients in one call. Clean and efficient.

163.text: Linearly reduce the learning rate toward zero over the course of training.

164.text: Update the optimizer's learning rate. Must wrap in mx.array, MLX is strict about types.

165.text: Push the computed gradients into the model's parameters via the optimizer. MLX's stateful optimizer applies the Adam update rule internally.

166.text: Force evaluation. MLX uses lazy evaluation, operations build a computation graph but don't execute until you call mx.eval. This lets MLX fuse and optimize operations. Without this call, nothing would actually compute.

168.text: Print the loss periodically so we can monitor whether the model is learning.

169.text: .item() extracts a Python scalar from an MLX array.

174.text: Temperature 0.5 makes generation more conservative: the model favors high-confidence predictions, producing more typical-sounding names.

176.text: Generate 20 brand-new names by sampling from the model's learned distribution.

177.text: Begin each generated name with the beginning-of-sequence token as the initial prompt.

178.text: Generate one character at a time, up to the maximum sequence length. Each step feeds all generated tokens through the full model.

180.text: Run the full model on the current token sequence to predict the next character.

181.text: Grab the logits for the last generated position and divide by temperature. Lower temperature sharpens the distribution toward the top predictions.

182.text: Sample from categorical distribution. MLX's RNG doesn't require explicit key management like JAX.

183.text: When the model outputs the BOS token during generation, it signals the name is complete and we stop adding characters.

185.text: Append the newly sampled token to the growing sequence for the next forward pass.

186.text: Map each token ID back to its character, skipping the leading BOS, to produce the generated name as a readable string.

187.text: Print the finished name. These names were invented by the model from statistical patterns it learned during training.

title: MLX Batched
tagline: Mini-batch training on Apple Silicon, following the same padding and masking approach as PyTorch batched.
code:
"""
microGPT — MLX batched edition.

Same architecture as 07_mlx, but with mini-batch training:
  - Batches of 32 sequences per step
  - Padding + attention masking (same approach as PyTorch batched)
  - Scaled-up model (2 layers, 64-dim embeddings, context 16)
  - 1000 training steps

Unlike JAX's vmap, MLX batching works the PyTorch way: reshape your tensors
to include a batch dimension and write the forward pass to handle (B, T, ...).
Same idea, same manual padding — unified memory is the difference.
"""

import math
import os
import random

import mlx.core as mx
import mlx.nn as nn
import mlx.optimizers as optim
import mlx.utils

random.seed(42)
mx.random.seed(42)

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Dataset & Tokenizer
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
input_path = os.path.join(os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__)), "..", "data", "input.txt")
if not os.path.exists(input_path):
    import urllib.request

    url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/karpathy/makemore/refs/heads/master/names.txt"
    urllib.request.urlretrieve(url, input_path)

docs = [l.strip() for l in open(input_path).read().strip().split("\n") if l.strip()]
random.shuffle(docs)
print(f"num docs: {len(docs)}")

uchars = sorted(set("".join(docs)))
BOS = len(uchars)
vocab_size = len(uchars) + 1
PAD = vocab_size
vocab_size_with_pad = vocab_size + 1
print(f"vocab size: {vocab_size} (+1 pad = {vocab_size_with_pad})")

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Hyperparameters (scaled up)
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
n_embd = 64     # embedding dimension
n_head = 4      # number of attention heads
n_layer = 2     # number of layers
block_size = 16 # maximum sequence length
head_dim = n_embd // n_head # dimension of each head
batch_size = 32
num_steps = 1000


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Model
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
class RMSNorm(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, _dim):
        super().__init__()
        self.eps = 1e-5

    def __call__(self, x):
        ms = mx.mean(x * x, axis=-1, keepdims=True)
        return x * mx.rsqrt(ms + self.eps)


class CausalSelfAttention(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.wq = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)
        self.wk = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)
        self.wv = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)
        self.wo = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)

    def __call__(self, x, pad_mask=None):
        B, T, C = x.shape
        q = self.wq(x).reshape(B, T, n_head, head_dim).transpose(0, 2, 1, 3)
        k = self.wk(x).reshape(B, T, n_head, head_dim).transpose(0, 2, 1, 3)
        v = self.wv(x).reshape(B, T, n_head, head_dim).transpose(0, 2, 1, 3)

        att = (q @ k.transpose(0, 1, 3, 2)) / math.sqrt(head_dim)
        # Causal mask
        causal = mx.triu(mx.ones((T, T)), k=1).astype(mx.bool_)
        att = mx.where(causal, mx.array(-1e9), att)
        # Padding mask: don't attend to PAD positions
        if pad_mask is not None:
            att = mx.where(pad_mask[:, None, None, :], mx.array(-1e9), att)
        att = mx.softmax(att, axis=-1)
        att = mx.where(mx.isnan(att), mx.array(0.0), att)

        out = (att @ v).transpose(0, 2, 1, 3).reshape(B, T, C)
        return self.wo(out)


class MLP(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.fc1 = nn.Linear(n_embd, 4 * n_embd, bias=False)
        self.fc2 = nn.Linear(4 * n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)

    def __call__(self, x):
        return self.fc2(mx.maximum(self.fc1(x), 0))


class Block(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.norm1 = RMSNorm(n_embd)
        self.attn = CausalSelfAttention()
        self.norm2 = RMSNorm(n_embd)
        self.mlp = MLP()

    def __call__(self, x, pad_mask=None):
        x = x + self.attn(self.norm1(x), pad_mask)
        x = x + self.mlp(self.norm2(x))
        return x


class MicroGPT(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.wte = nn.Embedding(vocab_size_with_pad, n_embd)
        self.wpe = nn.Embedding(block_size, n_embd)
        self.norm_in = RMSNorm(n_embd)
        self.layers = [Block() for _ in range(n_layer)]
        self.lm_head = nn.Linear(n_embd, vocab_size, bias=False)

    def __call__(self, idx, pad_mask=None):
        B, T = idx.shape
        tok_emb = self.wte(idx)
        pos_emb = self.wpe(mx.arange(T))
        x = self.norm_in(tok_emb + pos_emb)
        for layer in self.layers:
            x = layer(x, pad_mask)
        return self.lm_head(x)


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Batching utility
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
def make_batch(docs, step, batch_size):
    """Create a padded batch of token sequences."""
    batch_docs = [docs[(step * batch_size + i) % len(docs)] for i in range(batch_size)]
    sequences = []
    for doc in batch_docs:
        toks = [BOS] + [uchars.index(ch) for ch in doc] + [BOS]
        toks = toks[: block_size + 1]
        sequences.append(toks)

    max_len = max(len(s) for s in sequences)
    input_ids, target_ids, pad_masks, target_masks = [], [], [], []
    for s in sequences:
        n = len(s) - 1
        inp = s[:n] + [PAD] * (max_len - 1 - n)
        tgt = s[1 : n + 1] + [0] * (max_len - 1 - n)
        pmask = [False] * n + [True] * (max_len - 1 - n)
        tmask = [1.0] * n + [0.0] * (max_len - 1 - n)
        input_ids.append(inp)
        target_ids.append(tgt)
        pad_masks.append(pmask)
        target_masks.append(tmask)

    return (
        mx.array(input_ids),
        mx.array(target_ids),
        mx.array(pad_masks),
        mx.array(target_masks),
    )


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Training
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
model = MicroGPT()
# Match original init: N(0, 0.08) for all weights
weights = mlx.utils.tree_flatten(model.parameters())
model.load_weights([(k, mx.random.normal(v.shape) * 0.08) for k, v in weights])
num_params = sum(p.size for _, p in mlx.utils.tree_flatten(model.parameters()))
print(f"num params: {num_params}")

learning_rate, beta1, beta2, eps_adam = 1e-2, 0.85, 0.99, 1e-8


def loss_fn(model, input_ids, targets, pad_mask, target_mask):
    logits = model(input_ids, pad_mask)  # (B, T, V)
    log_probs = logits - mx.logsumexp(logits, axis=-1, keepdims=True)
    B, T, V = log_probs.shape
    # Gather log-probs for target tokens
    target_log_probs = log_probs[mx.arange(B)[:, None], mx.arange(T)[None, :], targets]
    target_log_probs = target_log_probs * target_mask
    loss = -mx.sum(target_log_probs) / mx.sum(target_mask)
    return loss


loss_and_grad = nn.value_and_grad(model, loss_fn)
optimizer = optim.Adam(learning_rate=learning_rate, betas=[beta1, beta2], eps=eps_adam)

for step in range(num_steps):
    input_ids, targets, pad_mask, target_mask = make_batch(docs, step, batch_size)

    loss_val, grads = loss_and_grad(model, input_ids, targets, pad_mask, target_mask)

    lr_t = learning_rate * (1 - step / num_steps)
    optimizer.learning_rate = mx.array(lr_t)
    optimizer.update(model, grads)
    mx.eval(model.parameters(), optimizer.state)

    if (step + 1) % 10 == 0 or step == 0:
        print(f"step {step + 1:4d} / {num_steps:4d} | loss {loss_val.item():.4f}")

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Inference
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
temperature = 0.5 # in (0, 1], control the "creativity" of generated text, low to high
print("\n--- inference (new, hallucinated names) ---")
for sample_idx in range(20):
    tokens = [BOS]
    for _ in range(block_size):
        input_ids = mx.array([tokens[-block_size:]])  # (1, T)
        logits = model(input_ids)
        logits = logits[0, -1] / temperature
        token_id = mx.random.categorical(logits).item()
        if token_id == BOS:
            break
        tokens.append(token_id)
    name = "".join(uchars[t] for t in tokens[1:])
    print(f"sample {sample_idx + 1:2d}: {name}")

14.text: We need math.sqrt for scaling attention scores. Dividing by the square root of head dimension keeps dot products from growing too large as dimensions increase.

15.text: Provides utilities for constructing file paths to locate the dataset on disk.

16.text: Python's built-in random module handles dataset shuffling. We keep this separate from MLX's random so shuffling is framework-independent.

19.text: MLX core, Apple Silicon tensor library with unified memory.

20.text: MLX's neural network module provides layers like Linear and Embedding that automatically track parameters for gradient computation.

21.text: MLX ships its own optimizer implementations. Unlike PyTorch, these work with MLX's lazy evaluation model and unified memory.

22.text: Utility functions for flattening and loading nested parameter trees. MLX stores parameters as nested dicts, and these helpers make them easier to manipulate.

24.text: Seed Python's random number generator for reproducible dataset shuffling.

25.text: Seed MLX's random number generator separately for reproducible weight initialization.

30.text: Path to the shared dataset file used consistently across all labs.

31.text: Download the names dataset from the internet if it is not already present locally.

34.text: URL pointing to the raw names dataset hosted in Karpathy's repository.

37.text: Read the text file and split it into individual name strings, one per line.

38.text: Randomize the training order so the model does not memorize sequences of names.

41.text: Gather every unique character from all names to build our token vocabulary. Sorting ensures a deterministic, reproducible mapping from characters to integer IDs.

42.text: The beginning-of-sequence token doubles as the end-of-sequence marker for name boundaries.

43.text: The real vocabulary has 27 tokens: 26 lowercase letters plus the BOS/EOS marker.

45.text: The total embedding table size includes the PAD token. The model needs an embedding for PAD even though we mask it during attention and loss.

51.text: 4x larger than lab 07, same scale-up as PyTorch batched (lab 04).

52.text: Four attention heads, each working on 16 dimensions. Multiple heads let the model attend to different relationship types simultaneously.

53.text: Two transformer layers for deeper representations, matching the batched PyTorch lab.

54.text: Each name can be at most 16 characters long, setting the attention window size.

55.text: Each attention head operates on 16 dimensions, giving finer-grained representations.

56.text: Process 32 names at once in each training step. Batching amortizes the overhead of each forward/backward pass and provides more stable gradient estimates.

57.text: Train for 1000 steps, same as all other labs but each step processes a full batch.

66.text: A tiny constant that prevents division by zero during normalization.

68.text: Run the input through RMSNorm to stabilize activations before the transformer.

69.text: Compute the mean of squared values along the last axis for RMS normalization.

70.text: Divide each element by the root-mean-square of its vector. This rescales activations to a consistent magnitude without shifting them by their mean, which is simpler than LayerNorm.

73.text: Attention module, now handles (B, T, C) shapes and padding masks, like lab 04.

76.text: Project the input into query vectors that will attend to other positions.

77.text: Project the input into key vectors that other positions will compare against.

78.text: Project the input into value vectors carrying content to aggregate by attention.

79.text: Project the concatenated multi-head output back into the embedding space.

83.text: Q reshape: (B, T, D) -> (B, T, nh, hd) -> (B, nh, T, hd). Note the 4D transpose, batch dimension is preserved, unlike lab 07's 3D version.

84.text: Reshape key projections into separate attention heads for multi-head processing.

85.text: Reshape value projections into separate attention heads for parallel computation.

87.text: Compute scaled dot-product attention scores between all query-key pairs.

89.text: Build an upper-triangular boolean mask that blocks attention to future positions.

90.text: Set future positions to a large negative value so softmax gives them zero weight.

92.text: Padding mask handling, identical logic to lab 04's PyTorch version.

93.text: Expand pad_mask to (B, 1, 1, T) and fill PAD positions with -inf.

94.text: Turn raw attention scores into a probability distribution so weights sum to one.

95.text: Replace NaN with 0 for fully-masked rows. MLX doesn't have nan_to_num, so we use where(isnan, 0, att).

97.text: Multiply attention weights by value vectors, then transpose heads back to the time dimension and flatten into the original embedding shape for the output projection.
