98.text: Project the combined multi-head output through the final linear layer.

101.text: A two-layer feed-forward network identical to lab 07. Attention mixes information between positions; the MLP then processes each position independently to add nonlinear capacity.

104.text: Widen the representation to 4x the embedding dimension for richer nonlinear transformations.

105.text: Compress the expanded hidden state back to the original embedding dimension.

107.text: Run the input through the MLP combining expansion, ReLU activation, and contraction.

108.text: MLX's equivalent of ReLU: mx.maximum(x, 0) zeroes out negative values, giving the network a simple nonlinearity between the two linear layers.

114.text: Normalize the input before passing it to the attention sub-layer for stable training.

115.text: The self-attention sub-layer that mixes information across positions in the sequence.

116.text: Normalize before the feed-forward network, following the pre-norm transformer pattern.

117.text: The feed-forward network that processes each position independently after attention mixing.

119.text: Forward passes mask through to attention.

120.text: Attention with residual and padding mask.

121.text: Apply the MLP with a residual connection that preserves the original signal.

125.text: The full transformer model, now handling batched input with padding masks. Same architecture as lab 07 but with an extra batch dimension throughout.

128.text: Embedding table with PAD token. Like lab 04, the embedding table has vocab_size_with_pad rows.

129.text: A learned vector for each position in the sequence. Added to token embeddings so the model knows where each token sits, since attention alone is position-agnostic.

130.text: Apply RMSNorm to stabilize embeddings before they enter the transformer layers.

131.text: Register all transformer layers so MLX can discover and manage their parameters.

132.text: Output head maps to real vocab (no PAD).

134.text: Forward pass with optional padding mask.

135.text: Unpack batch size and sequence length from the input shape. These dimensions drive how we construct position embeddings and propagate through the layers.

136.text: Look up a learned embedding vector for each input token in the batch.

137.text: Position embeddings broadcast across the batch.

138.text: Combine token and position embeddings and normalize before entering the transformer.

139.text: Feed the normalized embeddings through each transformer block in sequence. Each layer refines the representations through attention and MLP sublayers.

140.text: The padding mask tells each layer's attention which positions are filler so those positions get zero weight and do not corrupt the real token representations.

141.text: Project the final hidden states to vocabulary-sized logits for next-token prediction.

147.text: Batch creation, same strategy as labs 04 and 06.

149.text: Grab the next batch of names from the corpus, wrapping around if we reach the end. This cycling ensures we keep training even if the dataset is smaller than the total steps.

151.text: Convert each name into a sequence of integer token IDs. Each character maps to its position in the sorted vocabulary, with BOS tokens bookending the name.

152.text: Wrap each name with beginning-of-sequence tokens to mark the start and end boundaries.

153.text: Clip any name longer than the model's maximum context window. Names beyond this length would exceed the positional embedding table and cause errors.

156.text: Pad to longest sequence in batch.

157.text: Allocate four parallel arrays (input, target, pad mask, target mask) all padded to the same length so they can be stacked into rectangular tensors.

159.text: The number of real tokens in this name, before any padding was added. This tells us where the actual content ends and the padding begins.

162.text: Boolean mask marking which positions are padding so attention can ignore them.

170.text: Convert the target token IDs into an MLX array for loss computation.

171.text: Boolean mask marking which positions are padding so attention can skip them.

172.text: Float mask that weights the loss so padding positions contribute zero to the gradient.

180.text: Create the model with all its layers and projections. MLX initializes parameters immediately in unified memory shared between CPU and GPU.

183.text: N(0, 0.08) initialization, match all other labs.

184.text: Sum up all parameter sizes to report the total number of trainable weights.

187.text: Adam optimizer settings: a learning rate of 1e-2 with conservative momentum (beta1=0.85, beta2=0.99). These match the other labs for a fair comparison.

190.text: Loss function. Takes the model as first arg for MLX's autograd.

191.text: Batched forward pass with padding mask.

192.text: Subtract the log-sum-exp to get log-probabilities. This is numerically more stable than taking softmax and then log, which can lose precision on small probabilities.

193.text: Unpack the batch, sequence length, and vocabulary dimensions from the log-prob tensor.

195.text: Gather target log-probs via fancy indexing. Same pattern as lab 06.

196.text: Multiply by the target mask so padding positions contribute zero to the loss. Without this, the model would waste capacity trying to predict padding tokens.

197.text: Normalize by real token count, same as lab 06.

201.text: Value-and-grad: compute loss and gradients simultaneously.

202.text: MLX's built-in Adam optimizer manages momentum and adaptive learning rates for every parameter. It works with MLX's lazy evaluation, updating only when evaluated.

204.text: Iterate over training steps, processing a batch of 32 names per step.

205.text: Assemble a padded batch of 32 names for this training step. Each sequence is tokenized, truncated, and padded so all sequences share the same length.

207.text: Run the forward pass and backpropagation together. MLX's value_and_grad returns both the scalar loss and the gradient tree in one efficient pass.

209.text: Linearly reduce the learning rate toward zero over the course of training.

210.text: Write the decayed learning rate into the optimizer so the next weight update uses a slightly smaller step, gradually stabilizing training as we near convergence.

211.text: Push the computed gradients into the optimizer to update all model weights.

212.text: Force lazy evaluation. Without this, MLX builds up a huge unevaluated computation graph that eventually exhausts memory.

214.text: Print the loss periodically so we can monitor whether the model is learning.

220.text: Temperature controls how peaked or flat the sampling distribution is. At 0.5, the model becomes more confident, favoring high-probability characters over rare ones.

222.text: Generate 20 brand-new names by sampling from the model's learned distribution.

223.text: Begin each generated name with the beginning-of-sequence token as the initial prompt.

224.text: Autoregressive generation loop: predict one character at a time, append it to the sequence, and feed the extended sequence back into the model for the next prediction.

225.text: Feed as (1, T) batch, the batched model always expects a batch dimension.

226.text: Run the full batched model on the current token sequence to predict the next character.

227.text: Grab the logits for the very last position (the model's prediction for the next token) and divide by temperature to sharpen or soften the probability distribution.

228.text: Randomly pick one token from the probability distribution. This stochastic sampling produces diverse outputs instead of always choosing the most likely character.

229.text: When the model predicts BOS, it signals the name is complete, so we stop generating.

231.text: Append the newly sampled token to the growing sequence for the next forward pass.

232.text: Map each integer token ID back to its corresponding character and join them into a readable name string, skipping the initial BOS token.

233.text: Display the generated name so we can visually assess the model's output quality and check whether it learned plausible name patterns.

title: BPE Tokenizer
tagline: Byte-Pair Encoding from scratch in pure Python, with zero dependencies.
code:
"""
microGPT and Beyond — BPE Tokenizer

Byte-Pair Encoding from scratch, then a side-by-side comparison of
character-level vs BPE tokenization on the names dataset.

Zero dependencies. Pure Python. The algorithm is just counting and merging.
"""

import math
import os
import random

random.seed(42)

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Dataset
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
input_path = os.path.join(os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__)), "..", "data", "input.txt")
if not os.path.exists(input_path):
    import urllib.request

    url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/karpathy/makemore/refs/heads/master/names.txt"
    urllib.request.urlretrieve(url, input_path)

docs = [l.strip() for l in open(input_path).read().strip().split("\n") if l.strip()]
random.shuffle(docs)
print(f"num docs: {len(docs)}")

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Character-level tokenizer (baseline — same as lab 01)
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Every unique character gets an integer id. BOS marks start/end of sequence.
uchars = sorted(set("".join(docs)))
char_to_id = {ch: i for i, ch in enumerate(uchars)}
id_to_char = {i: ch for ch, i in char_to_id.items()}
CHAR_BOS = len(uchars)
char_vocab_size = len(uchars) + 1  # +1 for BOS

print(f"\n{'=' * 60}")
print("CHARACTER-LEVEL TOKENIZER (baseline)")
print(f"{'=' * 60}")
print(f"vocab size: {char_vocab_size} ({len(uchars)} chars + BOS)")


def char_encode(name):
    """Encode a name as a list of character token ids."""
    return [CHAR_BOS] + [char_to_id[ch] for ch in name] + [CHAR_BOS]


def char_decode(ids):
    """Decode token ids back to a string (strip BOS)."""
    return "".join(id_to_char[i] for i in ids if i != CHAR_BOS)


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# BPE tokenizer — trained from scratch
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# BPE starts with the character vocabulary and iteratively merges the most
# frequent adjacent pair into a new token. This is EXACTLY how GPT-2/3/4,
# LLaMA, etc. build their tokenizers (at much larger scale with ~50K merges).
#
# The tradeoff: larger vocab = shorter sequences = faster training/inference,
# but more parameters in the embedding table. At our tiny scale (names),
# character-level works great. At GPT scale (internet text), BPE with
# 50K-100K tokens is essential — without it, sequences would be absurdly long.
print(f"\n{'=' * 60}")
print("BPE TOKENIZER (training from scratch)")
print(f"{'=' * 60}")

NUM_MERGES = 50  # number of merge operations (new tokens to learn)

# Step 1: Start with character-level tokens for each name.
# We work with lists of integers. Initially each integer = one character.
# We reserve ids 0..25 for a-z and 26 for BOS, same as the char tokenizer.
BPE_BOS = len(uchars)
base_vocab_size = len(uchars) + 1  # characters + BOS

# Build the initial corpus: each name becomes a list of char token ids.
# We wrap with BOS just like the char tokenizer.
corpus = []
for name in docs:
    tokens = [BPE_BOS] + [char_to_id[ch] for ch in name] + [BPE_BOS]
    corpus.append(tokens)


def count_pairs(corpus):
    """Count frequency of each adjacent token pair across all sequences."""
    counts = {}
    for seq in corpus:
        for i in range(len(seq) - 1):
            pair = (seq[i], seq[i + 1])
            counts[pair] = counts.get(pair, 0) + 1
    return counts


def merge_pair(corpus, pair, new_id):
    """Replace every occurrence of `pair` with `new_id` in all sequences."""
    new_corpus = []
    for seq in corpus:
        new_seq = []
        i = 0
        while i < len(seq):
            if i < len(seq) - 1 and seq[i] == pair[0] and seq[i + 1] == pair[1]:
                new_seq.append(new_id)
                i += 2
            else:
                new_seq.append(seq[i])
                i += 1
        new_corpus.append(new_seq)
    return new_corpus


# Step 2: Iteratively merge the most frequent pair.
# Each merge creates a new token and shrinks all sequences by 1 wherever
# that pair appeared. The merge table records what was merged.
merges = {}  # (pair) -> new_token_id
vocab_size = base_vocab_size


def bpe_decode_token(tid):
    """Decode a single BPE token id to its string representation."""
    if tid == BPE_BOS:
        return ""
    if tid < len(uchars):
        return id_to_char[tid]
    # This is a merged token — find its components
    for (a, b), merged_id in merges.items():
        if merged_id == tid:
            return bpe_decode_token(a) + bpe_decode_token(b)
    return "?"


def token_str(tid):
    """Human-readable token string for display."""
    if tid == BPE_BOS:
        return "<BOS>"
    if tid < len(uchars):
        return id_to_char[tid]
    return bpe_decode_token(tid)


print(f"\nStarting vocab: {vocab_size} tokens ({len(uchars)} chars + BOS)")
print(f"Training {NUM_MERGES} merges...\n")
print(f"{'Merge':>5}  {'Pair':>20}  {'Visual':>12}  {'Freq':>6}  {'Vocab':>5}")
print("-" * 60)

for i in range(NUM_MERGES):
    counts = count_pairs(corpus)
    if not counts:
        print(f"No more pairs to merge after {i} merges.")
        break
    # Find the most frequent pair
    best_pair = max(counts, key=counts.get)
    best_count = counts[best_pair]
    new_id = vocab_size
    merges[best_pair] = new_id

    if i < 20 or i % 10 == 0 or i == NUM_MERGES - 1:
        visual = f"'{token_str(best_pair[0])}'+'{token_str(best_pair[1])}'"
        print(f"{i + 1:>5}  {best_pair!s:>20}  {visual:>12}  {best_count:>6}  {new_id:>5}")

    corpus = merge_pair(corpus, best_pair, new_id)
    vocab_size += 1

print(f"\nFinal BPE vocab size: {vocab_size} ({base_vocab_size} base + {NUM_MERGES} merges)")


def bpe_decode(ids):
    """Decode a list of BPE token ids back to a string."""
    return "".join(bpe_decode_token(tid) for tid in ids)


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# BPE encode: string -> token ids
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
def bpe_encode(name):
    """Encode a name using the learned BPE merges."""
    # Start with character-level tokens
    tokens = [BPE_BOS] + [char_to_id[ch] for ch in name] + [BPE_BOS]
    # Apply merges in the order they were learned (greedy, left-to-right)
    for pair, new_id in merges.items():
        tokens = merge_pair([tokens], pair, new_id)[0]
    return tokens


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Show encoding examples: character-level vs BPE
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
print(f"\n{'=' * 60}")
print("ENCODING EXAMPLES: Character vs BPE")
print(f"{'=' * 60}")
example_names = ["emma", "olivia", "charlotte", "alexander", "ann", "lee"]
# Also pick some random names from the dataset
example_names += random.sample(docs, 4)
# Deduplicate while preserving order
example_names = list(dict.fromkeys(example_names))[:10]

print(f"\n{'Name':<14} {'Char tokens':>11} {'BPE tokens':>10}  {'Compression':>11}  BPE encoding")
print("-" * 75)
for name in example_names:
    char_toks = char_encode(name)
    bpe_toks = bpe_encode(name)
    ratio = len(char_toks) / len(bpe_toks) if bpe_toks else 0
    bpe_visual = [f"'{bpe_decode_token(t)}'" if t != BPE_BOS else "<BOS>" for t in bpe_toks]
    print(f"{name:<14} {len(char_toks):>11} {len(bpe_toks):>10}  {ratio:>10.2f}x  {' '.join(bpe_visual)}")


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Corpus-wide compression statistics
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
print(f"\n{'=' * 60}")
print("COMPRESSION STATISTICS (full corpus)")
print(f"{'=' * 60}")

total_char_tokens = sum(len(char_encode(name)) for name in docs)
total_bpe_tokens = sum(len(bpe_encode(name)) for name in docs)
avg_char = total_char_tokens / len(docs)
avg_bpe = total_bpe_tokens / len(docs)

print(f"Total names:          {len(docs):>10}")
print(f"Char vocab size:      {char_vocab_size:>10}")
print(f"BPE vocab size:       {vocab_size:>10}")
print(f"Total char tokens:    {total_char_tokens:>10}")
print(f"Total BPE tokens:     {total_bpe_tokens:>10}")
print(f"Avg tokens/name char: {avg_char:>10.2f}")
print(f"Avg tokens/name BPE:  {avg_bpe:>10.2f}")
print(f"Overall compression:  {total_char_tokens / total_bpe_tokens:>10.2f}x")

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# BPE vocabulary: show what each merged token represents
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
print(f"\n{'=' * 60}")
print("LEARNED BPE VOCABULARY (merged tokens)")
print(f"{'=' * 60}")
print(f"\nBase tokens (0-{len(uchars) - 1}): {' '.join(uchars)}")
print(f"BOS token: {BPE_BOS}")
print("\nMerged tokens:")
print(f"{'ID':>5}  {'Represents':>12}  {'Components'}")
print("-" * 40)
for (a, b), new_id in merges.items():
    merged_str = bpe_decode_token(new_id)
    comp_a = bpe_decode_token(a) if a != BPE_BOS else "<BOS>"
    comp_b = bpe_decode_token(b) if b != BPE_BOS else "<BOS>"
    print(f"{new_id:>5}  {merged_str!r:>12}  '{comp_a}' + '{comp_b}'")

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Bigram language model — trained with BOTH tokenizations
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# A bigram model is the simplest language model: P(next_token | current_token).
# It's just a lookup table of transition counts, normalized to probabilities.
# No neural network, no autograd — pure counting.
#
# We train one bigram model on character tokens and one on BPE tokens to show
# how tokenization affects sequence modeling. With BPE, each "step" covers
# more text, so the bigram captures longer-range patterns.
print(f"\n{'=' * 60}")
print("BIGRAM LANGUAGE MODEL COMPARISON")
print(f"{'=' * 60}")


def train_bigram(encoded_corpus, v_size):
    """Train a bigram model: count transitions, add-1 smooth, normalize."""
    # counts[a][b] = number of times token b follows token a
    counts = [[0] * v_size for _ in range(v_size)]
    for seq in encoded_corpus:
        for i in range(len(seq) - 1):
            counts[seq[i]][seq[i + 1]] += 1
    # Normalize to probabilities with add-1 (Laplace) smoothing
    probs = []
    for row in counts:
        total = sum(row) + v_size  # add-1 smoothing
        probs.append([(c + 1) / total for c in row])
    return probs


def sample_bigram(probs, bos_token, max_len=20):
    """Sample a sequence from the bigram model."""
    tokens = [bos_token]
    for _ in range(max_len):
        # Sample next token from probability distribution
        p = probs[tokens[-1]]
        r = random.random()
        cumsum = 0
        for tid, prob in enumerate(p):
            cumsum += prob
            if r < cumsum:
                tokens.append(tid)
                break
        if tokens[-1] == bos_token and len(tokens) > 1:
            break
    return tokens


def bigram_log_likelihood(probs, encoded_corpus):
    """Compute average log-likelihood per token (higher = better)."""
    total_ll = 0.0
    total_tokens = 0
    for seq in encoded_corpus:
        for i in range(len(seq) - 1):
            p = probs[seq[i]][seq[i + 1]]
            if p > 0:
                total_ll += math.log(p)
            total_tokens += 1
    return total_ll / total_tokens if total_tokens > 0 else 0.0


# Train character-level bigram
print("\nTraining character-level bigram...")
char_corpus = [char_encode(name) for name in docs]
char_bigram = train_bigram(char_corpus, char_vocab_size)
char_ll = bigram_log_likelihood(char_bigram, char_corpus)
print(f"  Vocab size: {char_vocab_size}")
print(f"  Avg log-likelihood per token: {char_ll:.4f}")
print(f"  Perplexity per token: {math.exp(-char_ll):.2f}")

# Train BPE bigram
print("\nTraining BPE bigram...")
bpe_corpus = [bpe_encode(name) for name in docs]
bpe_bigram = train_bigram(bpe_corpus, vocab_size)
bpe_ll = bigram_log_likelihood(bpe_bigram, bpe_corpus)
print(f"  Vocab size: {vocab_size}")
print(f"  Avg log-likelihood per token: {bpe_ll:.4f}")
print(f"  Perplexity per token: {math.exp(-bpe_ll):.2f}")

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Generate names from both models
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
print(f"\n{'=' * 60}")
print("GENERATED NAMES")
print(f"{'=' * 60}")

print("\nCharacter-level bigram (27 tokens, ~6 tokens/name):")
for i in range(10):
    toks = sample_bigram(char_bigram, CHAR_BOS)
    name = char_decode(toks)
    print(f"  sample {i + 1:>2}: {name}")

print(f"\nBPE bigram ({vocab_size} tokens, ~{avg_bpe:.1f} tokens/name):")
for i in range(10):
    toks = sample_bigram(bpe_bigram, BPE_BOS)
    name = bpe_decode(toks)
    print(f"  sample {i + 1:>2}: {name}")

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Key takeaway
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
print(f"\n{'=' * 60}")
print("KEY TAKEAWAY")
print(f"{'=' * 60}")
print(f"""
BPE compresses the corpus from {total_char_tokens} to {total_bpe_tokens} tokens ({total_char_tokens / total_bpe_tokens:.1f}x shorter)
by learning {NUM_MERGES} merge rules that capture common character sequences.
Vocab grows from {char_vocab_size} to {vocab_size} tokens (more embedding params, but shorter sequences).

This is the algorithm behind GPT-2, LLaMA, and most modern LLM tokenizers.
At GPT scale, BPE with 50K-100K merges is essential to keep sequences tractable.
""")

10.text: Standard library math, used later for log in the bigram log-likelihood calculation.

11.text: Filesystem ops, building a portable path to the shared dataset.

14.text: Pin the random seed so every run sees the same training order.

19.text: Portable path that works regardless of where you run the script from.

20.text: Auto-download the dataset on first run.

21.text: Lazy import, only needed if the file is missing.

23.text: Karpathy's names dataset: ~32K human names, one per line.

24.text: Fetch the file from the internet once and save it locally. After this first download, every future run loads instantly from disk.

26.text: Load every name, strip whitespace, skip blanks. Each name becomes one "document" for training.

27.text: Shuffle so the model doesn't memorize alphabetical order.

28.text: A quick sanity check that we loaded the full dataset. With ~32K names, we have plenty of data to discover meaningful merge patterns.

34.text: Collect every unique character (a-z), this becomes the base vocabulary, same as lab 01.

35.text: Character-to-integer mapping. In labs 01-08, this WAS the tokenizer. Now it's the baseline we'll beat.

36.text: Reverse mapping for decoding back to text.

37.text: BOS (beginning/end of sequence) gets the next available id, just past the last character.

38.text: 27 tokens total: 26 letters + BOS. Tiny vocab, but each token is just one character.

46.text: Wrap each name with BOS markers on both ends, the model learns to start and stop generating.

48.text: BOS + character ids + BOS. Identical to how labs 01-08 encode, just factored into a function.

51.text: Reverse the encoding, filtering out BOS markers to recover the original name.

53.text: Skip BOS tokens, map ids back to characters, join into a string.

71.text: 50 merges is tiny, GPT-2 uses 50,000. But even 50 will noticeably compress our names corpus.

76.text: Reuse the same BOS id so char and BPE tokenizers are comparable.

77.text: 27 base tokens before any merges happen.

81.text: The corpus starts as character-level token sequences, identical to what the char tokenizer produces.

83.text: Wrap with BOS, convert characters to integer ids. This is the starting point BPE will compress.

84.text: Accumulate each tokenized name into the corpus list. This gives us the complete training set that BPE will iteratively compress through merges.

87.text: The heart of BPE: count how often each adjacent pair of tokens appears across the entire corpus.

89.text: Simple dictionary to accumulate pair frequencies.

90.text: Scan every tokenized name in the corpus.

91.text: Slide a window of size 2 across each sequence.

92.text: Each pair is a tuple of two adjacent token ids.

93.text: Increment the count. The pair with the highest count will be merged next.

97.text: Once we find the winning pair, replace every occurrence with a brand new token id.

99.text: Allocate a fresh corpus list that will hold the post-merge versions of every sequence. We rebuild rather than mutate in place to keep the logic clean.

101.text: Walk through the old sequence building a new one token by token, replacing any occurrence of the target pair with the merged token along the way.

102.text: Manual index, we sometimes skip ahead by 2 when we find the pair.

103.text: Walk through the sequence looking for the target pair.

104.text: Found the pair! Both tokens match at adjacent positions.

105.text: Replace the pair with the single merged token, this is where compression happens.

106.text: Skip past both original tokens since we consumed them as one.

108.text: No match here, keep the original token.

109.text: Move the scan pointer forward by one position. We only skip by two when we find and merge the target pair; otherwise we advance normally.

110.text: This name is done, add its merged sequence to the new corpus.

117.text: The merge table: maps each learned pair to its new token id. This IS the tokenizer.

118.text: Tracks the growing vocabulary size as merges create new tokens.

121.text: Recursively decode a BPE token back to its string. Merged tokens unpack into their components.

123.text: BOS decodes to empty string, it's a control token, not a character.

125.text: Base characters decode directly via the character mapping.

128.text: For merged tokens, find which pair was merged and recursively decode both halves.

129.text: Linear scan through merges, fine for 50 merges, would need a reverse map at scale.

130.text: Recursive call: a merged token might itself be composed of merged tokens.

134.text: Human-friendly token display for the training log.

136.text: Show BOS as a readable tag.

140.text: Merged tokens show their full decoded string (e.g., token 28 might display as "an").

148.text: The main BPE training loop: 50 rounds of find-best-pair, merge, repeat.

149.text: Count all adjacent pairs in the current corpus.

150.text: Safety check, if every sequence is length 1, nothing left to merge.

154.text: Greedily pick the most frequent pair. This greedy choice is what makes BPE BPE.

155.text: Track the frequency for the training log.

156.text: New token id = current vocab size (incrementing from 27).

157.text: Record this merge in the table. During encoding, merges are replayed in this exact order.

159.text: Log the first 20 merges and every 10th after, watch common English patterns emerge ("an", "er", "in").

160.text: Show what character sequences are being fused together.

163.text: Apply the merge to the entire corpus, every occurrence of the pair becomes one token.

164.text: Vocabulary grows by one with each merge.

169.text: Decode a full sequence of BPE token ids back to a readable string.

171.text: Concatenate the decoded string for each token in the sequence.

177.text: Encoding with BPE: start from characters, then replay all merges in order. This is how GPT-2 encodes text.

180.text: Start exactly like character encoding, BOS + char ids + BOS.

182.text: Replay merges in training order. Earlier merges are higher priority (more frequent patterns).

183.text: Apply each merge to the token sequence. After all merges, common subwords are single tokens.

193.text: A mix of short, long, common, and unusual names to show compression varies.

195.text: Add some random names so the examples aren't cherry-picked.

197.text: Remove duplicates while preserving order, cap at 10.

201.text: For each example, encode with both tokenizers and show the compression ratio.

202.text: Character encoding: always len(name) + 2 tokens (one per char plus two BOS markers).

203.text: BPE encoding: fewer tokens because common patterns like "an", "er" are now single tokens.

204.text: Compression ratio, how many character tokens each BPE token replaces on average.

205.text: Show the BPE tokenization visually, so you can see subword boundaries.

216.text: Sum up character tokens across the entire corpus.

217.text: Sum up BPE tokens across the entire corpus, this is the real compression measurement.

218.text: Average tokens per name with character encoding.

219.text: Average tokens per name with BPE, expect roughly 1.3-1.5x shorter.

228.text: The headline number: BPE compresses the corpus by ~1.3-1.5x with just 50 merges.

241.text: Display the full merge table, this IS the BPE vocabulary, showing exactly what patterns the algorithm discovered.

242.text: Decode each merged token to see its string representation.

243.text: Show the components that were merged (which might themselves be merged tokens).

262.text: A bigram model is the simplest possible language model: just a transition probability table. No neural network needed.

265.text: 2D count matrix: counts[a][b] = how often token b follows token a.

268.text: Tally one more occurrence of this token-to-token transition. The row with the highest total count will later define the most probable next tokens.

272.text: Add-1 (Laplace) smoothing prevents zero probabilities for unseen pairs.

273.text: Normalize to a proper probability distribution.

277.text: Generate a name by sampling one token at a time from the bigram distribution.

279.text: Start with BOS, the model will sample what typically follows the start marker.

282.text: Look up the probability distribution for the current (last) token.

283.text: Sample from the cumulative distribution using a uniform random number.

285.text: Walk through tokens by probability until we pass the random threshold.

287.text: This token was selected, the cumulative probability exceeded our random draw.

290.text: Stop generating when we hit BOS again (end of name).

295.text: Average log-likelihood per token measures how well the model predicts the training data.

297.text: Accumulate total log-likelihood across all tokens.

300.text: For each adjacent pair in each sequence, look up its probability.

301.text: The probability assigned to this actual transition, higher is better.

303.text: Log probability, the fundamental unit of information theory. Sums are easier than products.

305.text: Average over all tokens gives a per-token measure, comparable across different vocab sizes.

310.text: Encode the entire corpus with the character tokenizer for bigram training.

311.text: Train the character-level bigram, just counting transitions in a 27x27 table.

312.text: Measure how well it models the data. Lower perplexity = better model.

319.text: Encode the entire corpus with BPE, shorter sequences but larger vocabulary.

320.text: Train the BPE bigram, a 77x77 transition table. Each step now covers more text.

321.text: The BPE bigram should have better perplexity per token because each token carries more information.

334.text: Generate from the character bigram, expect babble-ish names since bigrams have no long-range memory.

335.text: Sample a complete name by repeatedly drawing from the transition table.

336.text: Decode character ids back to a readable string.

339.text: Generate from the BPE bigram, each step jumps through common letter patterns, so names sound a bit more natural.

341.text: Sample from the BPE bigram's 77x77 transition table.

342.text: Decode BPE tokens back to a string, merged tokens expand to multiple characters.

title: Rotary Position Embeddings (RoPE)
tagline: Position encoded by rotating Q and K vectors in complex space. Relative position without learned parameters.
code:
"""
microGPT — Rotary Position Embeddings (RoPE).

Same architecture as version 03 (PyTorch), but learned positional embeddings are
replaced with Rotary Position Embeddings. RoPE encodes position by rotating query
and key vectors in complex space, so the dot product q·k naturally depends on the
*relative* distance (m-n) rather than absolute positions m and n separately.

Every modern LLM (LLaMA, Mistral, GPT-NeoX) uses RoPE. This lab shows why.
"""

import math
import os
import random

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F

random.seed(42)
torch.manual_seed(42)

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Dataset & Tokenizer
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
input_path = os.path.join(os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__)), "..", "data", "input.txt")
if not os.path.exists(input_path):
    import urllib.request

    url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/karpathy/makemore/refs/heads/master/names.txt"
    urllib.request.urlretrieve(url, input_path)

docs = [l.strip() for l in open(input_path).read().strip().split("\n") if l.strip()]
random.shuffle(docs)
print(f"num docs: {len(docs)}")
uchars = sorted(set("".join(docs)))
BOS = len(uchars)
vocab_size = len(uchars) + 1
print(f"vocab size: {vocab_size}")

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Model config
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
n_embd = 16     # embedding dimension
n_head = 4      # number of attention heads
n_layer = 1     # number of layers
block_size = 16 # maximum sequence length
head_dim = n_embd // n_head # dimension of each head


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# RoPE helpers
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
def precompute_freqs(dim, max_len):
    """Precompute rotation frequencies for RoPE.

    theta_i = 1 / (10000 ^ (2i / dim))  for i in 0..dim//2
    Returns cos and sin of shape (max_len, dim//2).
    """
    i = torch.arange(0, dim, 2, dtype=torch.float32)  # (dim//2,)
    theta = 1.0 / (10000.0 ** (i / dim))  # (dim//2,)
    positions = torch.arange(max_len, dtype=torch.float32)  # (max_len,)
    angles = torch.outer(positions, theta)  # (max_len, dim//2)
    return torch.cos(angles), torch.sin(angles)


def apply_rope(x, cos_freqs, sin_freqs):
    """Apply rotary embeddings to x of shape (B, n_head, T, head_dim).

    Split head_dim into pairs, rotate each pair:
        [x1, x2] -> [x1*cos - x2*sin, x1*sin + x2*cos]
    """
    T = x.shape[2]
    cos_t = cos_freqs[:T].unsqueeze(0).unsqueeze(0)  # (1, 1, T, dim//2)
    sin_t = sin_freqs[:T].unsqueeze(0).unsqueeze(0)  # (1, 1, T, dim//2)
    x1 = x[..., 0::2]  # even indices
    x2 = x[..., 1::2]  # odd indices
    out1 = x1 * cos_t - x2 * sin_t
    out2 = x1 * sin_t + x2 * cos_t
    return torch.stack((out1, out2), dim=-1).flatten(-2)


# Precompute once for the whole model
rope_cos, rope_sin = precompute_freqs(head_dim, block_size)


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Model components
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
class RMSNorm(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, _dim, eps=1e-5):
        super().__init__()
        self.eps = eps

    def forward(self, x):
        return x * torch.rsqrt(x.pow(2).mean(-1, keepdim=True) + self.eps)


class CausalSelfAttention(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, use_rope=False):
        super().__init__()
        self.use_rope = use_rope
        self.wq = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)
        self.wk = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)
        self.wv = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)
        self.wo = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)

    def forward(self, x):
        B, T, C = x.shape
        q = self.wq(x).view(B, T, n_head, head_dim).transpose(1, 2)
        k = self.wk(x).view(B, T, n_head, head_dim).transpose(1, 2)
        v = self.wv(x).view(B, T, n_head, head_dim).transpose(1, 2)

        if self.use_rope:
            q = apply_rope(q, rope_cos, rope_sin)
            k = apply_rope(k, rope_cos, rope_sin)
            # V is NOT rotated — RoPE only affects Q and K

        att = (q @ k.transpose(-2, -1)) / math.sqrt(head_dim)
        mask = torch.triu(torch.ones(T, T, device=x.device), diagonal=1).bool()
        att = att.masked_fill(mask, float("-inf"))
        att = F.softmax(att, dim=-1)

        out = (att @ v).transpose(1, 2).reshape(B, T, C)
        return self.wo(out)


class MLP(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.fc1 = nn.Linear(n_embd, 4 * n_embd, bias=False)
        self.fc2 = nn.Linear(4 * n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)

    def forward(self, x):
        return self.fc2(F.relu(self.fc1(x)))


class Block(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, use_rope=False):
        super().__init__()
        self.norm1 = RMSNorm(n_embd)
        self.attn = CausalSelfAttention(use_rope=use_rope)
        self.norm2 = RMSNorm(n_embd)
        self.mlp = MLP()

    def forward(self, x):
        x = x + self.attn(self.norm1(x))
        x = x + self.mlp(self.norm2(x))
        return x


class MicroGPT(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, use_rope=False):
        super().__init__()
        self.use_rope = use_rope
        self.wte = nn.Embedding(vocab_size, n_embd)
        if not use_rope:
            self.wpe = nn.Embedding(block_size, n_embd)
        self.norm_in = RMSNorm(n_embd)
        self.layers = nn.ModuleList([Block(use_rope=use_rope) for _ in range(n_layer)])
        self.lm_head = nn.Linear(n_embd, vocab_size, bias=False)
        self.apply(self._init_weights)

    @staticmethod
    def _init_weights(module):
        if isinstance(module, (nn.Linear, nn.Embedding)):
            nn.init.normal_(module.weight, mean=0.0, std=0.08)

    def forward(self, idx):
        B, T = idx.shape
        x = self.wte(idx)
        if not self.use_rope:
            x = x + self.wpe(torch.arange(T, device=idx.device))
        x = self.norm_in(x)
        for layer in self.layers:
            x = layer(x)
        return self.lm_head(x)


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Training + comparison
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
def train(model, label, num_steps=1000):
    """Train a model and return the loss history."""
    optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=1e-2, betas=(0.85, 0.99), eps=1e-8)
    losses = []
    for step in range(num_steps):
        doc = docs[step % len(docs)]
        tokens = [BOS] + [uchars.index(ch) for ch in doc] + [BOS]
        n = min(block_size, len(tokens) - 1)

        input_ids = torch.tensor([tokens[:n]])
        targets = torch.tensor([tokens[1 : n + 1]])

        logits = model(input_ids)
        loss = F.cross_entropy(logits.view(-1, vocab_size), targets.view(-1))

        optimizer.zero_grad()
        loss.backward()
        lr_t = 1e-2 * (1 - step / num_steps)
        for pg in optimizer.param_groups:
            pg["lr"] = lr_t
        optimizer.step()
        losses.append(loss.item())

        if (step + 1) % 200 == 0:
            print(f"  [{label}] step {step + 1:4d}/{num_steps} | loss {loss.item():.4f}")

    return losses


def generate(model, label, num_samples=10, temperature=0.5):
    """Generate names from a trained model."""
    model.eval()
    print(f"\n--- {label}: generated names ---")
    with torch.no_grad():
        for i in range(num_samples):
            tokens = [BOS]
            for _ in range(block_size):
                idx = torch.tensor([tokens[-block_size:]])
                logits = model(idx)
                logits = logits[0, -1] / temperature
                probs = F.softmax(logits, dim=-1)
                token_id = torch.multinomial(probs, 1).item()
                if token_id == BOS:
                    break
                tokens.append(token_id)
            name = "".join(uchars[t] for t in tokens[1:])
            print(f"  sample {i + 1:2d}: {name}")
    model.train()


# Train both variants from the same random initialization
print("\n=== Training: Learned Positional Embeddings (baseline) ===")
torch.manual_seed(42)
model_learned = MicroGPT(use_rope=False)
n_params_learned = sum(p.numel() for p in model_learned.parameters())
print(f"  params: {n_params_learned}")
losses_learned = train(model_learned, "learned-pos")

print("\n=== Training: Rotary Position Embeddings (RoPE) ===")
torch.manual_seed(42)
model_rope = MicroGPT(use_rope=True)
n_params_rope = sum(p.numel() for p in model_rope.parameters())
print(f"  params: {n_params_rope} (no wpe — {n_params_learned - n_params_rope} fewer)")
losses_rope = train(model_rope, "rope")

# Compare final losses
print("\n=== Results ===")
avg_window = 50
avg_learned = sum(losses_learned[-avg_window:]) / avg_window
avg_rope = sum(losses_rope[-avg_window:]) / avg_window
print(f"  learned-pos  final avg loss (last {avg_window}): {avg_learned:.4f}")
print(f"  rope         final avg loss (last {avg_window}): {avg_rope:.4f}")

generate(model_learned, "Learned Positional Embeddings")
generate(model_rope, "RoPE")

12.text: Standard library imports for math, paths, and randomness.

13.text: Path operations for portable dataset loading.

14.text: Shuffle seed for reproducible training order.

20.text: Pin both Python and PyTorch random seeds for reproducibility.

21.text: Separate seed for PyTorch's tensor operations.

26.text: Portable path to the shared dataset.

27.text: If the dataset file is missing, we will download it automatically. This makes the script self-contained so you can run it without manual setup.

28.text: Lazy import, only when the file needs downloading.

30.text: The raw URL for Karpathy's names dataset. About 32K human names that serve as our training corpus across all labs.

31.text: Fetch the file from the internet once and save it locally. After the first download, every future run loads instantly from disk.

33.text: Read all names from disk, strip whitespace, and drop blank lines. Each name becomes one training document for next-token prediction.

34.text: Shuffle so the model doesn't learn alphabetical order.

36.text: Unique sorted characters become the base vocabulary.

37.text: BOS token gets the next available id.

44.text: Embedding dimension, small enough to train quickly, large enough to see RoPE's effect.

45.text: 4 attention heads, each operating on its own subspace.

46.text: Single layer keeps focus on the positional encoding change.

47.text: Maximum sequence length, names are short.

54.text: Precompute the rotation frequencies once, they never change during training or inference.

60.text: Pair up dimensions: indices 0,2,4,... Each pair will be rotated together as a 2D vector.

61.text: The frequency for each pair: theta_i = 1/10000^(2i/dim). Lower pairs rotate fast (local patterns), higher pairs rotate slowly (global patterns). 10000 is the standard base from the RoPE paper.

62.text: Position indices: 0, 1, 2, ... Each position gets a different rotation angle.

63.text: Outer product gives a 2D table: angles[pos][pair] = how much to rotate dimension-pair pair at position pos.

64.text: Return cos and sin, the two components of the rotation matrix. Precomputed once, reused every forward pass.

67.text: Apply the rotation to query or key tensors. This is where position information enters the attention computation.

73.text: Grab the sequence length to slice the precomputed frequencies.

74.text: Broadcast cos frequencies to match the (batch, heads, time, pairs) shape.

76.text: Split each head's dimensions into even-indexed (x1) and odd-indexed (x2), these form 2D rotation pairs.

77.text: The paired dimension, together with x1, this forms a 2D vector that gets rotated.

78.text: 2D rotation formula, real part: x1*cos - x2*sin. Same as rotating a point in 2D space.

79.text: 2D rotation formula, imaginary part: x1*sin + x2*cos. The angle encodes the absolute position.

80.text: Interleave rotated pairs back into the original dimension ordering. The key insight: when Q at position m and K at position n are dot-producted, the rotation angles subtract, making attention depend on m-n (relative distance) automatically.

84.text: Compute once at startup. These frequency tables are shared by all layers and all forward passes.

90.text: RMSNorm instead of LayerNorm, simpler (no mean subtraction), works just as well. Used by LLaMA and most modern LLMs.

91.text: Dimension parameter stored for documentation; RMSNorm doesn't actually need it.

93.text: A tiny epsilon (1e-5) added inside the square root to prevent division by zero when activations happen to be near-zero.

96.text: Normalize by root-mean-square of activations. No learned scale parameter in this minimal version.

99.text: Attention module with a flag to switch between learned positional embeddings and RoPE.

100.text: The use_rope flag lets us run a fair A/B comparison with identical architecture otherwise.

102.text: Store the flag so forward knows which position strategy to use.

103.text: Query projection, same for both variants.

104.text: Key projection, same weights, but RoPE will rotate these before the dot product.

105.text: Value projection, notably, RoPE does NOT touch values.

106.text: Output projection merges heads back together.

108.text: The attention forward pass takes a full input tensor and produces contextualized output. RoPE rotation happens internally before the dot product.

109.text: Unpack batch size, sequence length, and embedding dimension.

110.text: Project to queries, reshape into heads: (B, T, C) -> (B, n_head, T, head_dim).

111.text: Keys get the same per-head reshape as queries. Each head's keys will only be compared against that same head's queries.

112.text: Same for values, but values won't be rotated even with RoPE.

114.text: Branch: if RoPE is enabled, rotate Q and K by position-dependent angles.

115.text: Rotate queries, each position gets a unique rotation angle.

116.text: Rotate keys, when Q and K are dot-producted, the rotation angles subtract to give relative position.

120.text: Upper-triangular mask: each position can only attend to itself and earlier positions (causal/autoregressive).

121.text: Set future positions to -infinity so softmax gives them zero weight.

124.text: Weighted sum of values, reshape back from heads to embedding dimension.

125.text: Output projection, mix information across heads.

128.text: Feed-forward network: expand to 4x, apply nonlinearity, project back.

131.text: Expand to 4x the embedding dimension, the "hidden" layer of the MLP.

132.text: Project back down to embedding dimension.

134.text: ReLU activation between the two linear layers.

135.text: Two-layer MLP with ReLU: the pattern-matching complement to attention's pattern-finding.

138.text: Transformer block: attention + MLP, each with pre-norm and residual connection.

139.text: Thread the RoPE flag down to the attention layer so it knows whether to apply rotary position encoding or rely on the learned position embeddings.

141.text: Pre-norm before attention (LLaMA-style, more stable than post-norm).

142.text: Attention layer, configured for either learned positions or RoPE.

143.text: Normalize activations before the feed-forward network. Like the pre-attention norm, this stabilizes training by keeping activations on a consistent scale.

144.text: The position-independent feed-forward network. After attention has mixed information across positions, the MLP transforms each position's representation individually.

146.text: Forward: residual connections let gradients flow straight through.

152.text: The full model, identical architecture for both variants, differing only in positional encoding.

153.text: Constructor takes use_rope to choose the position strategy.

155.text: Remember whether this model uses RoPE so the forward pass knows whether to add learned position embeddings or skip them entirely.

156.text: Token embedding table: maps each of 27 token ids to a 16-dimensional vector.

157.text: Only create learned positional embeddings when NOT using RoPE, this is the parameter saving.

158.text: Learned position embeddings: one vector per position. RoPE eliminates this entire table.

160.text: Stack transformer blocks, each configured with the same position strategy.

161.text: Output projection: from embedding space back to vocabulary logits.

162.text: Walk through every submodule and apply the custom weight initializer. This ensures consistent starting conditions whether the module is a linear layer or an embedding.

165.text: Small random initialization (std=0.08) for all linear and embedding weights.

167.text: Normal distribution init, not too large (exploding), not too small (vanishing).

169.text: Forward pass: embed, add positions (if learned), normalize, transform, project to logits.

171.text: Convert each input token ID into a dense 16-dimensional vector. These embeddings are the model's learned representation of each character.

172.text: Only add learned positional embeddings for the baseline variant.

173.text: Add position vectors to token embeddings, the learned-position approach from labs 01-08.

175.text: Send the representation through each transformer block in sequence. Each block first applies attention (with or without RoPE) then the MLP.

177.text: Project to vocabulary-sized logits for next-token prediction.

183.text: Shared training function, both models train with identical hyperparameters for a fair comparison.

185.text: Adam optimizer with slightly aggressive betas for small-model training.

187.text: Train for 1000 steps, cycling through the shuffled dataset.

188.text: Cycle through names. With 32K names and 1000 steps, we see ~3% of the data.

190.text: Clip to block_size to handle names longer than 16 characters.

192.text: Input: all tokens except the last.

193.text: Target: all tokens shifted by one, the next-token prediction task.

196.text: Cross-entropy loss: how surprised is the model by the correct next token?

198.text: Zero out gradients from the previous step.

199.text: Backpropagate the loss through the entire computation graph.

200.text: Linear learning rate warmdown: start at 1e-2, linearly decay to 0.

201.text: Write the decayed learning rate into every optimizer parameter group so the next weight update takes a smaller step as training progresses.

212.text: Generate names autoregressively, one token at a time, feeding each back as input.

214.text: Switch to eval mode (disables dropout, etc.).

216.text: No gradient computation needed during generation.

218.text: Start with just the BOS token.

220.text: Feed the current sequence (up to block_size) through the model.

221.text: Get logits for the last position, the model's prediction for the next token.

222.text: Divide by temperature: lower = more confident/repetitive, higher = more random/creative.

223.text: Turn raw logits into a proper probability distribution via softmax. Sampling from these probabilities gives us the next token.

224.text: Sample from the distribution, stochastic generation.

225.text: If we sampled BOS, the name is complete.

227.text: Add the sampled token and continue generating.

228.text: Decode token ids back to characters.

234.text: Train the baseline first: standard learned positional embeddings, same as labs 01-08.

235.text: Reset seed so both models start from the same random initialization.

236.text: Baseline model with learned position embeddings.

237.text: Count parameters, the baseline has extra parameters for the position embedding table.

239.text: Train the baseline and capture loss history.

241.text: Now train the RoPE variant with the same seed and data order.

242.text: Same random init for a fair comparison.

243.text: RoPE model, no learned position embeddings, positions encoded through rotation.

244.text: Count parameters, fewer than baseline because there's no wpe embedding table.

245.text: Show the parameter saving: RoPE encodes position for free (no learned parameters).

246.text: Train RoPE variant with identical hyperparameters.

250.text: Moving average of recent losses smooths out step-to-step noise.

251.text: Average the last 50 losses for each model.

252.text: Same window for the RoPE model.

256.text: Generate names from the learned-position model.

257.text: Generate names from the RoPE model, quality should be comparable despite fewer parameters.

title: Grouped-Query Attention (GQA/MQA)
tagline: From MHA to MQA to GQA: sharing KV heads reduces memory during inference while preserving quality.
code:
"""
microGPT — Grouped-Query Attention (GQA/MQA) edition.

Shows the progression from Multi-Head Attention (MHA) → Multi-Query Attention
(MQA) → Grouped-Query Attention (GQA). The key idea: KV heads can be shared
across multiple query heads without significant quality loss, dramatically
reducing memory for the KV cache during inference.

Same base architecture as lab 03 (PyTorch), but the attention module is
parameterised by n_kv_head so a single class covers all three variants.
"""

import math
import os
import random

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F

random.seed(42)
torch.manual_seed(42)

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Dataset & Tokenizer
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
input_path = os.path.join(os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__)), "..", "data", "input.txt")
if not os.path.exists(input_path):
    import urllib.request

    url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/karpathy/makemore/refs/heads/master/names.txt"
    urllib.request.urlretrieve(url, input_path)

docs = [l.strip() for l in open(input_path).read().strip().split("\n") if l.strip()]
random.shuffle(docs)
print(f"num docs: {len(docs)}")
uchars = sorted(set("".join(docs)))
BOS = len(uchars)
vocab_size = len(uchars) + 1
print(f"vocab size: {vocab_size}")

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Model config
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
n_embd = 16     # embedding dimension
n_head = 4      # number of attention heads
n_layer = 1     # number of layers
block_size = 16 # maximum sequence length
head_dim = n_embd // n_head # dimension of each head


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Model components
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
class RMSNorm(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, _dim, eps=1e-5):
        super().__init__()
        self.eps = eps

    def forward(self, x):
        return x * torch.rsqrt(x.pow(2).mean(-1, keepdim=True) + self.eps)


class FlexAttention(nn.Module):
    """Unified attention that covers MHA, GQA, and MQA via n_kv_head."""

    def __init__(self, n_kv_head):
        super().__init__()
        self.n_kv_head = n_kv_head
        assert n_head % n_kv_head == 0, "n_head must be divisible by n_kv_head"
        self.repeats = n_head // n_kv_head

        # Q projection: always full heads
        self.wq = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)
        # K, V projections: only n_kv_head heads
        self.wk = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_kv_head * head_dim, bias=False)
        self.wv = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_kv_head * head_dim, bias=False)
        # Output projection
        self.wo = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)

    def forward(self, x):
        B, T, C = x.shape

        # Project queries (full heads), keys and values (fewer heads)
        q = self.wq(x).view(B, T, n_head, head_dim).transpose(1, 2)  # (B, n_head, T, hd)
        k = self.wk(x).view(B, T, self.n_kv_head, head_dim).transpose(1, 2)  # (B, n_kv_head, T, hd)
        v = self.wv(x).view(B, T, self.n_kv_head, head_dim).transpose(1, 2)  # (B, n_kv_head, T, hd)

        # Expand KV heads to match Q heads by repeating
        if self.repeats > 1:
            k = k.repeat_interleave(self.repeats, dim=1)  # (B, n_head, T, hd)
            v = v.repeat_interleave(self.repeats, dim=1)  # (B, n_head, T, hd)

        # Standard scaled dot-product attention with causal mask
        att = (q @ k.transpose(-2, -1)) / math.sqrt(head_dim)
        mask = torch.triu(torch.ones(T, T, device=x.device), diagonal=1).bool()
        att = att.masked_fill(mask, float("-inf"))
        att = F.softmax(att, dim=-1)

        out = (att @ v).transpose(1, 2).reshape(B, T, C)
        return self.wo(out)


class MLP(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.fc1 = nn.Linear(n_embd, 4 * n_embd, bias=False)
        self.fc2 = nn.Linear(4 * n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)

    def forward(self, x):
        return self.fc2(F.relu(self.fc1(x)))


class Block(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, n_kv_head):
        super().__init__()
        self.norm1 = RMSNorm(n_embd)
        self.attn = FlexAttention(n_kv_head)
        self.norm2 = RMSNorm(n_embd)
        self.mlp = MLP()

    def forward(self, x):
        x = x + self.attn(self.norm1(x))
        x = x + self.mlp(self.norm2(x))
        return x


class MicroGPT(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, n_kv_head):
        super().__init__()
        self.wte = nn.Embedding(vocab_size, n_embd)
        self.wpe = nn.Embedding(block_size, n_embd)
        self.norm_in = RMSNorm(n_embd)
        self.layers = nn.ModuleList([Block(n_kv_head) for _ in range(n_layer)])
        self.lm_head = nn.Linear(n_embd, vocab_size, bias=False)
        self.apply(self._init_weights)

    @staticmethod
    def _init_weights(module):
        if isinstance(module, (nn.Linear, nn.Embedding)):
            nn.init.normal_(module.weight, mean=0.0, std=0.08)

    def forward(self, idx):
        B, T = idx.shape
        tok_emb = self.wte(idx)
        pos_emb = self.wpe(torch.arange(T, device=idx.device))
        x = self.norm_in(tok_emb + pos_emb)
        for layer in self.layers:
            x = layer(x)
        return self.lm_head(x)


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Training + comparison
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
device = "cpu"
num_steps = 1000
temperature = 0.5 # in (0, 1], control the "creativity" of generated text, low to high

variants = [
    ("MHA", n_head),  # 4 KV heads — standard multi-head attention
    ("GQA", 2),  # 2 KV heads — grouped-query attention
    ("MQA", 1),  # 1 KV head  — multi-query attention
]

def generate(model, label, num_samples=10):
    """Generate names from a trained model."""
    model.eval()
    print(f"\n  --- {label} generated names ---")
    with torch.no_grad():
        for sample_idx in range(num_samples):
            tokens = [BOS]
            for _ in range(block_size):
                idx = torch.tensor([tokens[-block_size:]], device=device)
                logits = model(idx)
                logits = logits[0, -1] / temperature
                probs = F.softmax(logits, dim=-1)
                token_id = torch.multinomial(probs, 1).item()
                if token_id == BOS:
                    break
                tokens.append(token_id)
            name = "".join(uchars[t] for t in tokens[1:])
            print(f"  sample {sample_idx + 1:2d}: {name}")


for variant_name, n_kv_head in variants:
    print(f"\n{'=' * 60}")
    print(f"  {variant_name}: n_head={n_head}, n_kv_head={n_kv_head}")
    print(f"{'=' * 60}")

    # Reset seed for fair comparison
    random.seed(42)
    torch.manual_seed(42)
    random.shuffle(docs)

    model = MicroGPT(n_kv_head).to(device)
    num_params = sum(p.numel() for p in model.parameters())
    print(f"num params: {num_params}")

    # KV cache memory estimate (fp16): n_kv_head * head_dim * seq_len * 2(K+V) * 2(bytes) * n_layer
    kv_cache_bytes = n_kv_head * head_dim * block_size * 2 * 2 * n_layer
    print(
        f"KV cache memory (fp16): {kv_cache_bytes} bytes "
        f"({n_kv_head} kv_heads x {head_dim} head_dim x {block_size} seq_len x 2 tensors x 2 bytes x {n_layer} layers)"
    )

    optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=1e-2, betas=(0.85, 0.99), eps=1e-8)

    for step in range(num_steps):
        doc = docs[step % len(docs)]
        tokens = [BOS] + [uchars.index(ch) for ch in doc] + [BOS]
        n = min(block_size, len(tokens) - 1)

        input_ids = torch.tensor([tokens[:n]], device=device)
        targets = torch.tensor([tokens[1 : n + 1]], device=device)

        logits = model(input_ids)
        loss = F.cross_entropy(logits.view(-1, vocab_size), targets.view(-1))

        optimizer.zero_grad()
        loss.backward()
        lr_t = 1e-2 * (1 - step / num_steps)
        for pg in optimizer.param_groups:
            pg["lr"] = lr_t
        optimizer.step()

        if (step + 1) % 200 == 0 or step == 0:
            print(f"  step {step + 1:4d} / {num_steps:4d} | loss {loss.item():.4f}")

    print(f"\n  {variant_name} final loss: {loss.item():.4f}")
    generate(model, variant_name)

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Summary
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
print(f"\n{'=' * 60}")
print("  Summary: KV head sharing vs parameter count")
print(f"{'=' * 60}")
for variant_name, n_kv_head in variants:
    kv_params = 2 * n_kv_head * head_dim * n_embd * n_layer  # wk + wv params
    kv_cache = n_kv_head * head_dim * block_size * 2 * n_layer * 2
    print(
        f"  {variant_name:3s} (n_kv_head={n_kv_head}): "
        f"KV proj params={kv_params:4d}, "
        f"KV cache (fp16)={kv_cache:4d} bytes"
    )
print("\n  GQA gives a sweet spot: fewer KV params than MHA,")
print("  more capacity than MQA, with minimal quality loss.")

64.text: The star of this lab: one attention class that handles MHA, GQA, and MQA by varying n_kv_head.

67.text: The only parameter that changes between variants: how many KV heads to use.

70.text: Query heads must divide evenly into KV groups, each group of query heads shares one KV head.

71.text: How many query heads share each KV head. MHA: 1 (no sharing). GQA: 2. MQA: 4 (all share one).

74.text: Queries always have the full n_head heads, query capacity is never reduced.

76.text: Keys use fewer heads, this is where the memory saving comes from. With MQA, this is just 1 * head_dim = 4 parameters per position instead of 4 * head_dim = 16.

79.text: Output projection is always full-sized, it recombines all query heads.

81.text: Forward pass, project, expand KV if needed, attend.

82.text: Unpack batch, sequence length, embedding dimension.

85.text: Queries: always full heads. Shape: (B, n_head, T, head_dim).

86.text: Keys: only n_kv_head heads. With MQA this is (B, 1, T, head_dim), 4x smaller than queries.

87.text: Values: same reduced shape as keys.

90.text: If KV heads < query heads, we need to expand KV to match. This is the GQA/MQA trick.

91.text: Repeat each KV head to serve its group of query heads. Logically shared, but physically copied for the matmul. In practice, this repeat is nearly free, it's a view/broadcast, not a memcpy.

92.text: Values get the same expansion as keys so that each query head has a matching value vector to retrieve content from.

95.text: Standard scaled dot-product attention, identical for all three variants after KV expansion.

96.text: Causal mask: no peeking at future tokens.

107.text: Project each token's embedding into a 4x wider hidden space to give the network more room to compute.

108.text: Compress the expanded 4x-wide hidden representation back down to the original embedding dimension.

110.text: Run the input through the two-layer feed-forward network with ReLU activation in between.

114.text: Transformer block parameterized by KV head count.

117.text: Normalize activations before the attention sublayer. Pre-norm keeps gradients well-behaved and is the standard in modern transformers like LLaMA.

119.text: Normalize activations before the feed-forward network. Like the pre-attention norm, this stabilizes training by keeping activations on a consistent scale.

122.text: Forward: residual connections around attention and MLP.

123.text: Add the attention output back to the input via a residual connection. This lets the layer refine the representation without risk of destroying useful information.

124.text: Add the MLP output to the residual stream so the model can refine representations incrementally.

162.text: GQA: n_kv_head=2, two groups of two query heads, each group sharing one K,V pair. The sweet spot used by LLaMA-2 70B.

163.text: MQA: n_kv_head=1, all query heads share a single K,V pair. Maximum compression, used by PaLM and Falcon.
