title: KV Cache
tagline: The single most important inference optimization. Cache K and V tensors so each new token only processes one position.
code:
"""
microGPT — KV Cache edition.

Same model as lab 03, but with a KV-cache-aware inference path that avoids
recomputing Key and Value tensors for already-processed positions. This is
THE fundamental optimization behind every fast LLM serving system.

Without KV cache: each new token reprocesses the full sequence → O(n³) total.
With KV cache: each new token only computes attention for ONE position → O(n²) total.
"""

import math
import os
import random
import time

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F

random.seed(42)
torch.manual_seed(42)

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Dataset & Tokenizer
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
input_path = os.path.join(os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__)), "..", "data", "input.txt")
if not os.path.exists(input_path):
    import urllib.request

    url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/karpathy/makemore/refs/heads/master/names.txt"
    urllib.request.urlretrieve(url, input_path)

docs = [l.strip() for l in open(input_path).read().strip().split("\n") if l.strip()]
random.shuffle(docs)
print(f"num docs: {len(docs)}")

uchars = sorted(set("".join(docs)))
BOS = len(uchars)
vocab_size = len(uchars) + 1
print(f"vocab size: {vocab_size}")

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Model config
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
n_embd = 16     # embedding dimension
n_head = 4      # number of attention heads
n_layer = 1     # number of layers
block_size = 16 # maximum sequence length
head_dim = n_embd // n_head # dimension of each head

# ===========================================================================
# Unified model — supports both standard and KV-cached inference
# ===========================================================================
# One model, two inference paths. When kv_cache=None, attention behaves like
# standard causal attention (same as lab 03). When kv_cache is provided,
# only the new positions compute Q/K/V and cached K/V are prepended.


class RMSNorm(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, _dim, eps=1e-5):
        super().__init__()
        self.eps = eps

    def forward(self, x):
        return x * torch.rsqrt(x.pow(2).mean(-1, keepdim=True) + self.eps)


class CausalSelfAttention(nn.Module):
    """Attention with optional KV cache for efficient autoregressive decoding."""

    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.wq = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)
        self.wk = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)
        self.wv = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)
        self.wo = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)

    def forward(self, x, kv_cache=None):
        B, T_new, C = x.shape
        q = self.wq(x).view(B, T_new, n_head, head_dim).transpose(1, 2)
        k = self.wk(x).view(B, T_new, n_head, head_dim).transpose(1, 2)
        v = self.wv(x).view(B, T_new, n_head, head_dim).transpose(1, 2)

        if kv_cache is not None:
            cached_k, cached_v = kv_cache
            k = torch.cat([cached_k, k], dim=2)
            v = torch.cat([cached_v, v], dim=2)

        new_cache = (k, v)
        T_total = k.shape[2]

        att = (q @ k.transpose(-2, -1)) / math.sqrt(head_dim)
        # Causal mask: row i (absolute pos T_total-T_new+i) attends to cols 0..abs_pos
        mask = torch.zeros(T_new, T_total, device=x.device, dtype=torch.bool)
        for i in range(T_new):
            mask[i, T_total - T_new + i + 1 :] = True
        att = att.masked_fill(mask, float("-inf"))
        att = F.softmax(att, dim=-1)

        out = (att @ v).transpose(1, 2).reshape(B, T_new, C)
        return self.wo(out), new_cache


class MLP(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.fc1 = nn.Linear(n_embd, 4 * n_embd, bias=False)
        self.fc2 = nn.Linear(4 * n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)

    def forward(self, x):
        return self.fc2(F.relu(self.fc1(x)))


class Block(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.norm1 = RMSNorm(n_embd)
        self.attn = CausalSelfAttention()
        self.norm2 = RMSNorm(n_embd)
        self.mlp = MLP()

    def forward(self, x, kv_cache=None):
        attn_out, new_cache = self.attn(self.norm1(x), kv_cache=kv_cache)
        x = x + attn_out
        x = x + self.mlp(self.norm2(x))
        return x, new_cache


class MicroGPT(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.wte = nn.Embedding(vocab_size, n_embd)
        self.wpe = nn.Embedding(block_size, n_embd)
        self.norm_in = RMSNorm(n_embd)
        self.layers = nn.ModuleList([Block() for _ in range(n_layer)])
        self.lm_head = nn.Linear(n_embd, vocab_size, bias=False)
        self.apply(self._init_weights)

    @staticmethod
    def _init_weights(module):
        if isinstance(module, (nn.Linear, nn.Embedding)):
            nn.init.normal_(module.weight, mean=0.0, std=0.08)

    def forward(self, idx, past_caches=None, start_pos=0):
        B, T = idx.shape
        tok_emb = self.wte(idx)
        pos_emb = self.wpe(torch.arange(start_pos, start_pos + T, device=idx.device))
        x = self.norm_in(tok_emb + pos_emb)
        new_caches = []
        for i, layer in enumerate(self.layers):
            cache_i = past_caches[i] if past_caches is not None else None
            x, new_cache = layer(x, kv_cache=cache_i)
            new_caches.append(new_cache)
        return self.lm_head(x), new_caches


# ===========================================================================
# Training (identical to lab 03 — ignores caches during training)
# ===========================================================================
device = "cpu"
model = MicroGPT().to(device)
print(f"num params: {sum(p.numel() for p in model.parameters())}")

optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=1e-2, betas=(0.85, 0.99), eps=1e-8)
num_steps = 1000

for step in range(num_steps):
    doc = docs[step % len(docs)]
    tokens = [BOS] + [uchars.index(ch) for ch in doc] + [BOS]
    n = min(block_size, len(tokens) - 1)

    input_ids = torch.tensor([tokens[:n]], device=device)
    targets = torch.tensor([tokens[1 : n + 1]], device=device)

    logits, _ = model(input_ids)
    loss = F.cross_entropy(logits.view(-1, vocab_size), targets.view(-1))

    optimizer.zero_grad()
    loss.backward()
    lr_t = 1e-2 * (1 - step / num_steps)
    for pg in optimizer.param_groups:
        pg["lr"] = lr_t
    optimizer.step()

    if (step + 1) % 200 == 0 or step == 0:
        print(f"step {step + 1:4d} / {num_steps:4d} | loss {loss.item():.4f}")

# ===========================================================================
# Inference comparison — naive (no cache) vs KV cache
# ===========================================================================
# Same model, same weights — two generation strategies.
temperature = 0.5 # in (0, 1], control the "creativity" of generated text, low to high
num_samples = 20
model.eval()


def sample_token(logits):
    probs = F.softmax(logits[0, -1] / temperature, dim=-1)
    return torch.multinomial(probs, 1).item()


def generate_naive(num_samples=20):
    """Standard generation: re-run full sequence at every step. O(n^3) total attention."""
    total_attn_ops = 0
    names = []
    for sample_idx in range(num_samples):
        torch.manual_seed(1000 + sample_idx)
        tokens = [BOS]
        for _ in range(block_size):
            idx = torch.tensor([tokens[-block_size:]], device=device)
            T = idx.shape[1]
            total_attn_ops += T * T * n_head
            logits, _ = model(idx)  # no cache — recomputes everything
            token_id = sample_token(logits)
            if token_id == BOS:
                break
            tokens.append(token_id)
        names.append("".join(uchars[t] for t in tokens[1:]))
    return names, total_attn_ops


def generate_with_cache(num_samples=20):
    """KV-cache generation: only process new token each step. O(n^2) total attention."""
    total_attn_ops = 0
    names = []
    for sample_idx in range(num_samples):
        torch.manual_seed(1000 + sample_idx)
        tokens = [BOS]
        past_caches = None
        for _ in range(block_size):
            if past_caches is None:
                idx = torch.tensor([tokens], device=device)
                T_new, start_pos = len(tokens), 0
            else:
                idx = torch.tensor([[tokens[-1]]], device=device)
                T_new, start_pos = 1, len(tokens) - 1
            total_attn_ops += T_new * len(tokens) * n_head
            logits, past_caches = model(idx, past_caches=past_caches, start_pos=start_pos)
            token_id = sample_token(logits)
            if token_id == BOS:
                break
            tokens.append(token_id)
        names.append("".join(uchars[t] for t in tokens[1:]))
    return names, total_attn_ops


print("\n" + "=" * 60)
print("INFERENCE COMPARISON: Naive vs KV Cache")
print("=" * 60)

with torch.no_grad():
    t0 = time.perf_counter()
    names_naive, ops_naive = generate_naive(num_samples)
    t_naive = time.perf_counter() - t0

    t0 = time.perf_counter()
    names_cached, ops_cached = generate_with_cache(num_samples)
    t_cached = time.perf_counter() - t0

# Verify identical outputs
print("\n--- Generated names (both methods produce identical output) ---")
all_match = all(n1 == n2 for n1, n2 in zip(names_naive, names_cached))
for i, (n1, n2) in enumerate(zip(names_naive, names_cached)):
    match = "OK" if n1 == n2 else "MISMATCH!"
    print(f"  {i + 1:2d}: {n1:12s}  |  {n2:12s}  [{match}]")
print(f"\nAll outputs identical: {all_match}")

# Operation counts
print("\n--- Attention operation counts (Q*K multiply-adds) ---")
print(f"  Naive (full recompute):  {ops_naive:,} ops")
print(f"  KV cache (incremental):  {ops_cached:,} ops")
print(f"  Reduction:               {ops_naive / ops_cached:.1f}x fewer operations")

# Timing
print("\n--- Wall-clock time ---")
print(f"  Naive:    {t_naive * 1000:.1f} ms")
print(f"  KV cache: {t_cached * 1000:.1f} ms")
if t_cached > 0:
    print(f"  Speedup:  {t_naive / t_cached:.2f}x")

# Theoretical analysis
T = block_size
print("\n--- Theoretical analysis ---")
print(f"  For a sequence of length T = {T}:")
print(f"  Naive total attention:   sum(t^2 for t=1..T) = T(T+1)(2T+1)/6 = {T * (T + 1) * (2 * T + 1) // 6} (per head)")
print(f"  Cached total attention:  sum(t   for t=1..T) = T(T+1)/2       = {T * (T + 1) // 2} (per head)")
print(f"  Ratio:                   (2T+1)/3 = {(2 * T + 1) / 3:.1f}x")

# ===========================================================================
# Summary
# ===========================================================================
print("\n" + "=" * 60)
print("WHY KV CACHE MATTERS")
print("=" * 60)
print("""
  Cache stores K,V from previous positions. Each new token computes only its
  own Q,K,V and attends to all cached K,V — no recomputation.

                  Without cache         With cache
  Step 1:         1 x 1  attention      1 x 1  attention
  Step 2:         2 x 2  attention      1 x 2  attention
  Step 3:         3 x 3  attention      1 x 3  attention
  ...
  Step T:         T x T  attention      1 x T  attention
  ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  Total:          ~T^3/3 operations     ~T^2/2 operations

  At scale (7B model, 2048 ctx): ~680x speedup, ~1 GB cache per request.
  Prerequisite for speculative decoding, PagedAttention, disaggregated serving.
""")

12.text: Import math, os, random, and time from the standard library for various utilities.

13.text: Import os for building portable file paths to the dataset.

14.text: Import random for shuffling the dataset and seeding reproducibility.

15.text: Wall-clock timing for the naive vs cached benchmark.

17.text: Import the core PyTorch library for tensor operations and autograd.

18.text: Neural network building blocks like Linear, Embedding, and ModuleList that automatically register their parameters for training.

19.text: Import PyTorch's functional API for operations like softmax, cross-entropy, and relu.

22.text: Set the PyTorch random seed for reproducible weight initialization and sampling.

27.text: Construct a portable file path to the shared dataset, relative to this script's location, so the code works regardless of where you clone the repo.

28.text: If the dataset file is missing, we download it automatically. This keeps the script self-contained with no manual setup required.

34.text: Read all names from the dataset file, one per line, stripping whitespace.

35.text: Randomize the training order so the model does not memorize sequences of names.

38.text: Gather every unique character from all names and sort them to build a deterministic character-to-integer vocabulary mapping.

39.text: Use the length of the character set as the beginning-of-sequence token ID.

40.text: The full vocabulary has 27 tokens: 26 lowercase letters plus the BOS marker.

46.text: Same tiny model config as lab 03, the model is identical, only inference changes.

47.text: Four parallel attention heads, each processing a different 4-dimensional slice.

48.text: A single transformer layer keeps the comparison focused on the caching optimization.

49.text: The longest sequence the model can process at once. Names are short, so 16 tokens is more than enough for this dataset.

50.text: Each attention head operates on a 4-dimensional slice of the 16-dimensional embedding.

66.text: Divide by the root-mean-square of the activation vector. Simpler than LayerNorm since it skips mean subtraction, but equally effective for stabilizing training.

69.text: The critical change: attention now accepts an optional KV cache. Same weights, two inference paths.

74.text: Q, K, V, and output projections, identical to the uncached version.

75.text: Linear projection that transforms embeddings into key vectors for attention.

76.text: Linear projection that transforms embeddings into value vectors for attention.

77.text: Project the concatenated multi-head output back to the model dimension.

79.text: Forward now takes an optional kv_cache, this is the entire innovation of this lab.

80.text: T_new is 1 during cached inference (just the new token) vs. full sequence length without cache.

81.text: Project queries for the new positions only. During cached inference, this is a single token.

82.text: Project keys for the new positions only.

83.text: Project values for the new positions only.

85.text: If we have a cache from previous steps, prepend it.

86.text: Unpack the cached K and V tensors from previous positions.

87.text: Concatenate cached keys with the new key. Now K covers all positions seen so far.

88.text: Same for values. The new token can attend to the full history without recomputing it.

90.text: Save the updated cache for the next step, it grows by one position each time.

91.text: T_total = total sequence length including cached positions.

93.text: Attention: Q is (B, heads, T_new, hd), K is (B, heads, T_total, hd). The result is (B, heads, T_new, T_total).

95.text: Custom causal mask: each new query position can see all cached positions plus itself, but not future positions.

96.text: Build the mask row by row. Row i (absolute position T_total-T_new+i) masks out everything after itself.

97.text: Mask future positions for this query position.

98.text: Replace scores for future positions with negative infinity. After softmax, these become exactly zero, enforcing the causal constraint that tokens cannot peek ahead.

101.text: Weighted sum of values and reshape.

102.text: Return both the output AND the updated cache, the cache is passed to the next step.

105.text: The two-layer feed-forward network, unchanged from previous labs. The MLP operates per-position and does not need any caching since it has no cross-position state.

108.text: Project each token's representation into a 4x wider hidden space for richer computation.

109.text: Compress the expanded hidden representation back down to the original embedding dimension.

111.text: Run the input through the two-layer feed-forward network with ReLU activation.

112.text: Two linear layers with ReLU in between. The expansion to 4x width gives the network more room to learn nonlinear transformations before compressing back down.

115.text: Transformer block, now threads the KV cache through.

118.text: Normalize activations before the attention sublayer. Pre-norm keeps gradients well-behaved and is the standard in modern transformers like LLaMA.

119.text: The attention sublayer, now accepting an optional KV cache. When a cache is provided, it only processes new positions and prepends cached keys and values.

123.text: Forward now accepts and returns a cache.

124.text: Attention: receives cache, returns updated cache.

125.text: Add the attention output back to the input via a residual connection. The MLP and attention each contribute refinements without overwriting the original representation.

126.text: MLP with residual (MLP doesn't need a cache).

127.text: Return both the block's output and the updated KV cache. The caller collects caches from all layers and passes them back on the next generation step.

130.text: Full model, threads caches through all layers.

133.text: Look up a learned embedding vector for each input token to represent its identity.

134.text: Positional embeddings, start_pos will offset these during cached inference.

135.text: Normalize the combined embeddings before feeding them into the transformer layers.

136.text: A stack of transformer blocks, each with its own attention and MLP. During cached inference, each layer maintains its own independent KV cache.

137.text: Map the final hidden states to vocabulary-sized logits for next-token prediction.

138.text: Apply the custom weight initialization function to every module in the model.

141.text: Static method that initializes weights with a small Gaussian for stable early training.

143.text: Draw initial weights from a narrow Gaussian so activations stay reasonably scaled.

145.text: Forward now takes past_caches and start_pos, the KV cache interface.

146.text: Extract the batch size and sequence length from the input tensor shape.

147.text: Look up a learned embedding vector for each input token to represent its identity.

148.text: Position embeddings offset by start_pos. During cached inference, this is the position of the new token, not 0. Without this offset, the model would think every new token is at position 0.

149.text: Combine token and position embeddings, normalize.

150.text: Collect updated caches from each layer.

152.text: Grab the cache for this layer (or None on the first pass).

153.text: Forward through the layer, receiving the updated cache.

154.text: Save this layer's updated cache into the collection. On the next generation step, it will be passed back so the layer can skip recomputing past keys and values.

155.text: Return logits AND the full cache stack for the next generation step.

161.text: Use CPU for this tiny model since it trains in seconds without a GPU.

162.text: Instantiate the model with the same architecture as lab 03. The only difference is that the forward pass now supports an optional KV cache for efficient generation.

165.text: Set up the Adam optimizer with momentum and epsilon tuned for this small model.

166.text: Train for 1000 steps, which is enough for this tiny model to learn basic name patterns.

168.text: Training loop, caches are ignored during training (standard teacher-forced training).

169.text: Pick the next training document by cycling through the shuffled name list.

170.text: Encode each character in the name as a token ID, bookended by BOS markers.

171.text: Limit the sequence length to block_size so it fits the model's positional embeddings.

173.text: Convert the token IDs into a tensor the model can consume as its input sequence.

174.text: Build the target sequence by shifting the input one position to the right for next-token prediction.

176.text: Forward pass. The _ discards caches, training doesn't need them.

177.text: Measure how far the model's predictions are from the true next tokens using cross-entropy.

179.text: Clear out any leftover gradients from the previous step so they do not accumulate.

180.text: Compute gradients for every parameter by propagating the loss backward through the graph.

181.text: Linearly reduce the learning rate from its initial value down to zero over the course of training.

182.text: Write the decayed learning rate into every parameter group so the optimizer uses it.

184.text: Apply the optimizer step, adjusting every parameter in the direction that reduces the loss.

193.text: Temperature controls the sampling distribution's sharpness. At 0.5, high-probability tokens become even more dominant, producing more conservative but coherent names.

195.text: Switch to eval mode for inference.

198.text: Shared sampling helper: take logits, apply temperature, sample one token.

199.text: Temperature-scaled softmax on the last position's logits.

203.text: Naive generation: re-run the FULL sequence through the model at every step. This is O(n^3) total attention.

205.text: Track attention operations to quantify the difference.

207.text: Fixed seed per sample so naive and cached produce identical outputs.

208.text: Same seed as cached generation, outputs MUST match.

209.text: Begin each generated name with the beginning-of-sequence token so the model starts fresh.

211.text: Feed the ENTIRE sequence so far. This is the waste, we recompute Q/K/V for all previous tokens.

212.text: Count attention operations: T*T per head, for all heads.

213.text: O(T^2) per step, and T grows each step, giving O(T^3) total.

214.text: No cache, everything is recomputed from scratch every step.

215.text: Randomly draw one token from the probability distribution for stochastic generation.

216.text: If the model predicts BOS, treat it as an end-of-name signal and stop generating.

218.text: Add the newly sampled token to the growing sequence and continue generating.

219.text: Convert the list of generated token IDs back into a readable name string.

223.text: KV-cached generation: only process the NEW token each step. O(n^2) total attention.

225.text: Count the attention operations (Q times K multiply-adds) at each step so we can precisely measure how much computation the KV cache saves.

228.text: Same seed, outputs will be identical.

229.text: Begin each generated name with the beginning-of-sequence token so the model starts fresh.

230.text: Initialize the cache as empty since no tokens have been processed yet on the first pass.

232.text: First step: no cache yet, process the full initial sequence (just BOS).

234.text: Position zero for the very first token. The positional embedding needs this offset to assign the correct position encoding to the BOS token.

236.text: Subsequent steps: only feed the LAST token. This is the key optimization, one token instead of the full sequence.

237.text: Start position = where this token sits in the sequence.

238.text: Attention ops: T_new * T_total per head. With T_new=1, this is just T_total, linear, not quadratic.

239.text: Forward with cache. The cache grows by one position. Past K/V are NOT recomputed.

240.text: Randomly draw one token from the probability distribution for stochastic generation.

241.text: If the model predicts BOS, treat it as an end-of-name signal and stop generating.

243.text: Add the newly sampled token to the growing sequence and continue generating.

244.text: Convert the list of generated token IDs back into a readable name string.

252.text: Disable gradient tracking for both generation methods so the comparison is fair and neither wastes time computing gradients during inference.

253.text: Start the wall-clock timer for naive generation. Even at this tiny scale, the cubic cost is measurable against the quadratic cached version.

257.text: Start the wall-clock timer for cached generation. Comparing against the naive timer reveals the real-world speedup from avoiding recomputation.

263.text: Verify outputs are IDENTICAL, the cache is an optimization, not an approximation. Same model, same weights, same random seed = same output.

264.text: Side-by-side comparison of every generated name.

265.text: Flag any mismatch (there shouldn't be any).

273.text: The headline metric: how many fewer attention operations the cache requires.

279.text: Real timing comparison. At this tiny scale the speedup is modest; at 7B scale it's 100x+.

283.text: Theoretical analysis of the complexity difference.

286.text: Naive: sum of t^2 for t=1..T = T(T+1)(2T+1)/6. Cubic growth.

287.text: Cached: sum of t for t=1..T = T(T+1)/2. Quadratic growth.

288.text: The ratio grows linearly with sequence length, longer sequences benefit more from caching.

title: Sampling Strategies
tagline: Five strategies (greedy, temperature, top-k, top-p, min-p) producing completely different output from the same model.
code:
"""
microGPT — Sampling Strategies.

Same microGPT architecture as the PyTorch edition (03), but focused on
demonstrating how different sampling strategies shape model output.
Five strategies: greedy, temperature, top-k, top-p (nucleus), min-p.
Same model, completely different outputs.
"""

import math
import os
import random

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F

random.seed(42)
torch.manual_seed(42)

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Dataset & Tokenizer
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
input_path = os.path.join(os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__)), "..", "data", "input.txt")
if not os.path.exists(input_path):
    import urllib.request

    url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/karpathy/makemore/refs/heads/master/names.txt"
    urllib.request.urlretrieve(url, input_path)

docs = [l.strip() for l in open(input_path).read().strip().split("\n") if l.strip()]
random.shuffle(docs)
print(f"num docs: {len(docs)}")
uchars = sorted(set("".join(docs)))
BOS = len(uchars)
vocab_size = len(uchars) + 1
print(f"vocab size: {vocab_size}")

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Model
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
n_embd = 16     # embedding dimension
n_head = 4      # number of attention heads
n_layer = 1     # number of layers
block_size = 16 # maximum sequence length
head_dim = n_embd // n_head # dimension of each head


class RMSNorm(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, _dim, eps=1e-5):
        super().__init__()
        self.eps = eps

    def forward(self, x):
        return x * torch.rsqrt(x.pow(2).mean(-1, keepdim=True) + self.eps)


class CausalSelfAttention(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.wq = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)
        self.wk = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)
        self.wv = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)
        self.wo = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)

    def forward(self, x):
        B, T, C = x.shape
        q = self.wq(x).view(B, T, n_head, head_dim).transpose(1, 2)
        k = self.wk(x).view(B, T, n_head, head_dim).transpose(1, 2)
        v = self.wv(x).view(B, T, n_head, head_dim).transpose(1, 2)

        att = (q @ k.transpose(-2, -1)) / math.sqrt(head_dim)
        mask = torch.triu(torch.ones(T, T, device=x.device), diagonal=1).bool()
        att = att.masked_fill(mask, float("-inf"))
        att = F.softmax(att, dim=-1)

        out = (att @ v).transpose(1, 2).reshape(B, T, C)
        return self.wo(out)


class MLP(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.fc1 = nn.Linear(n_embd, 4 * n_embd, bias=False)
        self.fc2 = nn.Linear(4 * n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)

    def forward(self, x):
        return self.fc2(F.relu(self.fc1(x)))


class Block(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.norm1 = RMSNorm(n_embd)
        self.attn = CausalSelfAttention()
        self.norm2 = RMSNorm(n_embd)
        self.mlp = MLP()

    def forward(self, x):
        x = x + self.attn(self.norm1(x))
        x = x + self.mlp(self.norm2(x))
        return x


class MicroGPT(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.wte = nn.Embedding(vocab_size, n_embd)
        self.wpe = nn.Embedding(block_size, n_embd)
        self.norm_in = RMSNorm(n_embd)
        self.layers = nn.ModuleList([Block() for _ in range(n_layer)])
        self.lm_head = nn.Linear(n_embd, vocab_size, bias=False)
        self.apply(self._init_weights)

    @staticmethod
    def _init_weights(module):
        if isinstance(module, (nn.Linear, nn.Embedding)):
            nn.init.normal_(module.weight, mean=0.0, std=0.08)

    def forward(self, idx):
        B, T = idx.shape
        tok_emb = self.wte(idx)
        pos_emb = self.wpe(torch.arange(T, device=idx.device))
        x = self.norm_in(tok_emb + pos_emb)
        for layer in self.layers:
            x = layer(x)
        return self.lm_head(x)


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Training
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
device = "cpu"
model = MicroGPT().to(device)
print(f"num params: {sum(p.numel() for p in model.parameters())}")

optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=1e-2, betas=(0.85, 0.99), eps=1e-8)
num_steps = 1000

for step in range(num_steps):
    doc = docs[step % len(docs)]
    tokens = [BOS] + [uchars.index(ch) for ch in doc] + [BOS]
    n = min(block_size, len(tokens) - 1)

    input_ids = torch.tensor([tokens[:n]], device=device)
    targets = torch.tensor([tokens[1 : n + 1]], device=device)

    logits = model(input_ids)
    loss = F.cross_entropy(logits.view(-1, vocab_size), targets.view(-1))

    optimizer.zero_grad()
    loss.backward()
    lr_t = 1e-2 * (1 - step / num_steps)
    for pg in optimizer.param_groups:
        pg["lr"] = lr_t
    optimizer.step()

    if (step + 1) % 200 == 0 or step == 0:
        print(f"step {step + 1:4d} / {num_steps:4d} | loss {loss.item():.4f}")

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Sampling strategies
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------


def sample_greedy(logits):
    """Always pick the highest probability token. Deterministic but repetitive."""
    return logits.argmax().item()


def sample_temperature(logits, T=1.0):
    """Scale logits by 1/T before softmax.
    T->0: approaches greedy (peaked). T=1: model's learned distribution.
    T->inf: approaches uniform random."""
    probs = F.softmax(logits / T, dim=-1)
    return torch.multinomial(probs, 1).item()


def sample_top_k(logits, k=5, T=1.0):
    """Keep only top-k logits, set rest to -inf, then sample with temperature.
    Prevents sampling from the long tail of unlikely tokens."""
    scaled = logits / T
    topk_vals, _ = torch.topk(scaled, k)
    scaled[scaled < topk_vals[-1]] = float("-inf")
    probs = F.softmax(scaled, dim=-1)
    return torch.multinomial(probs, 1).item()


def sample_top_p(logits, p=0.9, T=1.0):
    """Nucleus sampling: sort by probability, keep smallest set whose
    cumulative probability >= p, zero out rest, renormalize, sample.
    Dynamically adjusts candidates based on distribution shape."""
    probs = F.softmax(logits / T, dim=-1)
    sorted_probs, sorted_idx = torch.sort(probs, descending=True)
    cumsum = torch.cumsum(sorted_probs, dim=-1)
    # Remove tokens with cumulative probability above p (keep first token always)
    mask = cumsum - sorted_probs > p
    sorted_probs[mask] = 0.0
    sorted_probs /= sorted_probs.sum()
    # Scatter back to original order
    out = torch.zeros_like(probs)
    out.scatter_(0, sorted_idx, sorted_probs)
    return torch.multinomial(out, 1).item()


def sample_min_p(logits, min_p=0.05, T=1.0):
    """Keep tokens with probability >= min_p * max_probability.
    Simpler than top-p, adapts to distribution shape naturally.
    Peaked distributions: few tokens pass. Flat: many pass."""
    probs = F.softmax(logits / T, dim=-1)
    threshold = min_p * probs.max()
    probs[probs < threshold] = 0.0
    probs /= probs.sum()
    return torch.multinomial(probs, 1).item()


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Generation
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------


def generate(model, sampler_fn, n=20):
    """Generate n names using the given sampling function."""
    names = []
    for _ in range(n):
        tokens = [BOS]
        for _ in range(block_size):
            idx = torch.tensor([tokens[-block_size:]], device=device)
            logits = model(idx)
            raw_logits = logits[0, -1]
            token_id = sampler_fn(raw_logits.clone())
            if token_id == BOS:
                break
            tokens.append(token_id)
        name = "".join(uchars[t] for t in tokens[1:])
        names.append(name)
    return names


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Compare all strategies
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
print("\n" + "=" * 60)
print("SAMPLING STRATEGY COMPARISON")
print("=" * 60)

model.eval()
with torch.no_grad():
    strategies = [
        ("Greedy", lambda lg: sample_greedy(lg)),
        ("Temperature 0.3 (conservative)", lambda lg: sample_temperature(lg, T=0.3)),
        ("Temperature 1.0 (model dist)", lambda lg: sample_temperature(lg, T=1.0)),
        ("Temperature 2.0 (chaotic)", lambda lg: sample_temperature(lg, T=2.0)),
        ("Top-k=3, T=0.8", lambda lg: sample_top_k(lg, k=3, T=0.8)),
        ("Top-k=10, T=0.8", lambda lg: sample_top_k(lg, k=10, T=0.8)),
        ("Top-p=0.5, T=0.8", lambda lg: sample_top_p(lg, p=0.5, T=0.8)),
        ("Top-p=0.95, T=0.8", lambda lg: sample_top_p(lg, p=0.95, T=0.8)),
        ("Min-p=0.1, T=0.8", lambda lg: sample_min_p(lg, min_p=0.1, T=0.8)),
        ("Min-p=0.01, T=0.8", lambda lg: sample_min_p(lg, min_p=0.01, T=0.8)),
    ]

    for label, sampler in strategies:
        # Reset RNG for reproducibility within each strategy
        torch.manual_seed(42)
        names = generate(model, sampler, n=20)
        print(f"\n--- {label} ---")
        for i, name in enumerate(names):
            print(f"  {i + 1:2d}. {name}")

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Visualization: probability distributions after applying each filter
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
print("\n" + "=" * 60)
print("PROBABILITY DISTRIBUTION VISUALIZATION")
print("=" * 60)

# Find a good example prefix by encoding "mar"
prefix_str = "mar"
prefix_tokens = [BOS] + [uchars.index(ch) for ch in prefix_str]

with torch.no_grad():
    idx = torch.tensor([prefix_tokens], device=device)
    logits = model(idx)
    raw_logits = logits[0, -1]  # logits for next token after "mar"

    def fmt_dist(probs, top_n=10):
        """Format the top-n probabilities as a readable string."""
        vals, idxs = torch.topk(probs, min(top_n, (probs > 0).sum().item()))
        parts = []
        for v, i in zip(vals, idxs):
            tok = uchars[i.item()] if i.item() < len(uchars) else "<BOS>"
            parts.append(f"{tok}={v.item():.2f}")
        n_active = (probs > 0).sum().item()
        return "  ".join(parts) + f"  ({n_active} tokens)"

    print(f'\nToken probabilities after "{prefix_str}" (top 10):')

    # Raw distribution (T=1.0)
    raw_probs = F.softmax(raw_logits, dim=-1)
    print(f"  Raw (T=1.0):  {fmt_dist(raw_probs)}")

    # Temperature 0.3
    t03_probs = F.softmax(raw_logits / 0.3, dim=-1)
    print(f"  T=0.3:        {fmt_dist(t03_probs)}")

    # Temperature 2.0
    t20_probs = F.softmax(raw_logits / 2.0, dim=-1)
    print(f"  T=2.0:        {fmt_dist(t20_probs)}")

    # Top-k=3
    topk_logits = raw_logits.clone() / 0.8
    topk_vals, _ = torch.topk(topk_logits, 3)
    topk_logits[topk_logits < topk_vals[-1]] = float("-inf")
    topk_probs = F.softmax(topk_logits, dim=-1)
    print(f"  Top-k=3:      {fmt_dist(topk_probs)}")

    # Top-p=0.9
    topp_probs = F.softmax(raw_logits / 0.8, dim=-1)
    sorted_probs, sorted_idx = torch.sort(topp_probs, descending=True)
    cumsum = torch.cumsum(sorted_probs, dim=-1)
    mask = cumsum - sorted_probs > 0.9
    sorted_probs[mask] = 0.0
    sorted_probs /= sorted_probs.sum()
    topp_out = torch.zeros_like(topp_probs)
    topp_out.scatter_(0, sorted_idx, sorted_probs)
    print(f"  Top-p=0.9:    {fmt_dist(topp_out)}")

    # Min-p=0.1
    minp_probs = F.softmax(raw_logits / 0.8, dim=-1)
    threshold = 0.1 * minp_probs.max()
    minp_probs[minp_probs < threshold] = 0.0
    minp_probs /= minp_probs.sum()
    print(f"  Min-p=0.1:    {fmt_dist(minp_probs)}")

print()

10.text: Import math, os, and random from the standard library for attention scaling and data loading.

11.text: Import os for building portable file paths to the dataset.

12.text: Import random for shuffling the dataset and seeding reproducibility.

16.text: Import PyTorch's functional API for operations like softmax, cross-entropy, and relu.

19.text: Set the PyTorch random seed for reproducible weight initialization and sampling.

24.text: Construct a portable file path to the shared dataset, relative to this script's location, so the code works regardless of where you clone the repo.

25.text: Download the dataset from the internet if it is not already on disk.

31.text: Read all names from the dataset file, one per line, stripping whitespace.

32.text: Randomize the training order so the model does not memorize sequences of names.

34.text: Build a sorted list of unique characters in the dataset to form the token vocabulary.

35.text: Use the length of the character set as the beginning-of-sequence token ID.

36.text: The full vocabulary has 27 tokens: 26 lowercase letters plus the BOS marker.

42.text: Same model config as lab 03, the model is identical, only the sampling changes.

43.text: Four parallel attention heads, each processing a different 4-dimensional slice of the embedding.

44.text: A single transformer layer is enough for this small character-level model.

45.text: Maximum context window of 16 tokens, which comfortably fits the longest names in the dataset.

46.text: Each attention head operates on a 4-dimensional slice of the 16-dimensional embedding.

49.text: Root-mean-square normalization that stabilizes activations without learned parameters.

55.text: Divide by the root-mean-square of the activation vector. Simpler than LayerNorm since it skips mean subtraction, but equally effective for stabilizing training.

58.text: Standard causal self-attention, identical to lab 03.

62.text: Linear projection that transforms embeddings into key vectors for attention.

63.text: Linear projection that transforms embeddings into value vectors for attention.

64.text: Project the concatenated multi-head output back to the model dimension.

66.text: Run the attention computation: project to Q, K, V, apply the causal mask, and combine.

67.text: Extract batch size, sequence length, and embedding dimension from the input tensor shape.

68.text: Project and reshape queries into heads.

69.text: Project and reshape the input into key vectors, one per attention head.

70.text: Project and reshape the input into value vectors, one per attention head.

72.text: Compute scaled dot-product attention scores between every pair of query and key vectors.

73.text: Build an upper-triangular boolean mask so each token can only attend to itself and earlier positions.

74.text: Replace future-position scores with negative infinity so softmax assigns them zero weight.

75.text: Convert attention scores into a probability distribution that sums to one over the key dimension.

77.text: Multiply attention weights by value vectors and reshape back to the original embedding layout.

78.text: Project the concatenated multi-head output back to the model dimension through a linear layer.

81.text: A standard two-layer feed-forward network with ReLU activation in between.

84.text: Project each token's embedding into a 4x wider hidden space for richer computation.

85.text: Compress the expanded hidden representation back down to the original embedding dimension.

87.text: Run the input through the two-layer MLP with ReLU activation in between.

88.text: Two linear layers with ReLU in between. The expansion to 4x width gives the network more room to learn nonlinear transformations before compressing back down.

91.text: One transformer block containing self-attention and a feed-forward network with residual connections.

94.text: Normalize activations before the attention sublayer to stabilize training gradients.

95.text: The causal self-attention sublayer that lets each token attend to previous positions.

96.text: Normalize activations before the MLP sublayer to stabilize training gradients.

97.text: The feed-forward network that processes each token independently after attention has mixed information across positions.

99.text: Apply attention and MLP sublayers with residual connections that let gradients flow easily.

100.text: Add the attention output to the residual stream, preserving the original information.

101.text: Add the MLP output to the residual stream so the model can refine representations incrementally.

105.text: The full transformer model, identical to lab 03, serving as the shared backbone for all sampling strategies.

108.text: Look up a learned embedding vector for each input token to represent its identity.

109.text: Learned positional embeddings that tell the model where each token sits in the sequence.

110.text: Normalize the combined token-plus-position embedding before feeding it into the transformer.

111.text: A stack of transformer blocks, each containing attention and MLP sublayers.

112.text: Map the final hidden states to vocabulary-sized logits for predicting the next token.

113.text: Apply the custom weight initialization function to every module in the model.

117.text: Draw initial weights from a narrow Gaussian so activations stay reasonably scaled at the start.

120.text: Run the full model on the input sequence to produce next-token logits for every position.

121.text: Extract the batch size and sequence length from the input tensor shape.

122.text: Look up a learned embedding vector for each input token to represent its identity.

123.text: A learned vector for each position in the sequence. Added to token embeddings so the model knows where each token sits, since attention alone is position-agnostic.

124.text: Add token and position embeddings together, then normalize before entering the transformer.

125.text: Pass the normalized representation through each transformer block in sequence.

127.text: Map the final hidden states to vocabulary-sized logits for predicting the next token.

133.text: Use CPU for this tiny model since it trains in seconds without a GPU.

134.text: Instantiate the model with the same architecture as lab 03. The same trained weights will be used by every sampling strategy, isolating the effect of the sampler.

135.text: Display the total parameter count to verify the model size before training.

137.text: Set up the Adam optimizer with momentum and epsilon tuned for this small model.

138.text: Train for 1000 steps, enough for this tiny model to learn basic character-level name patterns.

140.text: Standard training loop, identical to lab 03.

141.text: Pick the next training document by cycling through the shuffled name list.

142.text: Encode each character in the name as a token ID, bookended by BOS markers.

143.text: Limit the sequence length to block_size so it fits inside the model's positional embedding table.

145.text: Convert the first n token IDs into a tensor the model can consume as input.

146.text: Build the target sequence by shifting the input one position to the right for next-token prediction.

148.text: Run the full model on the current input batch to get next-token predictions.

149.text: Measure how far the model's predictions are from the true next tokens using cross-entropy loss.

151.text: Clear out any leftover gradients from the previous step so they do not accumulate.

152.text: Compute gradients for every parameter by propagating the loss backward through the graph.

153.text: Linearly reduce the learning rate from its initial value down to zero over the course of training.

154.text: Write the decayed learning rate into every parameter group so the optimizer uses it on the next step.

156.text: Apply the optimizer step, adjusting every parameter in the direction that reduces the loss.

166.text: The simplest possible strategy: always pick the most likely token. Deterministic, produces the same output every time. Safe but boring.

168.text: argmax = highest probability token. No randomness at all.

171.text: Temperature reshapes the probability distribution. Low temperature sharpens it (confident), high temperature flattens it (random). The model weights don't change, you're just adjusting the "confidence knob."

175.text: Divide logits by T before softmax. T=0.5 doubles the logits, making the peak sharper. T=2.0 halves them, spreading probability more evenly.

179.text: Top-k: hard cutoff at exactly k candidates. Simple and effective, but the cutoff is arbitrary, sometimes 5 tokens are plausible, sometimes 50 are.

182.text: Scale the logits by temperature first to reshape the distribution, then mask everything outside the top k. This order matters because temperature changes which tokens are "top."

183.text: Find the k-th highest logit value.

184.text: Everything below the threshold becomes -infinity. Exactly k tokens survive.

185.text: Softmax over only the surviving tokens, they now get all the probability mass.

189.text: Top-p (nucleus sampling): keep the smallest set of tokens whose cumulative probability exceeds p. Adapts to the distribution, peaked distributions get few candidates, flat ones get many.

193.text: Divide logits by temperature and convert to probabilities before sorting. The temperature reshapes the distribution first, which affects which tokens fall inside the nucleus.

194.text: Sort tokens by probability, highest first.

195.text: Cumulative sum: after each token, how much total probability have we accumulated?

197.text: Remove tokens once cumulative probability exceeds p, but always keep the top token. The subtraction ensures the first token that pushes past p is kept.

198.text: Zero out tokens beyond the nucleus.

201.text: Scatter back to original token ordering for sampling.

202.text: Put filtered probabilities back at their original indices.

203.text: Draw one token randomly from the nucleus. Only the tokens inside the cumulative probability threshold compete, so unlikely tokens are excluded naturally.

206.text: Min-p: keep tokens with probability >= min_p * max_prob. Simpler than top-p, naturally adapts: when the model is confident (high max_prob), threshold is high and few tokens pass; when uncertain, threshold drops and more tokens are considered.

210.text: Divide logits by temperature and convert to probabilities. The temperature is applied before min-p filtering to reshape which tokens clear the relative threshold.

211.text: Threshold = min_p fraction of the highest probability. A relative cutoff, not absolute.

212.text: Zero out anything below the threshold.

213.text: Divide by the new total so the surviving probabilities sum back to one after filtering.

214.text: Draw one token from the tokens that survived the min-p filter. Fewer survivors when the model is confident, more when it is uncertain, all handled automatically.

222.text: Shared generation function: same model, different sampler, that's the whole point of this lab.

224.text: Allocate an empty list to collect the generated names. Each strategy fills this list, and we display them side-by-side afterward for easy comparison.

226.text: Begin each generated name with the beginning-of-sequence token so the model starts fresh.

228.text: Feed current sequence through the model.

229.text: Extract the raw logit vector for the last position, which predicts what token comes next.

230.text: Extract the raw logits for the last position, the model's prediction for what comes next.

231.text: Clone so each sampler gets a fresh copy (some modify logits in-place).

232.text: If the model predicts BOS, treat it as an end-of-name signal and stop generating.

234.text: Add the newly sampled token to the growing sequence and move on to the next position.

235.text: Convert the list of generated token IDs back into characters and join them into a readable name string.

247.text: Switch to evaluation mode so the model produces consistent outputs without training noise.

248.text: Disable gradient tracking since we only need forward passes for generation, not training.

249.text: Ten sampling strategies, all using the exact same trained model.

250.text: Greedy: deterministic, always the most likely name. Same output every time.

251.text: T=0.3: nearly greedy but with a tiny bit of randomness. Very conservative names.

252.text: T=1.0: the model's own learned distribution, unmodified. The "honest" output.

253.text: T=2.0: chaotic, flattens probabilities so rare tokens get sampled often. Produces gibberish.

254.text: Top-k=3 with T=0.8: only the 3 most likely tokens compete. Simple but rigid.

255.text: Top-k=10 with T=0.8: 10 candidates. More variety than k=3.

256.text: Top-p=0.5 with T=0.8: tight nucleus, keeps just enough tokens to cover 50% of probability mass.

257.text: Top-p=0.95 with T=0.8: loose nucleus, almost everything gets a chance.

258.text: Min-p=0.1 with T=0.8: keep tokens with >= 10% of the top token's probability. Adapts automatically.

259.text: Min-p=0.01 with T=0.8: very permissive, most tokens pass the threshold.

262.text: Loop through all ten sampling configurations, generate 20 names with each, and print the results. Same model throughout, so any differences come purely from the sampler.

264.text: Reset RNG for each strategy so they all start from the same random state.

278.text: Visualize how each strategy reshapes the probability distribution for a specific context.

279.text: Encode "mar" to see what the model predicts next, likely 'i', 'y', 'a', etc.

283.text: Run the model on "mar" to get raw logits for the next token.

284.text: Logits for the next token after "mar".

286.text: Helper to format the top probabilities as a readable string.

288.text: Show the top tokens and their probabilities, plus how many tokens have non-zero probability.

299.text: Raw model distribution at T=1.0, the model's unfiltered opinion.

303.text: T=0.3: the distribution becomes very peaked, the top token dominates.

307.text: T=2.0: the distribution flattens out, even unlikely tokens get noticeable probability.

311.text: Top-k=3: exactly 3 tokens survive, rest are zero. Hard cutoff.

318.text: Top-p=0.9: keep tokens until cumulative probability hits 90%. Variable number of candidates.

329.text: Min-p=0.1: keep tokens with >= 10% of the max probability. Naturally adaptive.

title: LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation)
tagline: Freeze the model, inject tiny low-rank matrices, and train only those. The base model never changes.
code:
"""
microGPT with LoRA — parameter-efficient fine-tuning.

Pre-trains a standard microGPT on all names, then fine-tunes with LoRA
(Low-Rank Adaptation) on a filtered subset. Only the small LoRA matrices
are trained — the base model stays frozen. Demonstrates:
  - Freezing base weights and injecting low-rank adapters
  - Training <5% of parameters to shift the output distribution
  - Merging LoRA weights back into the base model (zero runtime overhead)
"""

import copy
import math
import os
import random

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F

random.seed(42)
torch.manual_seed(42)

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Dataset
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
input_path = os.path.join(os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__)), "..", "data", "input.txt")
if not os.path.exists(input_path):
    import urllib.request

    url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/karpathy/makemore/refs/heads/master/names.txt"
    urllib.request.urlretrieve(url, input_path)

docs = [l.strip() for l in open(input_path).read().strip().split("\n") if l.strip()]
random.shuffle(docs)
uchars = sorted(set("".join(docs)))
BOS = len(uchars)
vocab_size = len(uchars) + 1
print(f"num docs: {len(docs)}")
print(f"vocab size: {vocab_size}")

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# LoRA Linear layer
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------


class LoRALinear(nn.Module):
    """Wraps an existing nn.Linear with a low-rank adaptation.

    The base weight is frozen. Only lora_A and lora_B are trainable.
    B is zero-initialized so the adapter starts as a no-op (W + 0).
    """

    def __init__(self, base_linear, rank=4):
        super().__init__()
        self.base = base_linear
        self.base.weight.requires_grad_(False)  # freeze base
        d_out, d_in = base_linear.weight.shape
        self.lora_A = nn.Parameter(torch.randn(rank, d_in) * 0.01)
        self.lora_B = nn.Parameter(torch.zeros(d_out, rank))  # init B to zero

    def forward(self, x):
        # base output + low-rank adaptation
        # W*x + B*A*x  (B is zero-init, so LoRA starts as no-op)
        return self.base(x) + (x @ self.lora_A.T) @ self.lora_B.T


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Model (identical to lab 03)
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
n_embd = 16     # embedding dimension
n_head = 4      # number of attention heads
n_layer = 1     # number of layers
block_size = 16 # maximum sequence length
head_dim = n_embd // n_head # dimension of each head


class RMSNorm(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, _dim, eps=1e-5):
        super().__init__()
        self.eps = eps

    def forward(self, x):
        return x * torch.rsqrt(x.pow(2).mean(-1, keepdim=True) + self.eps)


class CausalSelfAttention(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.wq = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)
        self.wk = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)
        self.wv = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)
        self.wo = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)

    def forward(self, x):
        B, T, C = x.shape
        q = self.wq(x).view(B, T, n_head, head_dim).transpose(1, 2)
        k = self.wk(x).view(B, T, n_head, head_dim).transpose(1, 2)
        v = self.wv(x).view(B, T, n_head, head_dim).transpose(1, 2)

        att = (q @ k.transpose(-2, -1)) / math.sqrt(head_dim)
        mask = torch.triu(torch.ones(T, T, device=x.device), diagonal=1).bool()
        att = att.masked_fill(mask, float("-inf"))
        att = F.softmax(att, dim=-1)

        out = (att @ v).transpose(1, 2).reshape(B, T, C)
        return self.wo(out)


class MLP(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.fc1 = nn.Linear(n_embd, 4 * n_embd, bias=False)
        self.fc2 = nn.Linear(4 * n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)

    def forward(self, x):
        return self.fc2(F.relu(self.fc1(x)))


class Block(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.norm1 = RMSNorm(n_embd)
        self.attn = CausalSelfAttention()
        self.norm2 = RMSNorm(n_embd)
        self.mlp = MLP()

    def forward(self, x):
        x = x + self.attn(self.norm1(x))
        x = x + self.mlp(self.norm2(x))
        return x


class MicroGPT(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.wte = nn.Embedding(vocab_size, n_embd)
        self.wpe = nn.Embedding(block_size, n_embd)
        self.norm_in = RMSNorm(n_embd)
        self.layers = nn.ModuleList([Block() for _ in range(n_layer)])
        self.lm_head = nn.Linear(n_embd, vocab_size, bias=False)
        self.apply(self._init_weights)

    @staticmethod
    def _init_weights(module):
        if isinstance(module, (nn.Linear, nn.Embedding)):
            nn.init.normal_(module.weight, mean=0.0, std=0.08)

    def forward(self, idx):
        B, T = idx.shape
        tok_emb = self.wte(idx)
        pos_emb = self.wpe(torch.arange(T, device=idx.device))
        x = self.norm_in(tok_emb + pos_emb)
        for layer in self.layers:
            x = layer(x)
        return self.lm_head(x)


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Helpers
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
device = "cpu"
temperature = 0.5 # in (0, 1], control the "creativity" of generated text, low to high


def generate(model, n_samples=20, label="sample"):
    """Generate names from the model."""
    model.eval()
    names = []
    with torch.no_grad():
        for i in range(n_samples):
            tokens = [BOS]
            for _ in range(block_size):
                idx = torch.tensor([tokens[-block_size:]], device=device)
                logits = model(idx)
                logits = logits[0, -1] / temperature
                probs = F.softmax(logits, dim=-1)
                token_id = torch.multinomial(probs, 1).item()
                if token_id == BOS:
                    break
                tokens.append(token_id)
            name = "".join(uchars[t] for t in tokens[1:])
            names.append(name)
            print(f"  {label} {i + 1:2d}: {name}")
    model.train()
    return names


def train_step(model, optimizer, doc, step, num_steps, base_lr=1e-2):
    """Single training step (shared between pre-training and fine-tuning)."""
    tokens = [BOS] + [uchars.index(ch) for ch in doc] + [BOS]
    n = min(block_size, len(tokens) - 1)
    input_ids = torch.tensor([tokens[:n]], device=device)
    targets = torch.tensor([tokens[1 : n + 1]], device=device)

    logits = model(input_ids)
    loss = F.cross_entropy(logits.view(-1, vocab_size), targets.view(-1))

    optimizer.zero_grad()
    loss.backward()
    lr_t = base_lr * (1 - step / num_steps)
    for pg in optimizer.param_groups:
        pg["lr"] = lr_t
    optimizer.step()
    return loss.item()


# ===========================================================================
# Phase 1: Pre-train base model on ALL names
# ===========================================================================
print("\n" + "=" * 60)
print("PHASE 1: Pre-training base model on all names")
print("=" * 60)

model = MicroGPT().to(device)
total_params = sum(p.numel() for p in model.parameters())
print(f"total params: {total_params}")

optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=1e-2, betas=(0.85, 0.99), eps=1e-8)
num_steps = 1000

for step in range(num_steps):
    doc = docs[step % len(docs)]
    loss = train_step(model, optimizer, doc, step, num_steps)
    if (step + 1) % 200 == 0 or step == 0:
        print(f"  step {step + 1:4d} / {num_steps} | loss {loss:.4f}")

print("\nBaseline generation (pre-trained on all names):")
base_names = generate(model, n_samples=10, label="base")

# Save base model state for later comparison
base_state = copy.deepcopy(model.state_dict())

# ===========================================================================
# Phase 2: Create fine-tuning target — names starting with "m"
# ===========================================================================
target_letter = "m"
ft_docs = [d for d in docs if d.lower().startswith(target_letter)]
print(f"\n{'=' * 60}")
print(f"PHASE 2: Fine-tuning target — names starting with '{target_letter}'")
print(f"{'=' * 60}")
print(f"filtered dataset: {len(ft_docs)} names (out of {len(docs)} total)")

# ===========================================================================
# Phase 3: Apply LoRA — replace Linear layers with LoRALinear wrappers
# ===========================================================================
print(f"\n{'=' * 60}")
print("PHASE 3: Injecting LoRA adapters")
print("=" * 60)

lora_rank = 4


def inject_lora(module, rank=4):
    """Replace all nn.Linear layers with LoRALinear wrappers (recursively)."""
    for name, child in module.named_children():
        if isinstance(child, nn.Linear):
            setattr(module, name, LoRALinear(child, rank=rank))
        else:
            inject_lora(child, rank=rank)


# Apply LoRA to attention and MLP layers (not embeddings or lm_head)
for layer in model.layers:
    inject_lora(layer, rank=lora_rank)

total_params = sum(p.numel() for p in model.parameters())
trainable_params = sum(p.numel() for p in model.parameters() if p.requires_grad)
frozen_params = total_params - trainable_params
pct = 100.0 * trainable_params / total_params

print(f"total params:     {total_params}")
print(f"frozen params:    {frozen_params}")
print(f"trainable params: {trainable_params} ({pct:.1f}% of total)")

# ===========================================================================
# Phase 4: Fine-tune with LoRA on filtered subset
# ===========================================================================
print(f"\n{'=' * 60}")
print(f"PHASE 4: Fine-tuning with LoRA on '{target_letter}' names")
print("=" * 60)

# Only optimize LoRA parameters (the ones that require grad)
lora_params = [p for p in model.parameters() if p.requires_grad]
optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(lora_params, lr=1e-2, betas=(0.85, 0.99), eps=1e-8)
ft_steps = 500

for step in range(ft_steps):
    doc = ft_docs[step % len(ft_docs)]
    loss = train_step(model, optimizer, doc, step, ft_steps)
    if (step + 1) % 100 == 0 or step == 0:
        print(f"  step {step + 1:4d} / {ft_steps} | loss {loss:.4f}")

# ===========================================================================
# Phase 5: Compare results — base vs LoRA-adapted
# ===========================================================================
print(f"\n{'=' * 60}")
print("PHASE 5: Comparing base vs LoRA-adapted generation")
print("=" * 60)

# Generate from LoRA-adapted model
print(f"\nLoRA-adapted model (fine-tuned on '{target_letter}' names):")
torch.manual_seed(42)
lora_names = generate(model, n_samples=20, label="lora")

# Reload base model (without LoRA) for comparison
base_model = MicroGPT().to(device)
base_model.load_state_dict(base_state)
print("\nBase model (no fine-tuning):")
torch.manual_seed(42)
base_only_names = generate(base_model, n_samples=20, label="base")

# Measure distribution shift
lora_m_count = sum(1 for n in lora_names if n.lower().startswith(target_letter))
base_m_count = sum(1 for n in base_only_names if n.lower().startswith(target_letter))

print("\n--- Distribution shift ---")
print(f"  Base model:  {base_m_count}/20 names start with '{target_letter}'")
print(f"  LoRA model:  {lora_m_count}/20 names start with '{target_letter}'")
print(f"\n  Fine-tuned {trainable_params} params ({pct:.1f}% of {total_params}) to shift the distribution")

# ===========================================================================
# Phase 6: LoRA merge — fold adapters back into base weights
# ===========================================================================
print(f"\n{'=' * 60}")
print("PHASE 6: Merging LoRA weights into base model")
print("=" * 60)


def merge_lora(module):
    """Merge LoRA weights back: W_new = W_base + B @ A."""
    for name, child in module.named_children():
        if isinstance(child, LoRALinear):
            merged = nn.Linear(
                child.base.weight.shape[1],
                child.base.weight.shape[0],
                bias=False,
            )
            # W_new = W_base + B @ A
            merged.weight = nn.Parameter(child.base.weight + child.lora_B @ child.lora_A)
            setattr(module, name, merged)
        else:
            merge_lora(child)


merge_lora(model)

print("LoRA adapters merged into base weights (no runtime overhead)")

# Verify merged model produces identical output
print("\nMerged model generation (should match LoRA model exactly):")
torch.manual_seed(42)
merged_names = generate(model, n_samples=20, label="merged")

match = all(a == b for a, b in zip(lora_names, merged_names))
print(f"\nMerged output matches LoRA output: {match}")

merged_params = sum(p.numel() for p in model.parameters())
print(f"Merged model params: {merged_params} (same as original base model)")

11.text: copy for deep-copying the base model state before LoRA injection.

12.text: We need math.sqrt for scaling attention scores. Dividing by the square root of head dimension keeps dot products from growing too large as dimensions increase.

13.text: Import os for building portable file paths to the dataset.

14.text: Import random for shuffling the dataset and seeding reproducibility.

16.text: Import the core PyTorch library for tensor operations and autograd.

17.text: Neural network building blocks like Linear, Embedding, and ModuleList that automatically register their parameters for training.

18.text: Import PyTorch's functional API for operations like softmax, cross-entropy, and relu.

20.text: Fix both the Python and PyTorch random seeds so results are reproducible across runs.

21.text: Set the PyTorch random seed for reproducible weight initialization and sampling.

27.text: Build a portable file path to the dataset, relative to this script's location.

28.text: Download the dataset from the internet if it is not already on disk.

34.text: Read all names from the dataset file, one per line, stripping whitespace.

35.text: Randomize the training order so the model does not memorize sequences of names.

36.text: Build a sorted list of unique characters in the dataset to form the token vocabulary.

37.text: Use the length of the character set as the beginning-of-sequence token ID.

38.text: The full vocabulary has 27 tokens: 26 lowercase letters plus the BOS marker.

47.text: The LoRA wrapper: the key innovation of this lab. Wraps an existing nn.Linear and adds two tiny matrices that learn to "steer" the original layer's output.

54.text: Takes an existing trained linear layer and wraps it with low-rank adapters.

56.text: Detach the base weight from the model. The frozen base weight holds the pre-trained knowledge.

57.text: Freeze the base weight, its requires_grad is now False. During fine-tuning, no gradients flow here.

58.text: Read the dimensions from the existing weight matrix.

59.text: lora_A: the "down-projection" (rank x d_in). Random init, this matrix learns to read important features from the input. Small random values to break symmetry.

60.text: lora_B: the "up-projection" (d_out x rank). Zero init is crucial, it makes the adapter a no-op at the start. The model behaves exactly like the base model until training begins to shift B away from zero.

62.text: Forward: base output + adapter output. Two paths, summed.

65.text: The math: W*x + B*A*x. Since B starts at zero, the adapter contributes nothing initially. As B learns non-zero values, the adapter gently steers the output. With rank=4, A is (4, d_in) and B is (d_out, 4), tiny compared to W's (d_out, d_in).

72.text: Four parallel attention heads, each processing a different 4-dimensional slice of the embedding.

73.text: A single transformer layer is enough for this small character-level model.

74.text: Maximum context window of 16 tokens, which comfortably fits the longest names.

75.text: Each attention head operates on a 4-dimensional slice of the 16-dimensional embedding.

78.text: Root-mean-square normalization that stabilizes activations without learned parameters.

84.text: Normalize by root-mean-square to keep activations stable without learned scale parameters.

87.text: Standard causal attention, identical to lab 03.

90.text: Q, K, V, output projections. These will be wrapped with LoRA later.

91.text: Linear projection that transforms embeddings into key vectors for attention.

92.text: Linear projection that transforms embeddings into value vectors for attention.

93.text: Project the concatenated multi-head output back to the model dimension.

96.text: Extract batch size, sequence length, and embedding dimension from the input tensor shape.

97.text: Project and reshape the input into query vectors, one per attention head.

98.text: Project and reshape the input into key vectors, one per attention head.

99.text: Project and reshape the input into value vectors, one per attention head.

101.text: Compute scaled dot-product attention scores between every pair of query and key vectors.

102.text: Build an upper-triangular boolean mask so each token can only attend to itself and earlier positions.

103.text: Replace future-position scores with negative infinity so softmax assigns them zero weight.

104.text: Convert attention scores into a probability distribution that sums to one over the key dimension.

106.text: Multiply attention weights by value vectors and reshape back to the original embedding layout.

107.text: Project the concatenated multi-head output back to the model dimension through a linear layer.

110.text: A standard two-layer feed-forward network with ReLU activation in between.

113.text: Project each token's embedding into a 4x wider hidden space for richer computation.

114.text: Compress the expanded hidden representation back down to the original embedding dimension.

116.text: Run the input through the two-layer feed-forward network with ReLU activation.

117.text: Run the two-layer MLP with ReLU, producing the feed-forward output for this position.

120.text: One transformer block containing self-attention and a feed-forward network with residual connections.

123.text: Normalize activations before the attention sublayer to stabilize training gradients.

124.text: The causal self-attention sublayer that lets each token attend to previous positions.

125.text: Normalize activations before the MLP sublayer to stabilize training gradients.

126.text: The feed-forward network that processes each token independently after attention has mixed information across positions.

128.text: Apply attention and MLP sublayers with residual connections that let gradients flow easily.

129.text: Add the attention output to the residual stream, preserving the original information.

130.text: Add the MLP output to the residual stream so the model can refine representations incrementally.

134.text: Full model, identical to lab 03 for pre-training.

137.text: Look up a learned embedding vector for each input token to represent its identity.

138.text: Learned positional embeddings that tell the model where each token sits in the sequence.

139.text: Normalize the combined token-plus-position embedding before feeding it into the transformer.

140.text: A stack of transformer blocks, each containing attention and MLP sublayers.

141.text: Map the final hidden states to vocabulary-sized logits for predicting the next token.

142.text: Apply the custom weight initialization function to every module in the model.

146.text: Draw initial weights from a narrow Gaussian (std=0.08) so activations start at a reasonable scale, neither exploding nor vanishing through the layers.

149.text: Run the full model on the input sequence to produce next-token logits for every position.

150.text: Extract the batch size and sequence length from the input tensor shape.

151.text: Look up a learned embedding vector for each input token to represent its identity.

152.text: Create a position embedding for each slot in the sequence so the model knows token ordering.

153.text: Add token and position embeddings together, then normalize before entering the transformer.

154.text: Pass the normalized representation through each transformer block in sequence.

156.text: Map the final hidden states to vocabulary-sized logits for predicting the next token.

162.text: Use CPU for this tiny model since it trains in seconds without a GPU.

163.text: Set the sampling temperature to 0.5 for moderately conservative name generation.

166.text: Generate names from the model (used for both base and LoRA-adapted models).

168.text: Switch to evaluation mode so the model produces consistent outputs during generation.

170.text: Disable gradient tracking since we only need forward passes for generation, not training.

172.text: Begin each generated name with the beginning-of-sequence token so the model starts fresh.

174.text: Feed the current token sequence through the model to get next-token logits. During generation we re-run the full sequence at each step since there is no KV cache.

175.text: Extract the raw logit vector for the last position, which predicts what token comes next.

176.text: Divide the logits by the temperature before softmax to control how peaked the distribution is.

177.text: Convert the temperature-scaled logits into a proper probability distribution that sums to one.

178.text: Randomly draw one token from the probability distribution to introduce controlled diversity.

179.text: If the model predicts BOS, treat it as an end-of-name signal and stop generating.

181.text: Add the newly sampled token to the growing sequence and continue generating the next one.

182.text: Convert the list of generated token IDs back into characters and join them into a readable name string.

189.text: Shared training step, used for both pre-training and LoRA fine-tuning.

191.text: Encode each character in the name as a token ID, bookended by BOS markers.

192.text: Limit the sequence length to block_size so it fits the model's positional embedding table.

193.text: Convert the token IDs into a tensor the model can consume as its input sequence.

194.text: Build the target sequence by shifting the input one position to the right for next-token prediction.

196.text: Run the full model on the current input batch to get next-token predictions.

197.text: Measure how far the model's predictions are from the true next tokens using cross-entropy loss.

199.text: Clear out any leftover gradients from the previous step so they do not accumulate.

200.text: Backprop, during LoRA fine-tuning, gradients only flow into the unfrozen LoRA parameters.

201.text: Linearly reduce the learning rate from its initial value down to zero over the course of training.

203.text: Write the decayed learning rate into every parameter group so the optimizer uses it on the next step.

204.text: Update, only LoRA parameters move because base weights are frozen.

215.text: Instantiate the base model that will first be pre-trained on all names. Later, we will freeze these weights and inject LoRA adapters for fine-tuning.

220.text: Train for 1000 steps of standard next-token prediction on the full names corpus. This gives the model a solid foundation before we specialize it with LoRA.

222.text: Pre-training loop, standard next-token prediction on the full names corpus.

223.text: Pick the next name from the shuffled list, wrapping around when we reach the end. This cycling ensures continuous training across all 1000 steps.

224.text: Run one training step on the current document, returning the loss value.

229.text: Generate some baseline names to see the pre-trained model's behavior.

232.text: Save the pre-trained state so we can compare with LoRA later. Deep copy because LoRA will modify the model in-place.

237.text: Choose a fine-tuning target: names starting with 'm'. LoRA will shift the distribution toward these.

238.text: Filter the dataset to only 'm' names.

251.text: Rank 4: the adapter matrices are (4 x d_in) and (d_out x 4). Tiny compared to the full weight matrices.

254.text: Recursively walk the model and wrap every nn.Linear with LoRALinear.

256.text: Find linear layers in the module tree.

257.text: Found one, replace it with a LoRA wrapper.

258.text: Swap the original linear layer for the LoRA-wrapped version in-place.

260.text: Recurse into child modules to find nested linear layers.

264.text: Only inject LoRA into the transformer layers, not embeddings or lm_head. Embedding layers don't benefit from LoRA at this scale.

265.text: Apply LoRA to each transformer layer's attention and MLP sublayers.

267.text: Recount parameters, total grows slightly because of the LoRA matrices.

268.text: Count trainable parameters, only the LoRA A and B matrices have requires_grad=True.

270.text: What percentage of parameters are trainable? Expect ~5-15% depending on architecture.

284.text: Only feed LoRA parameters to the optimizer, the frozen base weights are excluded.

285.text: Fresh optimizer for fine-tuning, only LoRA parameters.

286.text: 500 fine-tuning steps, less than pre-training because we're making a small adjustment.

288.text: Fine-tuning loop, same training step, but on 'm'-filtered data.

290.text: Train step, gradients flow through LoRA A and B only.

303.text: Pin the random seed before generation so the LoRA-adapted model and the base model produce samples from the same random sequence for a fair comparison.

304.text: Generate 20 names from the LoRA-adapted model, expect a strong bias toward 'm' names.

307.text: Reload the original pre-trained weights (without LoRA) for comparison.

308.text: Restore the saved base model state.

311.text: Generate from the base model, these should show the original, unbiased distribution.

314.text: Count 'm' names from the LoRA model, should be substantially higher than base.

315.text: Count 'm' names from the base model, should reflect the natural ~4% rate.

330.text: Merge LoRA: fold the learned adapter weights back into the base weight matrix. After merging, the model is a standard nn.Linear again with zero runtime overhead.

332.text: Walk the module tree looking for LoRA-wrapped layers.

333.text: Found a LoRA layer, merge it back.

334.text: Allocate a standard nn.Linear with the same dimensions as the original layer, ready to receive the merged weight matrix with no adapter overhead.

340.text: The merge: W_new = W_base + B @ A. This is mathematically identical to the LoRA forward pass but baked into a single weight matrix. No adapter overhead at inference time.

341.text: Replace the LoRA wrapper with the merged standard linear layer.

343.text: Descend into child modules to find and merge any LoRA wrappers nested deeper in the model tree, ensuring no adapter layers remain after merging.

346.text: Merge all LoRA layers in the model.

352.text: Same seed, merged model should produce IDENTICAL output to the LoRA model.

355.text: Verify every name matches, merging is exact, not an approximation.

358.text: After merging, parameter count should equal the original base model, LoRA overhead is gone.

359.text: Confirm: same size as the original model, but with the fine-tuned behavior baked in.

title: Text Diffusion
tagline: Names emerge from pure noise through iterative unmasking. Generation without left-to-right decoding.
code:
"""
A masked diffusion language model trained and run in pure, dependency-free Python.
Instead of generating names left-to-right like a GPT, names emerge from pure noise
— all [MASK] tokens — through iterative unmasking. Same autograd, same transformer,
fundamentally different generative paradigm.

Autograd engine and transformer architecture from @karpathy's microGPT.
