Diffusion framework from MDLM (Sahoo et al., 2024) and LLaDA (Nie et al., 2025).
"""

import math  # math.log, math.exp
import os  # os.path.exists
import random  # random.seed, random.choices, random.gauss, random.shuffle

random.seed(42)  # Let there be order among chaos

# Let there be an input dataset `docs`: list[str] of documents (e.g. a dataset of names)
input_path = os.path.join(os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__)), "..", "data", "input.txt")
if not os.path.exists(input_path):
    import urllib.request

    names_url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/karpathy/makemore/refs/heads/master/names.txt"
    urllib.request.urlretrieve(names_url, input_path)
docs = [l.strip() for l in open(input_path).read().strip().split("\n") if l.strip()]  # list[str] of documents
random.shuffle(docs)
print(f"num docs: {len(docs)}")

# Let there be a Tokenizer — same characters, but MASK replaces BOS and PAD handles fixed-length sequences
uchars = sorted(set("".join(docs)))  # unique characters in the dataset become token ids 0..n-1
MASK = len(uchars)  # token id for [MASK] — the "noise" state that the model learns to denoise
PAD = len(uchars) + 1  # token id for [PAD] — fills unused positions in fixed-length sequences
vocab_size = len(uchars) + 2  # total number of unique tokens
print(f"vocab size: {vocab_size}")


# Let there be Autograd, to recursively apply the chain rule through a computation graph
class Value:
    __slots__ = ('data', 'grad', '_children', '_local_grads') # Python optimization for memory usage

    def __init__(self, data, children=(), local_grads=()):
        self.data = data                # scalar value of this node calculated during forward pass
        self.grad = 0                   # derivative of the loss w.r.t. this node, calculated in backward pass
        self._children = children       # children of this node in the computation graph
        self._local_grads = local_grads # local derivative of this node w.r.t. its children

    def __add__(self, other):
        other = other if isinstance(other, Value) else Value(other)
        return Value(self.data + other.data, (self, other), (1, 1))

    def __mul__(self, other):
        other = other if isinstance(other, Value) else Value(other)
        return Value(self.data * other.data, (self, other), (other.data, self.data))

    def __pow__(self, other): return Value(self.data**other, (self,), (other * self.data**(other-1),))
    def log(self): return Value(math.log(self.data), (self,), (1/self.data,))
    def exp(self): return Value(math.exp(self.data), (self,), (math.exp(self.data),))
    def relu(self): return Value(max(0, self.data), (self,), (float(self.data > 0),))
    def __neg__(self): return self * -1
    def __radd__(self, other): return self + other
    def __sub__(self, other): return self + (-other)
    def __rsub__(self, other): return other + (-self)
    def __rmul__(self, other): return self * other
    def __truediv__(self, other): return self * other**-1
    def __rtruediv__(self, other): return other * self**-1

    def backward(self):
        topo = []
        visited = set()
        def build_topo(v):
            if v not in visited:
                visited.add(v)
                for child in v._children:
                    build_topo(child)
                topo.append(v)
        build_topo(self)
        self.grad = 1
        for v in reversed(topo):
            for child, local_grad in zip(v._children, v._local_grads):
                child.grad += local_grad * v.grad


# Initialize the parameters, to store the knowledge of the model.
n_embd = 16  # embedding dimension
n_head = 4  # number of attention heads
n_layer = 2  # number of layers (diffusion needs depth to gather scattered clues)
block_size = 16  # maximum sequence length (names are padded/truncated to this)
head_dim = n_embd // n_head  # dimension of each head


def matrix(nout, nin, std=0.08):
    return [[Value(random.gauss(0, std)) for _ in range(nin)] for _ in range(nout)]


state_dict = {"wte": matrix(vocab_size, n_embd), "wpe": matrix(block_size, n_embd)}
state_dict["lm_head"] = state_dict["wte"]  # weight tying — same matrix for input embeddings and output projection
for i in range(n_layer):
    state_dict[f"layer{i}.attn_wq"] = matrix(n_embd, n_embd)
    state_dict[f"layer{i}.attn_wk"] = matrix(n_embd, n_embd)
    state_dict[f"layer{i}.attn_wv"] = matrix(n_embd, n_embd)
    state_dict[f"layer{i}.attn_wo"] = matrix(n_embd, n_embd)
    state_dict[f"layer{i}.mlp_fc1"] = matrix(4 * n_embd, n_embd)
    state_dict[f"layer{i}.mlp_fc2"] = matrix(n_embd, 4 * n_embd)
params = [p for mat in state_dict.values() for row in mat for p in row]  # flatten params into a single list[Value]
print(f"num params: {len(params)}")


# Define the model architecture: a bidirectional transformer that predicts clean tokens from masked input.
# Same building blocks as GPT-2 (rmsnorm, multi-head attention, MLP), but every position sees every other.
# No causal mask, no KV cache — the model looks in all directions to fill in the blanks.
def linear(x, w):
    return [sum(wi * xi for wi, xi in zip(wo, x)) for wo in w]


def softmax(logits):
    max_val = max(val.data for val in logits)
    exps = [(val - max_val).exp() for val in logits]
    total = sum(exps)
    return [e / total for e in exps]


def rmsnorm(x):
    ms = sum(xi * xi for xi in x) / len(x)
    scale = (ms + 1e-5) ** -0.5
    return [xi * scale for xi in x]


def mask_predictor(token_ids):
    """Takes a (partially masked) sequence, returns logits for every position."""
    n = len(token_ids)

    # Embed all tokens: what each token is + where it sits
    xs = [
        rmsnorm([t + p for t, p in zip(state_dict["wte"][tid], state_dict["wpe"][pos])])
        for pos, tid in enumerate(token_ids)
    ]

    for li in range(n_layer):
        # 1) Multi-head bidirectional self-attention — every position attends to every other
        residuals = xs
        xs_norm = [rmsnorm(x) for x in xs]
        qs = [linear(x, state_dict[f"layer{li}.attn_wq"]) for x in xs_norm]
        ks = [linear(x, state_dict[f"layer{li}.attn_wk"]) for x in xs_norm]
        vs = [linear(x, state_dict[f"layer{li}.attn_wv"]) for x in xs_norm]
        xs_attn = []
        for i in range(n):
            x_attn = []
            for h in range(n_head):
                hs = h * head_dim
                q_h = qs[i][hs : hs + head_dim]
                attn_logits = [sum(q_h[j] * ks[t][hs + j] for j in range(head_dim)) / head_dim**0.5 for t in range(n)]
                attn_weights = softmax(attn_logits)
                head_out = [sum(attn_weights[t] * vs[t][hs + j] for t in range(n)) for j in range(head_dim)]
                x_attn.extend(head_out)
            x = linear(x_attn, state_dict[f"layer{li}.attn_wo"])
            xs_attn.append([a + b for a, b in zip(x, residuals[i])])
        # 2) MLP block
        xs = []
        for x in xs_attn:
            x_residual = x
            x = rmsnorm(x)
            x = linear(x, state_dict[f"layer{li}.mlp_fc1"])
            x = [xi.relu() for xi in x]
            x = linear(x, state_dict[f"layer{li}.mlp_fc2"])
            xs.append([a + b for a, b in zip(x, x_residual)])

    return [linear(x, state_dict["lm_head"]) for x in xs]  # logits for every position


# Let there be Adam, the blessed optimizer and its buffers
learning_rate, beta1, beta2, eps_adam = 0.01, 0.85, 0.99, 1e-8
m = [0.0] * len(params)  # first moment buffer
v = [0.0] * len(params)  # second moment buffer

# Repeat in sequence — but now with noise instead of next-token prediction
num_steps = 2000  # number of training steps
for step in range(num_steps):
    # Take a single document, tokenize it, pad to fixed length with PAD
    doc = docs[step % len(docs)]
    clean = [uchars.index(ch) for ch in doc] + [PAD] * (block_size - len(doc))
    clean = clean[:block_size]  # truncate if longer than block_size

    # Forward process: corrupt the clean sequence by masking each token with probability t
    # Log-uniform t sampling: t ∝ 1/t, which cancels the 1/t ELBO weight (importance sampling)
    t = math.exp(random.uniform(math.log(0.2), 0))
    noisy = [MASK if random.random() < t else c for c in clean]
    n_masked = sum(1 for x in noisy if x == MASK)
    if n_masked == 0:
        continue  # nothing to learn from if nothing was masked

    # Forward the noisy sequence through the model, compute loss only on masked positions
    all_logits = mask_predictor(noisy)
    losses = []
    for i in range(block_size):
        if noisy[i] == MASK:
            logits_i = all_logits[i][:]  # copy logits for this position
            logits_i[MASK] = logits_i[MASK] - 1e6  # never predict MASK (same as MDLM subs parameterization)
            probs = softmax(logits_i)
            losses.append(-probs[clean[i]].log())
    loss = (1.0 / n_masked) * sum(losses)  # the 1/t cancels — just average cross-entropy. May yours be low.

    # Backward the loss, calculating the gradients with respect to all model parameters.
    loss.backward()

    # Adam optimizer update: update the model parameters based on the corresponding gradients.
    lr_t = learning_rate * (1 - step / num_steps)  # linear learning rate decay
    for i, p in enumerate(params):
        m[i] = beta1 * m[i] + (1 - beta1) * p.grad
        v[i] = beta2 * v[i] + (1 - beta2) * p.grad**2
        m_hat = m[i] / (1 - beta1 ** (step + 1))
        v_hat = v[i] / (1 - beta2 ** (step + 1))
        p.data -= lr_t * m_hat / (v_hat**0.5 + eps_adam)
        p.grad = 0

    if (step + 1) % 10 == 0 or step == 0:
        print(f"step {step + 1:4d} / {num_steps:4d} | loss {loss.data:.4f}")

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Inference — extract weights to plain floats (no autograd overhead)
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
W = {name: [[v.data for v in row] for row in mat] for name, mat in state_dict.items()}


def softmax_plain(logits):
    max_val = max(logits)
    exps = [math.exp(l - max_val) for l in logits]
    total = sum(exps)
    return [e / total for e in exps]


def rmsnorm_plain(x):
    ms = sum(xi * xi for xi in x) / len(x)
    scale = (ms + 1e-5) ** -0.5
    return [xi * scale for xi in x]


def linear_plain(x, w):
    return [sum(wi * xi for wi, xi in zip(wo, x)) for wo in w]


def mask_predictor_plain(token_ids):
    """Same as mask_predictor but on plain floats — no computation graph."""
    n = len(token_ids)
    xs = [rmsnorm_plain([t + p for t, p in zip(W["wte"][tid], W["wpe"][pos])]) for pos, tid in enumerate(token_ids)]
    for li in range(n_layer):
        residuals = xs
        xs_norm = [rmsnorm_plain(x) for x in xs]
        qs = [linear_plain(x, W[f"layer{li}.attn_wq"]) for x in xs_norm]
        ks = [linear_plain(x, W[f"layer{li}.attn_wk"]) for x in xs_norm]
        vs = [linear_plain(x, W[f"layer{li}.attn_wv"]) for x in xs_norm]
        xs_attn = []
        for i in range(n):
            x_attn = []
            for h in range(n_head):
                hs = h * head_dim
                q_h = qs[i][hs : hs + head_dim]
                scores = [sum(q_h[j] * ks[t][hs + j] for j in range(head_dim)) / head_dim**0.5 for t in range(n)]
                weights = softmax_plain(scores)
                head_out = [sum(weights[t] * vs[t][hs + j] for t in range(n)) for j in range(head_dim)]
                x_attn.extend(head_out)
            x = linear_plain(x_attn, W[f"layer{li}.attn_wo"])
            xs_attn.append([a + b for a, b in zip(x, residuals[i])])
        xs = []
        for x in xs_attn:
            x_res = x
            x = rmsnorm_plain(x)
            x = linear_plain(x, W[f"layer{li}.mlp_fc1"])
            x = [max(0.0, xi) for xi in x]
            x = linear_plain(x, W[f"layer{li}.mlp_fc2"])
            xs.append([a + b for a, b in zip(x, x_res)])
    return [linear_plain(x, W["lm_head"]) for x in xs]


# Inference: may names emerge from the noise
num_denoise_steps = 64  # how many steps to go from all-MASK to a clean name
print("\n--- inference (new, hallucinated names) ---")
for sample_idx in range(20):
    seq = [MASK] * block_size  # start from pure noise: every position is [MASK]

    for step_i in range(num_denoise_steps, 0, -1):
        t = math.cos(math.pi / 2 * (1 - step_i / num_denoise_steps))  # cosine schedule: more time in the middle
        s = math.cos(math.pi / 2 * (1 - (step_i - 1) / num_denoise_steps))  # next noise level (less noisy)
        temperature = 0.3 + 0.5 * t  # anneal: explore early (0.8), commit late (0.3)

        # Predict what each masked position should be (plain floats — no autograd needed)
        all_logits = mask_predictor_plain(seq)
        predicted = list(seq)
        confidences = []  # (confidence, position) — how sure the model is about each prediction
        for i in range(block_size):
            if seq[i] == MASK:
                logits_i = list(all_logits[i])
                logits_i[MASK] = logits_i[MASK] - 1e6  # never predict MASK
                probs = softmax_plain([l / temperature for l in logits_i])
                predicted[i] = random.choices(range(vocab_size), weights=probs)[0]
                confidences.append((max(probs), i))

        # Remask: re-mask the least confident predictions (unless this is the final step)
        if s > 0 and confidences:
            n_to_remask = int(len(confidences) * s / t)
            confidences.sort()  # lowest confidence first
            for _, i in confidences[:n_to_remask]:
                predicted[i] = MASK
        seq = predicted

    # Decode: strip PAD tokens and print the name
    name = "".join(uchars[c] for c in seq if c < len(uchars))
    print(f"sample {sample_idx + 1:2d}: {name}")

11.text: Standard library math for log/exp, the noise schedule and loss both live in log-space.

12.text: os for file path handling when loading the dataset.

13.text: random for masking decisions, weight init, sampling, randomness is the soul of diffusion.

15.text: Fixed seed for reproducibility, even though diffusion is inherently stochastic.

18.text: Builds the path to the dataset file relative to this script, one directory up in data/.

20.text: Lazy import: only load urllib if we actually need to download.

22.text: Karpathy's makemore names dataset, 32K human names, perfect for character-level generation.

23.text: Saves the file locally so we only hit the network once. After the first run, training starts instantly from the cached copy.

24.text: Each line becomes one document. Stripping whitespace prevents ghost tokens.

25.text: Shuffling prevents the model from memorizing alphabetical order.

26.text: Quick sanity check: we should see ~32K names.

29.text: Sorted unique characters become our vocabulary, each character gets a deterministic token ID from 0 to n-1.

30.text: MASK is the "noise" token. In diffusion, we corrupt data by replacing real tokens with MASK, then train the model to reverse that corruption. This is the fundamental difference from GPT: noise replaces the BOS token.

31.text: PAD fills unused positions in fixed-length sequences. Unlike GPT which processes variable-length inputs, diffusion needs all sequences padded to the same length because it predicts every position simultaneously.

32.text: Two extra tokens beyond the character set: MASK and PAD.

37.text: The Value class, exact same autograd engine from Lab 01. Each node stores a scalar, its gradient, and how it connects to parent nodes in the computation graph.

38.text: __slots__ tells Python exactly which attributes exist, saving ~40% memory per object. Critical when you have thousands of Values.

40.text: Constructor takes a scalar, optional parent nodes, and local gradients for chain rule.

41.text: The forward-pass value. During training, this flows left to right through the graph.

42.text: Accumulates the gradient during backprop. Starts at zero, filled in by backward().

43.text: Pointers to child nodes, the computation graph is built implicitly as you compute.

44.text: Local derivatives: how much this node changes when each child changes. The chain rule multiplies these.

46.text: Addition operator: creates a new node whose local gradients are both 1 (derivative of a+b w.r.t. both a and b).

47.text: Auto-wrap raw numbers into Value so you can write x + 3 naturally.

48.text: New node: data is the sum, children are the operands, both local grads are 1.

50.text: Multiplication: local grads are the cross terms (product rule).

51.text: Wrap raw Python numbers into Value nodes so you can write x * 3 naturally without explicit conversion.

52.text: Product rule: d(a*b)/da = b, d(a*b)/db = a. Stored as (other.data, self.data).

54.text: Power rule: d(x^n)/dx = n*x^(n-1). Used for inverse, square root, and normalization.

55.text: Log: derivative is 1/x. Essential for cross-entropy loss.

56.text: Exp: its own derivative, the most elegant fact in calculus. Used inside softmax.

57.text: ReLU activation: gradient is 1 if positive, 0 if negative. The simplest nonlinearity.

61.text: Allows writing 3 - value. Python calls this when the left operand doesn't know how to subtract a Value.

62.text: Allows writing 3 * value. Python calls this when the left operand doesn't know how to multiply with a Value.

63.text: Rewrite division as multiplication by the inverse power, reusing the existing __pow__ and __mul__ operators to keep the autograd graph simple.

64.text: Handle the case where a number is divided by a Value, dispatching to the standard division logic.

66.text: Backpropagation: propagate gradients from loss back through the entire computation graph.

67.text: Topological order ensures each node is processed after all nodes that depend on it.

68.text: Track visited nodes to handle shared subexpressions (diamond patterns in the graph).

69.text: Recursive DFS to build topological sort.

70.text: If we've already traversed this node, bail out. Without this check, shared subexpressions would cause exponential blowup in the graph walk.

73.text: Recursive call, goes all the way to leaf nodes (parameters) first.

74.text: Append after children, ensures this node comes after its dependencies in the topo list.

75.text: Start the topological sort from the loss node.

76.text: Seed gradient: dloss/dloss = 1. Everything flows from here.

77.text: Walk the graph in reverse topological order: from loss toward parameters.

78.text: For each child, multiply the local gradient by the parent's gradient (chain rule).

79.text: Accumulate (+=) because a node can be used by multiple parents. This is the chain rule in action.

83.text: 16-dimensional embeddings, small but enough for character-level patterns.

84.text: 4 attention heads, each seeing 4 dimensions of the 16-dim embedding.

85.text: 2 layers instead of 1, diffusion needs more depth because it must gather clues from scattered unmasked positions across the sequence, unlike autoregressive models that always have full left context.

86.text: Fixed sequence length of 16. All names are padded or truncated to fit. Diffusion processes every position in parallel.

87.text: Each head operates on a 4-dimensional slice: 16 / 4 = 4.

90.text: Factory function for creating weight matrices filled with small random Value nodes.

91.text: Each weight is a Value drawn from a Gaussian, small std (0.08) prevents exploding activations at init.

94.text: Token embedding wte and position embedding wpe, the model's input vocabulary.

95.text: Weight tying: the same matrix that embeds input tokens also projects outputs to logits. Halves embedding parameters and creates a consistent token space.

96.text: Allocate the four projection matrices (Q, K, V, output) plus two MLP matrices for each transformer layer, storing them in the state dict by name.

97.text: Query projection: transforms each token's embedding into a vector that asks "what am I looking for?" in attention.

98.text: Key projection: transforms each token into a vector that advertises "here is what I contain" for other tokens to match against.

99.text: Value projection: transforms each token into the actual content that attention will retrieve and blend when a query matches a key.

100.text: Output projection that recombines attention heads.

101.text: MLP first layer: expands from 16 to 64 dimensions (4x expansion).

102.text: MLP second layer: projects back from 64 to 16 dimensions.

103.text: Flatten all weight matrices into a single list for the optimizer to iterate over.

104.text: Print total parameter count for sanity checking.

110.text: Matrix-vector multiply: each output is a dot product of one weight row with the input.

111.text: List comprehension computes all output dimensions in one line.

114.text: Softmax converts raw logits to a probability distribution that sums to 1.

115.text: Subtract max for numerical stability, prevents overflow in exp without changing the result.

116.text: Raise e to each shifted logit. The shift by max ensures the largest exponent is 0, preventing overflow while preserving the relative differences.

117.text: Total of all exponentiated values. Dividing each exp by this total produces a proper probability distribution that sums to 1.

118.text: Divide each exp by the total, now they sum to 1.

121.text: RMSNorm: simpler than LayerNorm, just scales by inverse root-mean-square. No learnable parameters here.

122.text: The core statistic of RMSNorm: average the squares of all elements. This measures how "large" the vector is, ignoring direction.

123.text: Inverse square root with epsilon for numerical safety.

124.text: Scale each element, all vectors end up with roughly unit magnitude.

127.text: The core model: takes a partially masked sequence, returns predictions for every position. This is bidirectional, no causal mask, so every token can see every other token.

129.text: Sequence length, always block_size due to padding.

132.text: Build the input representations: combine what each token is with where it sits.

133.text: Add token embedding + position embedding, then normalize. Every position gets full context about identity and location.

134.text: Enumerate gives us both position and token ID.

135.text: Closes the list comprehension that built normalized embeddings for every token position in the input sequence.

139.text: Snapshot the representation before attention transforms it. The residual connection will add this back, so the layer can only refine, not destroy.

144.text: Empty list that will collect each position's output after multi-head attention processes the full bidirectional context.

145.text: Loop over every position, each one attends to all others.

146.text: Accumulator for this position's multi-head output.

147.text: Process each attention head independently. Each head learns different patterns by operating on its own slice of the embedding dimensions.

148.text: Starting index for this head's slice of the embedding.

149.text: Pull out this head's 4-dimensional query slice from the full projection, giving this head its own private "question" vector.

150.text: Compute attention scores: dot product of query with every key, scaled by sqrt(head_dim). No causal mask, this is the key difference from GPT. Every position sees every other position, which lets the model gather clues from all unmasked tokens.

151.text: Softmax over all positions gives attention weights.

152.text: Weighted sum of value vectors, the attended output for this head.

153.text: Concatenate this head's output to the growing multi-head vector.

154.text: Output projection mixes information across heads.

155.text: Residual connection: add the original input back. Gradients flow through both the attention path and the skip connection.

157.text: Start building new representations through the MLP.

158.text: Process each position through the feed-forward network.

159.text: Snapshot before the MLP transforms it. The residual path ensures gradient flow and lets the MLP only add refinements.

160.text: Normalize activations before the feed-forward network. Pre-norm architecture keeps gradients stable without needing careful initialization.

161.text: Expand to 4x width, the "thinking space" of the MLP.

162.text: ReLU activation: zero out negatives, creating sparse representations.

164.text: Residual connection for the MLP block.

166.text: Project every position to vocabulary logits using the tied embedding matrix. Returns predictions for all positions simultaneously.

170.text: Adam optimizer hyperparameters: learning rate, momentum terms, and epsilon for numerical stability.

171.text: First moment (mean of gradients) buffer, one slot per parameter.

172.text: Second moment (mean of squared gradients) buffer.

175.text: 2000 steps instead of the usual 1000, diffusion training needs more iterations because each step only sees a random subset of masked positions.

176.text: Begin the main training loop, iterating over documents and noise levels for each step.

178.text: Cycle through documents, one per step.

179.text: Tokenize the name and pad with PAD tokens to fill the fixed-length sequence.

180.text: Truncate if the name is longer than block_size.

184.text: Sample noise level t from a log-uniform distribution between 0.2 and 1.0. Log-uniform means t ~ 1/t, which gives equal weight to all scales of corruption. This cancels the 1/t factor in the ELBO loss, a clever importance sampling trick from MDLM.

185.text: Corrupt the clean sequence: each token is independently replaced with MASK with probability t. High t means heavy corruption (mostly MASK), low t means light corruption (mostly clean).

186.text: Count how many positions were actually masked.

187.text: If nothing got masked, skip this step, no learning signal.

188.text: Continue to the next training step.

191.text: Forward the noisy sequence through the bidirectional model, it sees all positions at once.

192.text: Collect the cross-entropy loss at each masked position. We'll average these to get the training signal for this batch.

193.text: Loop over all positions in the sequence.

194.text: Only compute loss on masked positions, the model should predict what was hidden.

195.text: Copy logits to avoid modifying the original.

196.text: Suppress the MASK token in predictions by subtracting a huge number. The model should predict real tokens, never MASK itself. This is the "subs" parameterization from MDLM.

197.text: Turn the raw logit scores into a probability distribution via softmax, so we can compute cross-entropy loss against the true token.

198.text: Cross-entropy loss: negative log probability of the correct (clean) token.

199.text: Average the per-position losses. The 1/t ELBO weight is already handled by our log-uniform sampling of t, so we just take the mean.

202.text: Backpropagate: compute gradients for every parameter in the computation graph.

205.text: Linear learning rate decay, start fast, end slow.

207.text: Update first moment estimate (exponential moving average of gradients).

208.text: Update second moment estimate (exponential moving average of squared gradients).

209.text: Bias correction for first moment, compensates for zero initialization.

211.text: The actual parameter update: step size is adapted per-parameter based on gradient history.

214.text: Log every 10 steps and the very first step.

215.text: Print step count and loss, watch it decrease as the model learns to unmask.

220.text: Strip autograd wrappers: extract plain floats from all Value nodes. Inference doesn't need gradients, so this eliminates overhead.

223.text: Plain-float softmax for inference, no computation graph, just numbers.

224.text: Max subtraction for numerical stability, same idea as the training version.

226.text: Sum all exponentiated values to get the normalization constant. Dividing each exp by this total produces probabilities summing to 1.

227.text: Divide each exponentiated value by the total so the result is a proper probability distribution that sums to 1.

230.text: Inference version of RMSNorm using plain Python floats instead of Value nodes, avoiding the overhead of building a computation graph.

231.text: Compute the mean of squared values, which is the core statistic RMSNorm uses for scaling.

232.text: Compute the inverse square root of the mean-square plus epsilon for numerical safety.

233.text: Multiply each element by the inverse RMS scale so all vectors end up with roughly unit magnitude.

237.text: Same dot products, just on raw floats.

240.text: Inference version of the mask predictor, same architecture, no computation graph overhead.

242.text: Store the sequence length, which is always block_size because diffusion pads every input.

243.text: Embed and normalize all tokens, identical to training, but with plain floats.

244.text: Walk through each transformer layer in sequence, progressively refining the token representations at each step.

245.text: Save the current representations as a residual so they can be added back after attention.

246.text: Normalize activations before the MLP sublayer to stabilize training gradients.

247.text: Project every token's normalized representation into query vectors. Queries encode "what information am I looking for?" at each position.

248.text: Project every token into key vectors. Keys encode "what information do I contain?" for other tokens to match against.

249.text: Project every token into value vectors. Values carry the actual content that will be retrieved and blended based on attention weights.

250.text: Empty list that will collect each position's output after the multi-head attention computation across all positions.

251.text: Iterate through every position in the sequence so each token can attend to all others in this bidirectional model.

252.text: Empty list that accumulates the output from each attention head before concatenation into the full multi-head result.

253.text: Iterate over each attention head so they can each focus on different parts of the embedding.

254.text: Compute the starting index into the embedding vector for this head's 4-dimensional slice.

255.text: Slice out this head's query from the full projection, isolating the dimensions this particular head is responsible for.

256.text: Scaled dot-product attention scores against all keys.

257.text: Convert the raw attention scores into a probability distribution over all positions, determining how much each token contributes.

258.text: Blend all value vectors together weighted by the attention probabilities, producing a summary of the relevant context for this head.

259.text: Concatenate this head's output onto the growing multi-head vector before the output projection.

260.text: Project the concatenated multi-head output back to the model dimension through a linear layer.

261.text: Add the attention output back to the residual stream, letting gradients flow through the skip path.

262.text: Start building new representations through the position-wise feed-forward network.

263.text: Apply the MLP independently to each position in the sequence.

264.text: Save the current representations as a residual so they can be added back after the MLP.

265.text: Normalize activations before the MLP sublayer to stabilize training gradients.

266.text: Project each token into the wider 4x hidden space where the feed-forward network does its work.

268.text: Compress the expanded representation back to the model dimension after the nonlinearity.

269.text: Add the MLP output back to the residual stream so information can flow through unchanged.

270.text: Project all positions to vocabulary logits.

274.text: 64 denoising steps: each step unmasks a few more positions, gradually revealing the name from pure noise.

275.text: Print a section header before displaying the generated names.

276.text: Generate 20 names to see what the model has learned. Each starts from pure noise and crystallizes through iterative unmasking.

277.text: Start from pure noise: every position is MASK. The name doesn't exist yet, it will crystallize through iterative unmasking.

279.text: Count down from full noise to clean. Each step reduces the noise level.

280.text: Cosine noise schedule: more time is spent at intermediate noise levels where the interesting decisions happen. Rapid change near endpoints, slow in the middle.

281.text: Next noise level (one step less noisy). The gap between t and s determines how many tokens to unmask this step.

282.text: Temperature annealing: explore broadly early on (0.8) when the model is uncertain, commit to specific choices later (0.3) as the name takes shape.

285.text: Get the model's predictions for all masked positions in one bidirectional forward pass.

286.text: Copy current sequence to fill in predictions.

287.text: Track confidence for each prediction, used to decide which tokens to keep and which to re-mask.

288.text: Iterate through every position in the sequence to check which ones are currently masked and need predictions.

289.text: Only predict for currently masked positions.

290.text: Grab the model's raw prediction scores for this position, which we'll convert to probabilities for sampling.

291.text: Suppress MASK in predictions, same trick as training.

292.text: Apply temperature scaling before sampling. Dividing logits by temperature sharpens (low temp) or flattens (high temp) the distribution.

293.text: Sample a token from the probability distribution. Unlike argmax, this preserves diversity.

294.text: Record the model's confidence (max probability) alongside the position, high confidence means the model is sure about this prediction.

297.text: If we haven't reached the final step (s > 0), re-mask the least confident predictions to give them another chance in future steps.

298.text: How many to re-mask: proportional to remaining noise ratio s/t. As noise decreases, fewer tokens get re-masked.

299.text: Sort by confidence (ascending) so the least confident predictions are first.

300.text: Iterate over the least confident predictions.

301.text: Put MASK back, this position will get another try in the next denoising step.

302.text: Update the sequence with the (partially re-masked) predictions.

305.text: Decode: convert token IDs back to characters, skipping any PAD tokens.

306.text: Print each generated name, these emerged from pure noise through iterative unmasking, with no left-to-right constraint.

title: PyTorch Quantized
tagline: Compress a trained model from FP32 to INT8. The technique behind deploying models on phones and edge devices.
code:
"""
microGPT — PyTorch quantized edition.

Same architecture as 03_pytorch, but with INT8 quantization for inference.
Trains in FP32, quantizes Linear layers to INT8, compares size and speed.
Shows ~4× memory reduction. Production systems also get speed improvements.
"""

import math
import os
import random
import time

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F

random.seed(42)
torch.manual_seed(42)

# Dataset
input_path = os.path.join(os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__)), "..", "data", "input.txt")
if not os.path.exists(input_path):
    import urllib.request

    url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/karpathy/makemore/refs/heads/master/names.txt"
    urllib.request.urlretrieve(url, input_path)

docs = [l.strip() for l in open(input_path).read().strip().split("\n") if l.strip()]
random.shuffle(docs)
print(f"num docs: {len(docs)}")

# Tokenizer (character-level, identical to the original)
uchars = sorted(set("".join(docs)))
BOS = len(uchars)
vocab_size = len(uchars) + 1
print(f"vocab size: {vocab_size}")

# Model (larger than 03 to show quantization benefits)
n_embd = 64     # embedding dimension
n_head = 4      # number of attention heads
n_layer = 2     # number of layers
block_size = 16 # maximum sequence length
head_dim = n_embd // n_head # dimension of each head


class RMSNorm(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, _dim, eps=1e-5):
        super().__init__()
        self.eps = eps

    def forward(self, x):
        return x * torch.rsqrt(x.pow(2).mean(-1, keepdim=True) + self.eps)


class CausalSelfAttention(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.wq = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)
        self.wk = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)
        self.wv = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)
        self.wo = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)

    def forward(self, x):
        B, T, C = x.shape
        q = self.wq(x).view(B, T, n_head, head_dim).transpose(1, 2)
        k = self.wk(x).view(B, T, n_head, head_dim).transpose(1, 2)
        v = self.wv(x).view(B, T, n_head, head_dim).transpose(1, 2)

        att = (q @ k.transpose(-2, -1)) / math.sqrt(head_dim)
        mask = torch.triu(torch.ones(T, T, device=x.device), diagonal=1).bool()
        att = att.masked_fill(mask, float("-inf"))
        att = F.softmax(att, dim=-1)

        out = (att @ v).transpose(1, 2).reshape(B, T, C)
        return self.wo(out)


class MLP(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.fc1 = nn.Linear(n_embd, 4 * n_embd, bias=False)
        self.fc2 = nn.Linear(4 * n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)

    def forward(self, x):
        return self.fc2(F.relu(self.fc1(x)))


class Block(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.norm1 = RMSNorm(n_embd)
        self.attn = CausalSelfAttention()
        self.norm2 = RMSNorm(n_embd)
        self.mlp = MLP()

    def forward(self, x):
        x = x + self.attn(self.norm1(x))
        x = x + self.mlp(self.norm2(x))
        return x


class MicroGPT(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.wte = nn.Embedding(vocab_size, n_embd)
        self.wpe = nn.Embedding(block_size, n_embd)
        self.norm_in = RMSNorm(n_embd)
        self.layers = nn.ModuleList([Block() for _ in range(n_layer)])
        self.lm_head = nn.Linear(n_embd, vocab_size, bias=False)
        self.apply(self._init_weights)

    @staticmethod
    def _init_weights(module):
        if isinstance(module, (nn.Linear, nn.Embedding)):
            nn.init.normal_(module.weight, mean=0.0, std=0.08)

    def forward(self, idx):
        B, T = idx.shape
        tok_emb = self.wte(idx)
        pos_emb = self.wpe(torch.arange(T, device=idx.device))
        x = self.norm_in(tok_emb + pos_emb)
        for layer in self.layers:
            x = layer(x)
        return self.lm_head(x)


# Training
device = "cpu"
model = MicroGPT().to(device)
print(f"num params: {sum(p.numel() for p in model.parameters())}")

optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=1e-2, betas=(0.85, 0.99), eps=1e-8)
num_steps = 1000

for step in range(num_steps):
    doc = docs[step % len(docs)]
    tokens = [BOS] + [uchars.index(ch) for ch in doc] + [BOS]
    n = min(block_size, len(tokens) - 1)

    input_ids = torch.tensor([tokens[:n]], device=device)
    targets = torch.tensor([tokens[1 : n + 1]], device=device)

    logits = model(input_ids)
    loss = F.cross_entropy(logits.view(-1, vocab_size), targets.view(-1))

    optimizer.zero_grad()
    loss.backward()
    lr_t = 1e-2 * (1 - step / num_steps)
    for pg in optimizer.param_groups:
        pg["lr"] = lr_t
    optimizer.step()

    if (step + 1) % 10 == 0 or step == 0:
        print(f"step {step + 1:4d} / {num_steps:4d} | loss {loss.item():.4f}")

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Quantization
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
print("\n--- quantization ---")


class QuantizedLinear(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, fp32_linear):
        super().__init__()
        w = fp32_linear.weight.data
        scale = w.abs().max() / 127.0
        self.register_buffer("weight_int8", torch.round(w / scale).to(torch.int8))
        self.register_buffer("scale", torch.tensor(scale))
        self.bias = fp32_linear.bias

    def forward(self, x):
        return F.linear(x, self.weight_int8.to(x.dtype) * self.scale, self.bias)


def quantize_model(model):
    model_q = type(model)()
    model_q.load_state_dict(model.state_dict())
    for module in model_q.modules():
        for name, child in module.named_children():
            if isinstance(child, nn.Linear):
                setattr(module, name, QuantizedLinear(child))
    return model_q


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Inference & Benchmark
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
temperature = 0.5 # in (0, 1], control the "creativity" of generated text, low to high


@torch.no_grad()
def generate(model, n=1):
    model.eval()
    samples = []
    for _ in range(n):
        tokens = [BOS]
        for _ in range(block_size):
            idx = torch.tensor([tokens[-block_size:]], device=device)
            probs = F.softmax(model(idx)[0, -1] / temperature, dim=-1)
            token_id = torch.multinomial(probs, 1).item()
            if token_id == BOS:
                break
            tokens.append(token_id)
        samples.append(tokens[1:])
    return samples


def benchmark(model):
    import tempfile

    with tempfile.TemporaryDirectory() as d:
        path = os.path.join(d, "model.pt")
        torch.save(model.state_dict(), path)
        size_mb = os.path.getsize(path) / (1024 * 1024)
    start = time.time()
    generate(model, 100)
    return size_mb, (time.time() - start) * 10


fp32_size, fp32_time = benchmark(model)
print(f"FP32: {fp32_size:.3f} MB, {fp32_time:.2f} ms/sample")

model_int8 = quantize_model(model)
int8_size, int8_time = benchmark(model_int8)
print(
    f"INT8: {int8_size:.3f} MB ({int8_size / fp32_size:.1%}), {int8_time:.2f} ms/sample ({int8_time / fp32_time:.1%})"
)
print("\nNote: This implementation prioritizes memory savings.")
print("Production systems use INT8 kernels for both size and speed benefits.")

for label, m in [("FP32", model), ("INT8", model_int8)]:
    print(f"\n--- {label} samples ---")
    for i, s in enumerate(generate(m, 10)):
        print(f"  {i + 1:2d}: {''.join(uchars[t] for t in s)}")

9.text: Standard library math for the sqrt function used to scale attention scores by the head dimension.

10.text: Import os for building portable file paths to the dataset.

11.text: Import random for shuffling the dataset and seeding reproducibility.

12.text: Wall-clock timing for the FP32 vs INT8 benchmark.

14.text: Import the core PyTorch library for tensor operations and autograd.

15.text: Imports PyTorch's neural network module, which provides layers like Linear and Embedding that we'll later quantize to INT8.

16.text: Import PyTorch's functional API for operations like softmax, cross-entropy, and relu.

18.text: Fix both the Python and PyTorch random seeds so results are reproducible across runs.

19.text: Set the PyTorch random seed for reproducible weight initialization and sampling.

22.text: Build a portable file path to the dataset, relative to this script's location.

23.text: Download the dataset from the internet if it is not already on disk.

29.text: Read all names from the dataset file, one per line, stripping whitespace.

34.text: Build a sorted list of unique characters in the dataset to form the token vocabulary.

35.text: Use the length of the character set as the beginning-of-sequence token ID.

36.text: The full vocabulary has 27 tokens: 26 lowercase letters plus the BOS marker.

40.text: Larger model than lab 03 (64 vs 16 embedding dim) to make quantization effects visible. At 16 dimensions the savings are tiny; at 64 the 4x compression is meaningful.

43.text: Names rarely exceed 16 characters, so this caps the positional embedding table and keeps sequences compact.

44.text: Each attention head operates on a 16-dimensional slice of the 64-dimensional embedding.

47.text: Root-mean-square normalization that stabilizes activations without learned parameters.

53.text: Scale the input by its inverse root-mean-square, ensuring activations have roughly unit magnitude without learned parameters.

56.text: Standard causal attention, same as lab 03.

59.text: Q, K, V, output projections. These are the weights that will be quantized to INT8.

60.text: Linear projection that transforms embeddings into key vectors for attention.

61.text: Linear projection that transforms embeddings into value vectors for attention.

62.text: Project the concatenated multi-head output back to the model dimension.

64.text: Run the attention computation: project to Q, K, V, apply the causal mask, and combine.

65.text: Extract batch size, sequence length, and embedding dimension from the input tensor shape.

66.text: Project and reshape the input into query vectors, one per attention head.

67.text: Project and reshape the input into key vectors, one per attention head.

68.text: Project and reshape the input into value vectors, one per attention head.

70.text: Compute Q dot K transposed, scaled by sqrt(head_dim). The scaling prevents softmax from saturating as dimensionality grows.

71.text: Build an upper-triangular boolean mask so each token can only attend to itself and earlier positions.

72.text: Set all future positions to negative infinity so softmax assigns them zero weight, enforcing the causal left-to-right constraint.

73.text: Convert attention scores into a probability distribution that sums to one over the key dimension.

75.text: Multiply attention weights by value vectors and reshape. Each position now holds a context-aware blend of information from earlier tokens.

76.text: Project the concatenated multi-head output back to the model dimension through a linear layer.

79.text: A standard two-layer feed-forward network with ReLU activation in between.

83.text: Compress the expanded 256-dimensional representation back to the 64-dimensional embedding.

85.text: Run the input through the two-layer feed-forward network with ReLU activation.

86.text: The complete feed-forward computation: expand to 4x width, apply ReLU nonlinearity, and compress back in a single expression.

89.text: One transformer block containing self-attention and a feed-forward network with residual connections.

92.text: Normalize activations before the attention sublayer to stabilize training gradients.

93.text: The causal self-attention sublayer that lets each token attend to previous positions.

94.text: Normalize activations before the MLP sublayer to stabilize training gradients.

95.text: The feed-forward network that processes each token independently after attention has mixed information across positions.

97.text: Apply attention and MLP sublayers with residual connections that let gradients flow easily.

98.text: Add the attention output to the residual stream, preserving the original information.

99.text: Add the MLP output to the residual stream so the model can refine representations incrementally.

103.text: Full model, same architecture as lab 03 but larger (64-dim instead of 16-dim).

106.text: Look up learned token embeddings in a 27 by 64 table, one vector per token.

107.text: Look up learned position embeddings in a 16 by 64 table, one vector per position.

108.text: Normalize the combined token-plus-position embedding before feeding it into the transformer.

109.text: A stack of transformer blocks, each containing attention and MLP sublayers.

111.text: Apply the custom weight initialization function to every module in the model.

115.text: Draw initial weights from a narrow Gaussian so activations stay reasonably scaled at the start.

118.text: Run the full model on the input sequence to produce next-token logits for every position.

119.text: Extract the batch size and sequence length from the input tensor shape.

120.text: Look up a learned embedding vector for each input token to represent its identity.

121.text: Create a position embedding for each slot in the sequence so the model knows token ordering.

122.text: Add token and position embeddings together, then normalize before entering the transformer.

123.text: Pass the normalized representation through each transformer block in sequence.

125.text: Map the final hidden states to vocabulary-sized logits for predicting the next token.

129.text: Use CPU for this tiny model since it trains in seconds without a GPU.

130.text: Instantiate the model and move it to the target device for training.

131.text: Show parameter count, larger than lab 03 to make quantization meaningful.

133.text: Set up the Adam optimizer with momentum and epsilon tuned for this small model.

134.text: Train for 1000 steps, enough for this model to learn basic character-level name patterns.

136.text: Standard training loop, identical to other labs.

137.text: Pick the next training document by cycling through the shuffled name list.

138.text: Encode each character in the name as a token ID, bookended by BOS markers.

139.text: Limit the sequence length to block_size so it fits the model's positional embedding table.

141.text: Convert the first n token IDs into a tensor the model can consume as input.

142.text: Build the target sequence by shifting the input one position to the right for next-token prediction.

144.text: Run the full model on the current input batch to get next-token predictions.

145.text: Measure how far the model's predictions are from the true next tokens using cross-entropy loss.

147.text: Clear out any leftover gradients from the previous step so they do not accumulate.

148.text: Compute gradients for every parameter by propagating the loss backward through the graph.

149.text: Linearly reduce the learning rate from its initial value down to zero over the course of training.

150.text: Write the decayed learning rate into every parameter group so the optimizer uses it on the next step.

152.text: Apply the optimizer step, adjusting every parameter in the direction that reduces the loss.

163.text: The quantized linear layer: stores weights as INT8 integers (1 byte each) instead of FP32 floats (4 bytes each). A single scale factor maps between the two representations.

164.text: Takes a trained FP32 linear layer and quantizes its weights.

166.text: Detach the FP32 weight tensor from the computation graph since we only need the raw numbers for the quantization math.

167.text: Compute the scale factor: map the weight range [-max, +max] to [-127, +127]. This single scalar is the key, it determines the precision of the quantization.

168.text: Round to nearest integer and cast to INT8. Each weight now fits in 1 byte instead of 4. The register_buffer saves it as a non-parameter tensor (no gradients).

169.text: Keep the scale factor as a buffer so we can undo the quantization during inference by multiplying INT8 values back to float.

170.text: Carry over bias if the original layer had one (ours don't).

172.text: Forward: dequantize on-the-fly by casting back to float and multiplying by scale. In production, INT8 matmul kernels avoid this cast entirely, they compute directly in integer arithmetic.

173.text: Cast INT8 to float, multiply by scale, then do the matrix multiply. Mathematically: x @ (W_int8 * scale)^T. The approximation error is small because neural network weights are typically well-distributed.

176.text: Quantize the entire model: create a fresh copy and replace all Linear layers with QuantizedLinear.

177.text: Instantiate a fresh model with the same architecture. We'll load trained weights into it and then swap its Linear layers for quantized ones.

178.text: Copy the trained FP32 weights into the new model so we preserve learned behavior before replacing layers with quantized versions.

179.text: Walk every module in the model.

180.text: Look for Linear layers to quantize.

181.text: Found one, replace with quantized version.

189.text: Controls how peaked the sampling distribution is. Lower temperature means more deterministic output; higher means more creative variety.

192.text: Decorator: no gradient tracking needed for inference.

193.text: Generate names, same function for both FP32 and INT8 models.

194.text: Switch to evaluation mode so the model produces consistent outputs during generation.

196.text: Loop through each sample, generating one name at a time from the model.

197.text: Begin each generated name with the beginning-of-sequence token so the model starts fresh.

199.text: Feed the current token sequence through the model to get next-token logits, keeping only the last block_size tokens.

200.text: Temperature-scaled softmax and sample in one line.

201.text: Randomly draw one token from the probability distribution for stochastic generation.

202.text: If the model predicts BOS, treat it as an end-of-name signal and stop generating.

204.text: Add the newly sampled token to the growing sequence and continue generating the next one.

209.text: Benchmark: measure model size on disk and generation speed.

210.text: Temp file import for saving the model.

212.text: Write the model's state dict to a temporary file on disk so we can measure its serialized size in bytes.

214.text: Serialize the state dict, this is what you'd ship to production.

215.text: File size in MB, INT8 should be roughly 4x smaller.

216.text: Time 100 generations for a stable measurement.

217.text: Generate 100 names in a batch to get a stable timing measurement for the benchmark.

218.text: Package the file size in MB and average time per generation into a tuple for the benchmark comparison table.

224.text: Quantize the trained model to INT8.

225.text: Run the same benchmark suite on the INT8 model to compare size and speed against the original FP32 baseline.

232.text: Side-by-side comparison: generate 10 names from each model. Quality should be nearly identical despite 4x compression.

235.text: Decode and print each generated name.

title: Speculative Decoding
tagline: A small draft model guesses ahead, a larger target model verifies in one pass. Same distribution, faster.
code:
"""
microGPT — Speculative decoding edition.

Same architecture as the PyTorch version (03/16), but demonstrating
speculative decoding: a small "draft" model guesses multiple tokens ahead,
then the larger "target" model verifies them all in a single forward pass.
The output distribution is mathematically identical to the target model alone.

This is the #1 technique used in production inference systems (vLLM,
TensorRT-LLM, SGLang) because autoregressive decoding is memory-bound —
each token requires reading all model weights but does very little compute.
"""

import math
import os
import random
import time

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F

random.seed(42)
torch.manual_seed(42)

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Dataset & Tokenizer
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
input_path = os.path.join(os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__)), "..", "data", "input.txt")
if not os.path.exists(input_path):
    import urllib.request

    url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/karpathy/makemore/refs/heads/master/names.txt"
    urllib.request.urlretrieve(url, input_path)

docs = [l.strip() for l in open(input_path).read().strip().split("\n") if l.strip()]
random.shuffle(docs)
print(f"num docs: {len(docs)}")

uchars = sorted(set("".join(docs)))
BOS = len(uchars)
vocab_size = len(uchars) + 1
print(f"vocab size: {vocab_size}")

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Model — two sizes: a small draft model and a larger target model
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
block_size = 16 # maximum sequence length


class RMSNorm(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, _dim, eps=1e-5):
        super().__init__()
        self.eps = eps

    def forward(self, x):
        return x * torch.rsqrt(x.pow(2).mean(-1, keepdim=True) + self.eps)


class CausalSelfAttention(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, n_embd, n_head):
        super().__init__()
        self.n_head = n_head
        self.head_dim = n_embd // n_head
        self.wq = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)
        self.wk = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)
        self.wv = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)
        self.wo = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)

    def forward(self, x):
        B, T, C = x.shape
        q = self.wq(x).view(B, T, self.n_head, self.head_dim).transpose(1, 2)
        k = self.wk(x).view(B, T, self.n_head, self.head_dim).transpose(1, 2)
        v = self.wv(x).view(B, T, self.n_head, self.head_dim).transpose(1, 2)
        att = (q @ k.transpose(-2, -1)) / math.sqrt(self.head_dim)
        mask = torch.triu(torch.ones(T, T, device=x.device), diagonal=1).bool()
        att = att.masked_fill(mask, float("-inf"))
        att = F.softmax(att, dim=-1)
        out = (att @ v).transpose(1, 2).reshape(B, T, C)
        return self.wo(out)


class MLP(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, n_embd):
        super().__init__()
        self.fc1 = nn.Linear(n_embd, 4 * n_embd, bias=False)
        self.fc2 = nn.Linear(4 * n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)

    def forward(self, x):
        return self.fc2(F.relu(self.fc1(x)))


class Block(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, n_embd, n_head):
        super().__init__()
        self.norm1 = RMSNorm(n_embd)
        self.attn = CausalSelfAttention(n_embd, n_head)
        self.norm2 = RMSNorm(n_embd)
        self.mlp = MLP(n_embd)

    def forward(self, x):
        x = x + self.attn(self.norm1(x))
        x = x + self.mlp(self.norm2(x))
        return x


class MicroGPT(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, n_embd, n_head, n_layer):
        super().__init__()
        self.wte = nn.Embedding(vocab_size, n_embd)
        self.wpe = nn.Embedding(block_size, n_embd)
        self.norm_in = RMSNorm(n_embd)
        self.layers = nn.ModuleList([Block(n_embd, n_head) for _ in range(n_layer)])
        self.lm_head = nn.Linear(n_embd, vocab_size, bias=False)
        self.apply(self._init_weights)

    @staticmethod
    def _init_weights(module):
        if isinstance(module, (nn.Linear, nn.Embedding)):
            nn.init.normal_(module.weight, mean=0.0, std=0.08)

    def forward(self, idx):
        B, T = idx.shape
        tok_emb = self.wte(idx)
        pos_emb = self.wpe(torch.arange(T, device=idx.device))
        x = self.norm_in(tok_emb + pos_emb)
        for layer in self.layers:
            x = layer(x)
        return self.lm_head(x)


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Training
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
device = "cpu"

# Draft model: small, fast, less accurate
draft_cfg = {"n_embd": 32, "n_head": 4, "n_layer": 1}
# Target model: larger, slower, better quality
target_cfg = {"n_embd": 64, "n_head": 4, "n_layer": 2}


def train_model(model, name, num_steps=1000):
    print(f"\n--- training {name} ({sum(p.numel() for p in model.parameters())} params) ---")
    optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=1e-2, betas=(0.85, 0.99), eps=1e-8)
    for step in range(num_steps):
        doc = docs[step % len(docs)]
        tokens = [BOS] + [uchars.index(ch) for ch in doc] + [BOS]
        n = min(block_size, len(tokens) - 1)
        input_ids = torch.tensor([tokens[:n]], device=device)
        targets = torch.tensor([tokens[1 : n + 1]], device=device)
        logits = model(input_ids)
        loss = F.cross_entropy(logits.view(-1, vocab_size), targets.view(-1))
        optimizer.zero_grad()
        loss.backward()
        lr_t = 1e-2 * (1 - step / num_steps)
        for pg in optimizer.param_groups:
            pg["lr"] = lr_t
        optimizer.step()
        if (step + 1) % 10 == 0 or step == 0:
            print(f"step {step + 1:4d} / {num_steps:4d} | loss {loss.item():.4f}")


draft_model = MicroGPT(**draft_cfg).to(device)
target_model = MicroGPT(**target_cfg).to(device)

train_model(draft_model, "draft")
train_model(target_model, "target")

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Inference methods
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
temperature = 0.5 # in (0, 1], control the "creativity" of generated text, low to high


@torch.no_grad()
def get_probs(model, tokens):
    """Get probability distribution for the next token(s) given a token sequence."""
    idx = torch.tensor([tokens[-block_size:]], device=device)
    logits = model(idx)[0] / temperature  # (T, V)
    return F.softmax(logits, dim=-1)  # (T, V)


# --- 1) Naive autoregressive decoding (baseline) ---


def generate_autoregressive(model, max_len=block_size):
    """Standard decoding: one token at a time, one forward pass per token."""
    tokens = [BOS]
    with torch.no_grad():
        for _ in range(max_len):
            probs = get_probs(model, tokens)[-1]  # last position
            token_id = torch.multinomial(probs, 1).item()
            if token_id == BOS:
                break
            tokens.append(token_id)
    return tokens[1:]  # strip BOS


# --- 2) Speculative decoding ---


def generate_speculative(draft, target, max_len=block_size, K=4):
    """
    Speculative decoding: the draft model proposes K tokens, the target
    model verifies them all in one forward pass.

    The acceptance/rejection algorithm guarantees the output distribution
    is IDENTICAL to target-only generation:

    For each drafted token at position i with draft probability q(x):
      - Target model gives probability p(x) for the same token
      - Accept with probability min(1, p(x) / q(x))
      - On rejection: sample from adjusted distribution max(0, p(x) - q(x))
        (normalized), which corrects for the draft model's bias

    This is mathematically proven to sample from p(x) exactly.
    """
    tokens = [BOS]
    stats = {"drafted": 0, "accepted": 0, "target_fwd": 0, "draft_fwd": 0}

    with torch.no_grad():
        while len(tokens) - 1 < max_len:
            # Step 1: Draft model generates K candidate tokens
            draft_tokens = list(tokens)
            draft_probs_list = []  # probability of each drafted token under draft model
            for _ in range(K):
                if len(draft_tokens) - 1 >= max_len:
                    break
                probs_d = get_probs(draft, draft_tokens)[-1]
                stats["draft_fwd"] += 1
                token_id = torch.multinomial(probs_d, 1).item()
                draft_probs_list.append((token_id, probs_d[token_id].item()))
                draft_tokens.append(token_id)

            if not draft_probs_list:
                break

            stats["drafted"] += len(draft_probs_list)

            # Step 2: Target model scores ALL positions in one forward pass
            # This is the key efficiency win: one forward pass instead of K
            target_probs = get_probs(target, draft_tokens)  # (T, V)
            stats["target_fwd"] += 1

            # Step 3: Accept/reject each drafted token
            n_accepted = 0
            for i, (drafted_token, q_x) in enumerate(draft_probs_list):
                # Target model's probability for the drafted token
                # Position in target_probs: tokens before draft + i
                pos = len(tokens) - 1 + i
                if pos >= target_probs.shape[0]:
                    break
                p_x = target_probs[pos, drafted_token].item()

                # Accept with probability min(1, p(x) / q(x))
                if q_x == 0:
                    break
                accept_prob = min(1.0, p_x / q_x)

                if random.random() < accept_prob:
                    # Accept this token
                    tokens.append(drafted_token)
                    n_accepted += 1
                    if drafted_token == BOS:
                        tokens.pop()  # remove BOS, end generation
                        stats["accepted"] += n_accepted - 1
                        return tokens[1:], stats
                else:
                    # Reject: sample from adjusted distribution max(0, p - q)
                    pos_probs = target_probs[pos]
                    accepted_drafts = draft_tokens[len(tokens) : len(tokens) + i + 1]
                    draft_full = get_probs(draft, tokens + accepted_drafts)[-1]
                    adjusted = torch.clamp(pos_probs - draft_full, min=0)
                    adj_sum = adjusted.sum()
                    if adj_sum > 0:
                        adjusted = adjusted / adj_sum
                    else:
                        adjusted = pos_probs
                    token_id = torch.multinomial(adjusted, 1).item()
                    if token_id == BOS:
                        stats["accepted"] += n_accepted
                        return tokens[1:], stats
                    tokens.append(token_id)
                    break  # restart drafting from the new position

            else:
                # All K tokens accepted — sample one bonus token from target
                bonus_pos = len(tokens) - 1
                if bonus_pos < target_probs.shape[0]:
                    bonus_probs = target_probs[bonus_pos]
                    token_id = torch.multinomial(bonus_probs, 1).item()
                    if token_id != BOS:
                        tokens.append(token_id)

            stats["accepted"] += n_accepted

    return tokens[1:], stats


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Compare the three approaches
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
print("\n--- speculative decoding comparison ---\n")

draft_model.eval()
target_model.eval()
n_samples = 50


def timed_generate(name, gen_fn):
    print(f"generating {n_samples} samples with {name}...")
    start = time.time()
    samples = [gen_fn() for _ in range(n_samples)]
    elapsed = time.time() - start
    print(f"  time: {elapsed:.3f}s ({elapsed / n_samples * 1000:.1f} ms/sample)")
    return samples, elapsed


auto_samples, auto_time = timed_generate(
    "autoregressive decoding (target)", lambda: generate_autoregressive(target_model)
)

# Speculative: also collect accept/reject stats
total_stats = {"drafted": 0, "accepted": 0, "target_fwd": 0, "draft_fwd": 0}
spec_results = []

spec_samples, spec_time = timed_generate(
    "speculative decoding (K=4)",
    lambda: spec_results.append(generate_speculative(draft_model, target_model, K=4)) or spec_results[-1][0],
)
for _, stats in spec_results:
    for k in total_stats:
        total_stats[k] += stats[k]

draft_samples, draft_time = timed_generate(
    "autoregressive decoding (draft)", lambda: generate_autoregressive(draft_model)
)

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Results
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
acceptance_rate = total_stats["accepted"] / max(total_stats["drafted"], 1)
avg_target_fwd = total_stats["target_fwd"] / n_samples
avg_draft_fwd = total_stats["draft_fwd"] / n_samples
avg_auto_tokens = sum(len(s) for s in auto_samples) / n_samples
avg_spec_tokens = sum(len(s) for s in spec_samples) / n_samples

print("\n--- results ---\n")
print(f"acceptance rate: {acceptance_rate:.1%} (of draft tokens accepted by target)")
print(f"avg target forward passes: {avg_target_fwd:.1f} (speculative) vs {avg_auto_tokens:.1f} (autoregressive)")
print(f"avg draft forward passes:  {avg_draft_fwd:.1f}")
print(f"speedup: {auto_time / spec_time:.2f}x (speculative vs autoregressive)")
print(f"avg tokens generated: {avg_auto_tokens:.1f} (auto) vs {avg_spec_tokens:.1f} (spec)")

print("""
--- why this matters ---

Autoregressive decoding is memory-bound: each token requires reading ALL model
weights from memory, but only computes a single vector-matrix product per layer.
The GPU's compute units sit mostly idle, waiting for data from slow memory.

Speculative decoding exploits this: the target model can verify K tokens in
nearly the same time as generating 1, because the bottleneck is reading weights
(which happens once regardless of sequence length), not computing attention.

In production (7B-70B models on GPUs), speculative decoding achieves:
  - 2-3x speedup with a well-matched draft model
  - Higher acceptance rates with better draft models (EAGLE, Medusa)
  - Zero quality loss — the output distribution is mathematically identical

At our tiny scale, the overhead of Python loops can mask the benefit.
On real hardware with large models, this is transformative.
""")

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Show sample outputs
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
print("--- sample outputs ---\n")
for label, samples in [
    ("autoregressive (target)", auto_samples),
    ("speculative decoding", spec_samples),
    ("draft model only", draft_samples),
]:
    print(f"{label}:")
    for i, s in enumerate(samples[:10]):
        print(f"  {i + 1:2d}: {''.join(uchars[t] for t in s)}")
    print()

14.text: Standard math library for attention scaling.

15.text: File path handling for the dataset.

16.text: Random number generation for sampling and shuffling.

17.text: time for benchmarking speculative vs autoregressive decoding speed.

19.text: PyTorch, this lab uses real tensors because we need two full models and speed benchmarks.

20.text: Provides the building blocks for defining neural network layers like Linear and Embedding used in both draft and target models.

21.text: Functional API for softmax, ReLU, cross-entropy.

23.text: Fix Python's random seed for reproducible shuffling.

24.text: Fix PyTorch's random seed for reproducible weight initialization and sampling.

29.text: Builds the path to the dataset file relative to this script.

30.text: If the names dataset isn't cached locally yet, download it on the fly so the script works out of the box.

31.text: Only import the URL library when we actually need to download, keeping startup fast if the file already exists.

34.text: Fetch the dataset from GitHub and save it locally so future runs skip the download entirely.

36.text: Read the file line by line, stripping whitespace. Each line is one name that becomes a training document.

37.text: Shuffle to prevent memorizing alphabetical order.

38.text: Show how many names we loaded so we can verify the dataset is intact.

40.text: Build character vocabulary from unique characters in all names.

43.text: Display the character vocabulary size, including the special BOS/EOS token.

48.text: Max sequence length for all models, names rarely exceed 16 characters.

51.text: RMSNorm: cheaper than LayerNorm, just rescales by inverse root-mean-square.

52.text: Dimension parameter unused but kept for API consistency.

53.text: Call the parent class constructor to properly register this module in PyTorch's module hierarchy for parameter tracking.

54.text: Small epsilon prevents division by zero.

56.text: The forward method computes the RMSNorm normalization for the input tensor.

57.text: Scale x by inverse RMS, rsqrt computes 1/sqrt efficiently.

60.text: Causal (left-to-right) attention, both draft and target use the same architecture, just different sizes.

61.text: Constructor takes embedding dimension and number of heads.

62.text: Standard PyTorch module initialization, calling the parent class constructor.

63.text: Remember how many heads we're using so the forward pass can split the projected vectors into the right number of slices.

64.text: Calculate how many dimensions each head gets by dividing the total embedding size evenly among all heads.

65.text: Linear projection that transforms input embeddings into query vectors for attention.

66.text: Linear projection that transforms input embeddings into key vectors for attention.

67.text: Linear projection that transforms input embeddings into value vectors for attention.

70.text: Run the complete attention computation: project to Q/K/V, compute scores, mask future positions, and blend values.

71.text: Unpack batch size, sequence length, and embedding dimension.

72.text: Project to queries and reshape: (B, T, C) → (B, n_head, T, head_dim). This splits the embedding into independent heads.

73.text: Reshape keys into the same multi-head format as queries, splitting the embedding dimension across independent heads.

74.text: Reshape values into multi-head format. Unlike Q and K which compute scores, values carry the content that attention retrieves.

75.text: Scaled dot-product attention: Q·K^T / sqrt(d). The scaling prevents softmax from saturating in high dimensions.

76.text: Upper triangular mask: prevents each position from attending to future positions. This is what makes it causal.

77.text: Fill future positions with -inf so softmax gives them zero weight.

78.text: Softmax over the key dimension gives attention weights.

79.text: Weighted sum of values, then reshape back to (B, T, C).

80.text: Output projection mixes information across heads.

83.text: Feed-forward network: expand then contract, with nonlinearity in between.

84.text: Store the embedding dimension so the two Linear layers know their input and 4x-expanded output sizes.

85.text: Standard PyTorch module initialization, calling the parent class constructor.

86.text: Expand to 4x width, the MLP's "thinking space".

89.text: Run the input through the expand-ReLU-contract sequence and return the result.

90.text: ReLU sandwiched between two linear layers.

93.text: A transformer block: attention + MLP with residual connections.

95.text: Standard PyTorch module initialization, calling the parent class constructor.

96.text: RMSNorm before attention stabilizes the input scale, so the projection matrices see consistent activation magnitudes.

97.text: The multi-head causal self-attention layer that lets each token gather information from all previous positions in the sequence.

98.text: RMSNorm before the feed-forward network, ensuring the MLP sees well-scaled inputs regardless of what attention produced.

99.text: The position-wise feed-forward network that processes each token independently through an expand-ReLU-contract pattern.

101.text: Run input through attention and MLP sublayers with residual connections.

103.text: Add the MLP's refinement to the residual stream, preserving the original signal so the layer can only improve representations.

104.text: Pass the updated hidden states back to the caller. These flow into the next transformer block or the final projection.

107.text: The full GPT model, used for both draft and target, just with different sizes.

108.text: Parameterized constructor: embedding size, heads, and layers are configurable for creating differently-sized models.

109.text: Standard PyTorch module initialization, calling the parent class constructor.

110.text: Token embedding: maps token IDs to dense vectors.

111.text: Position embedding: maps positions to dense vectors.

112.text: Normalize the sum of token and position embeddings before entering the transformer stack, stabilizing the first layer's input.

113.text: Stack of transformer blocks, the draft model gets 1, the target gets 2.

114.text: Language model head: projects hidden states to vocabulary logits.

115.text: Walk through every submodule and apply the weight initialization function, ensuring all layers start with well-scaled parameters.

118.text: Defines how weights are initialized. Called once per module by self.apply() during model construction.

119.text: Only initialize Linear and Embedding layers.

120.text: Small Gaussian initialization (std=0.08) prevents exploding activations.

122.text: Forward pass: token IDs in, logits out.

124.text: Convert each input token ID into a dense vector by indexing into the learned embedding table.

125.text: Fetch a learned position vector for each slot in the sequence so the model knows the ordering of tokens.

126.text: Add token + position embeddings, then normalize.

127.text: Feed the normalized embeddings through each transformer block in sequence, progressively refining the representations.

128.text: Run one transformer block, updating the hidden representations for this layer.

129.text: Project to vocabulary logits, one score per token in the vocabulary.

135.text: CPU for this demo, the speedup story is cleaner on GPU where memory-boundedness is stark.

138.text: Draft model: 32-dim embeddings, 4 heads, 1 layer. Small and fast, but less accurate. It will propose tokens cheaply.

140.text: Target model: 64-dim embeddings, 4 heads, 2 layers. Better quality, but 4x slower per forward pass. This is the model whose distribution we want.

143.text: Shared training function, both models train the same way, just with different architectures.

144.text: Print model name and parameter count.

145.text: Adam optimizer with the standard hyperparameters.

146.text: Iterate over training steps, processing one name per step with teacher forcing.

147.text: Pick the next training example by cycling through the shuffled name list with a modulo index.

148.text: Tokenize: BOS + characters + BOS (BOS doubles as EOS).

150.text: Input: all tokens except the last.

151.text: Target: all tokens except the first (shifted by one).

154.text: Zero out gradients from the previous step.

156.text: Scale the learning rate down linearly from its initial value toward zero as training progresses, for smoother convergence.

157.text: Write the decayed learning rate into every parameter group so the optimizer uses the updated value on the next step.

158.text: Assign the computed decayed learning rate value to this parameter group's learning rate entry.

159.text: Apply the Adam optimizer update rule to adjust all model parameters using gradients.

160.text: Print a status update at regular intervals and on the first step, so we can watch the loss decrease during training.

161.text: Show the current training step number and loss value so we can track convergence at a glance.

164.text: Instantiate the small draft model with 32-dim embeddings and 1 layer, then move it to the compute device for training.

165.text: Create the target model, same architecture class, different size.

167.text: Run the standard training loop on the small draft model so it learns basic character-level patterns for proposing tokens.

168.text: Train the target model separately, in production these would be pre-trained independently.

173.text: Temperature 0.5: moderate creativity. Lower values make output more deterministic.

176.text: Disable gradient computation for inference, saves memory and speeds things up.

177.text: Helper: run a model on tokens and return the probability distribution for every position.

179.text: Wrap tokens in a tensor, keeping only the last block_size tokens (sliding window).

180.text: Forward pass and apply temperature. Index [0] removes the batch dimension.

181.text: Softmax converts logits to probabilities. Returns (T, V), one distribution per position.

187.text: Standard autoregressive generation: one token, one forward pass, repeat. This is the baseline we want to beat.

189.text: Start with just the BOS token.

192.text: Run the model on the full token sequence and return only the last position's probability distribution for next-token prediction.

193.text: Sample one token from the distribution.

194.text: The BOS token doubles as an end-of-sequence marker. When sampled, it signals that the name is complete.

195.text: End-of-sequence detected, so stop generating further tokens for this name.

196.text: Append the new token and continue.

197.text: Hand back the generated token sequence with the leading BOS removed, since it was just a start signal, not part of the name.
