203.text: The heart of the lab: speculative decoding with draft model draft and target model target. K=4 means the draft model guesses 4 tokens ahead.

220.text: Statistics tracking: how many tokens drafted, accepted, and how many forward passes each model ran.

222.text: Disable PyTorch's gradient tracking during inference since we don't need backpropagation, saving memory and compute.

225.text: Copy current tokens, the draft model will extend this speculatively.

226.text: Track each drafted token and its probability under the draft model.

227.text: Speculatively generate K tokens using the cheap draft model. These guesses will be verified against the target in one batch.

228.text: Stop if we've hit the length limit.

229.text: Break out of the draft loop.

230.text: Get draft model's next-token probabilities. This is cheap because the draft model is small.

231.text: Keep a running count of how many forward passes the draft model has executed, for the efficiency comparison later.

232.text: Sample a token from the draft distribution.

233.text: Record the token and its draft probability q(x), we'll need q(x) for the acceptance ratio.

234.text: Append the newly sampled token to the draft sequence so the next draft step can condition on it.

236.text: Edge case: no tokens were drafted.

237.text: Edge case: if no tokens were drafted (e.g., length limit hit immediately), skip the verification step entirely.

239.text: Record how many tokens the draft model proposed.

243.text: The key efficiency win: the target model scores ALL drafted positions in ONE forward pass. Autoregressive decoding would need K separate forward passes. Since inference is memory-bound (reading weights dominates), one pass costs almost the same as K.

244.text: Count target forward passes, should be much fewer than autoregressive.

247.text: Track how many consecutive drafts get accepted in this round.

248.text: Verify each drafted token against the target model's distribution.

251.text: Map draft position to target's output position.

252.text: Make sure the draft position maps to a valid index in the target's output tensor.

253.text: The position is beyond what the target model computed, so exit the verification loop.

254.text: Get the target model's probability p(x) for the exact token the draft proposed.

257.text: Zero draft probability means the draft model would never have picked this token, reject.

259.text: The acceptance probability: min(1, p(x)/q(x)). If the target likes this token at least as much as the draft, always accept. If the target likes it less, accept proportionally. This ratio is what guarantees the output distribution matches the target exactly.

261.text: Coin flip: accept with the computed probability.

263.text: Token accepted, add to the final sequence.

264.text: Bump the accepted token counter. We'll compare accepted vs. drafted to compute the acceptance rate.

265.text: Special case: accepted token is BOS (end of sequence).

266.text: Remove BOS from the output and stop generating.

267.text: Update stats (don't count BOS as accepted).

268.text: Bundle the final token sequence with the draft/target statistics so the caller can analyze both quality and efficiency.

271.text: Retrieve the target model's full probability distribution at the rejected position, needed for the correction sampling step.

272.text: Reconstruct the draft context up to the rejected position.

273.text: Re-run draft model to get its full distribution (needed for the correction term).

274.text: The rejection correction: max(0, p(x) - q(x)). This subtracts the draft's contribution, leaving only the "extra" probability mass that the target assigns. After normalization, sampling from this corrected distribution guarantees overall samples come from p(x).

275.text: Sum the corrected distribution to check if it has positive mass. If it's all zeros, we'll fall back to the raw target distribution.

276.text: If there's positive mass in the adjusted distribution...

277.text: Normalize to a proper probability distribution.

279.text: Fallback: if adjustment zeroes everything out, use the target's raw distribution.

281.text: If the sampled token is BOS (end-of-sequence marker), the name is done and we should stop generating further tokens.

282.text: Record the final counts and return early since the name ended with the corrected token.

283.text: Package the completed sequence and return it to the caller along with the generation statistics.

284.text: Add the corrected token to the output sequence. This token was sampled from the adjusted distribution to match the target's behavior.

285.text: After rejection, restart the draft loop from the new position. Don't try to reuse remaining drafts.

287.text: Python for-else: this runs if the loop completed without break, meaning ALL K tokens were accepted.

289.text: When all K drafts are accepted, we get a bonus: the target already computed the probability distribution for the position after the last draft.

290.text: Bounds check on the bonus position.

291.text: Look up what the target model would predict at the bonus position, which we get for free since the target already computed it.

292.text: Sample a bonus token, a free extra token from the target with no additional cost.

293.text: Don't append BOS (that ends the sequence).

294.text: Add this free bonus token to the sequence. It cost zero extra forward passes since it came from the target's existing computation.

296.text: Update accepted count at end of round.

298.text: Package the completed token sequence together with the acceptance statistics for the final benchmark comparison.

306.text: Switch draft model to evaluation mode (disables dropout if any).

307.text: Switch target model to evaluation mode.

311.text: Utility: time a generation function over n_samples runs.

312.text: Print a label identifying which generation method is about to be timed, so the benchmark output is easy to read.

313.text: Record the wall-clock start time so we can measure total generation duration.

314.text: Run the generation function for all samples, collecting both the outputs and the total wall-clock time.

315.text: Record the end time so we can compute the total elapsed duration and per-sample average.

317.text: Bundle the generated samples together with the elapsed time so the caller can analyze both output quality and speed.

320.text: Benchmark 1: standard autoregressive decoding with the target model (the baseline).

321.text: One forward pass per token, the slowest approach.

325.text: Zero out the counters for drafted tokens, accepted tokens, and forward passes before aggregating across all samples.

326.text: List to collect results with stats.

328.text: Benchmark 2: speculative decoding (draft proposes, target verifies).

329.text: K=4: draft guesses 4 tokens per round.

330.text: Lambda collects both the tokens and the acceptance statistics from each generation.

333.text: Add up each statistic category (drafted, accepted, target passes, draft passes) across all generated samples.

334.text: Add this sample's individual stat values into the running totals for the final summary.

336.text: Benchmark 3: draft-only autoregressive (for reference, fast but lower quality).

337.text: Uses the small draft model alone.

343.text: Acceptance rate: what fraction of draft tokens the target model approved. Higher means the draft model closely matches the target.

344.text: Average target forward passes per sample, should be much less than the average token count.

345.text: Average draft forward passes per sample.

347.text: Average tokens generated speculatively (should match autoregressive closely).

349.text: Print the statistics section header before showing acceptance rates and forward pass counts.

350.text: Display what fraction of the draft model's proposed tokens the target model accepted, showing how well the models agree.

351.text: Compare forward pass counts, this is the efficiency win.

353.text: Wall-clock speedup ratio: how much faster speculative is.

354.text: Token counts should be similar, the distribution is mathematically identical.

379.text: Show actual generated names from each method.

381.text: The names generated by standard autoregressive decoding with the full target model, our quality baseline.

382.text: The names generated by speculative decoding, which should match the target's distribution exactly despite being faster.

383.text: Names from the small draft model alone. Faster but lower quality, included to show the quality gap that speculative decoding bridges.

385.text: Print which generation method produced the following names so we can compare quality across approaches.

386.text: Show the first 10 samples from each method.

387.text: Decode token IDs back to characters and print.

388.text: Visual separator between the three methods' output blocks for cleaner readability in the terminal.

title: Tiled Attention (FlashAttention)
tagline: Three attention implementations producing identical outputs. The difference is memory trips. This is why FlashAttention matters.
code:
"""
microGPT — Tiled attention edition.

Same architecture as the PyTorch version (03), but demonstrating three
attention implementations for inference: standard, online-softmax, and
tiled (FlashAttention algorithm). All three produce identical outputs;
the difference is how many "slow memory" (HBM) operations they need.

Training uses PyTorch; the attention implementations are in explicit NumPy
to make every memory access visible and countable.
"""

import math
import os
import random

import numpy as np
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F

random.seed(42)
rng = np.random.default_rng(42)
torch.manual_seed(42)
torch.set_default_dtype(torch.float64)

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Dataset & Tokenizer
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
input_path = os.path.join(os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__)), "..", "data", "input.txt")
if not os.path.exists(input_path):
    import urllib.request

    urllib.request.urlretrieve(
        "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/karpathy/makemore/refs/heads/master/names.txt", input_path
    )

docs = [l.strip() for l in open(input_path).read().strip().split("\n") if l.strip()]
random.shuffle(docs)
print(f"num docs: {len(docs)}")

uchars = sorted(set("".join(docs)))
BOS, vocab_size = len(uchars), len(uchars) + 1
print(f"vocab size: {vocab_size}")

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Model (PyTorch — identical architecture to Lab 03)
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
n_embd, n_head, n_layer, block_size = 16, 4, 1, 16
head_dim = n_embd // n_head


class RMSNorm(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, _dim, eps=1e-5):
        super().__init__()
        self.eps = eps

    def forward(self, x):
        return x * torch.rsqrt(x.pow(2).mean(-1, keepdim=True) + self.eps)


class CausalSelfAttention(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.wq = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)
        self.wk = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)
        self.wv = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)
        self.wo = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)

    def forward(self, x):
        B, T, C = x.shape
        q = self.wq(x).view(B, T, n_head, head_dim).transpose(1, 2)
        k = self.wk(x).view(B, T, n_head, head_dim).transpose(1, 2)
        v = self.wv(x).view(B, T, n_head, head_dim).transpose(1, 2)
        att = (q @ k.transpose(-2, -1)) / math.sqrt(head_dim)
        mask = torch.triu(torch.ones(T, T, device=x.device), diagonal=1).bool()
        att = att.masked_fill(mask, float("-inf"))
        out = (F.softmax(att, dim=-1) @ v).transpose(1, 2).reshape(B, T, C)
        return self.wo(out)


class Block(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.norm1 = RMSNorm(n_embd)
        self.attn = CausalSelfAttention()
        self.norm2 = RMSNorm(n_embd)
        self.fc1 = nn.Linear(n_embd, 4 * n_embd, bias=False)
        self.fc2 = nn.Linear(4 * n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)

    def forward(self, x):
        x = x + self.attn(self.norm1(x))
        x = x + self.fc2(F.relu(self.fc1(self.norm2(x))))
        return x


class MicroGPT(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.wte = nn.Embedding(vocab_size, n_embd)
        self.wpe = nn.Embedding(block_size, n_embd)
        self.norm_in = RMSNorm(n_embd)
        self.layers = nn.ModuleList([Block() for _ in range(n_layer)])
        self.lm_head = nn.Linear(n_embd, vocab_size, bias=False)
        self.apply(self._init_weights)

    @staticmethod
    def _init_weights(module):
        if isinstance(module, (nn.Linear, nn.Embedding)):
            nn.init.normal_(module.weight, mean=0.0, std=0.08)

    def forward(self, idx):
        B, T = idx.shape
        x = self.norm_in(self.wte(idx) + self.wpe(torch.arange(T, device=idx.device)))
        for layer in self.layers:
            x = layer(x)
        return self.lm_head(x)


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Training
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
model = MicroGPT()
print(f"num params: {sum(p.numel() for p in model.parameters())}")
optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=1e-2, betas=(0.85, 0.99), eps=1e-8)
num_steps = 1000

for step in range(num_steps):
    doc = docs[step % len(docs)]
    tokens = [BOS] + [uchars.index(ch) for ch in doc] + [BOS]
    n = min(block_size, len(tokens) - 1)
    input_ids = torch.tensor([tokens[:n]])
    targets = torch.tensor([tokens[1 : n + 1]])
    logits = model(input_ids)
    loss = F.cross_entropy(logits.view(-1, vocab_size), targets.view(-1))
    optimizer.zero_grad()
    loss.backward()
    lr_t = 1e-2 * (1 - step / num_steps)
    for pg in optimizer.param_groups:
        pg["lr"] = lr_t
    optimizer.step()
    if (step + 1) % 10 == 0 or step == 0:
        print(f"step {step + 1:4d} / {num_steps:4d} | loss {loss.item():.4f}")

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Extract trained weights to NumPy for attention experiments
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Training used PyTorch for convenience. The attention implementations below
# are in explicit NumPy so every memory access is visible and countable.
sd = {k: v.detach().numpy() for k, v in model.state_dict().items()}
P = {"wte": sd["wte.weight"], "wpe": sd["wpe.weight"], "lm_head": sd["lm_head.weight"]}
for i in range(n_layer):
    for w in ("wq", "wk", "wv", "wo"):
        P[f"l{i}.{w}"] = sd[f"layers.{i}.attn.{w}.weight"].T
    for w in ("fc1", "fc2"):
        P[f"l{i}.{w}"] = sd[f"layers.{i}.{w}.weight"].T


def softmax_np(logits):
    exps = np.exp(logits - logits.max(axis=-1, keepdims=True))
    return exps / exps.sum(axis=-1, keepdims=True)


def rmsnorm_np(x):
    return x / np.sqrt(np.mean(x**2, axis=-1, keepdims=True) + 1e-5)


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Three attention implementations — the core of this lab
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
print("\n--- comparing attention implementations ---\n")


def inference_forward(token_ids, attn_fn):
    """Forward pass using a pluggable attention function (all NumPy)."""
    n = len(token_ids)
    last_stats = {}
    x = rmsnorm_np(P["wte"][token_ids] + P["wpe"][np.arange(n)])
    for li in range(n_layer):
        x_res = x.copy()
        x_n = rmsnorm_np(x)
        Q_h, K_h, V_h = (
            (x_n @ P[f"l{li}.{w}"]).reshape(n, n_head, head_dim).transpose(1, 0, 2) for w in ("wq", "wk", "wv")
        )
        attn_out, last_stats = attn_fn(Q_h, K_h, V_h, n)
        x = attn_out.transpose(1, 0, 2).reshape(n, n_embd) @ P[f"l{li}.wo"] + x_res
        x_res = x.copy()
        x_n = rmsnorm_np(x)
        h = x_n @ P[f"l{li}.fc1"]
        x = np.maximum(h, 0) @ P[f"l{li}.fc2"] + x_res
    return x @ P["lm_head"].T, last_stats


# --- 1) Standard attention: materialize the full NxN matrix ---


def standard_attention(Q, K, V, seq_len):
    """
    Classic attention: compute the full attention matrix, write it to memory.
    On a real GPU this writes the NxN matrix to HBM (slow main memory).
    """
    stats = {"hbm_reads": 0, "hbm_writes": 0}
    stats["hbm_reads"] += 2 * n_head * seq_len * head_dim
    att = Q @ K.transpose(0, 2, 1) / math.sqrt(head_dim)
    stats["hbm_writes"] += n_head * seq_len * seq_len
    causal = np.triu(np.ones((seq_len, seq_len)), k=1).astype(bool)
    att = np.where(causal, -1e9, att)
    stats["hbm_reads"] += n_head * seq_len * seq_len
    att_probs = softmax_np(att)
    stats["hbm_writes"] += n_head * seq_len * seq_len
    stats["hbm_reads"] += n_head * seq_len * seq_len + n_head * seq_len * head_dim
    out = att_probs @ V
    stats["hbm_writes"] += n_head * seq_len * head_dim
    return out, stats


# --- 2) Online softmax attention: never store the full NxN matrix ---


def online_softmax_attention(Q, K, V, seq_len):
    """
    Online softmax (Milakov & Gimelshein, 2018): compute attention row by row
    without ever materializing the full NxN attention matrix.

    This is the algorithmic foundation of FlashAttention.
    """
    stats = {"hbm_reads": 0, "hbm_writes": 0}
    out = np.zeros_like(Q)

    for h in range(n_head):
        for i in range(seq_len):
            q_i = Q[h, i]
            stats["hbm_reads"] += head_dim

            running_max = -np.inf
            running_sum = 0.0
            running_out = np.zeros(head_dim)

            for j in range(i + 1):  # causal: only attend to positions <= i
                k_j = K[h, j]
                v_j = V[h, j]
                stats["hbm_reads"] += 2 * head_dim

                score = np.dot(q_i, k_j) / math.sqrt(head_dim)
                new_max = max(running_max, score)
                correction = math.exp(running_max - new_max) if running_max != -np.inf else 0.0
                running_sum = running_sum * correction + math.exp(score - new_max)
                running_out = running_out * correction + math.exp(score - new_max) * v_j
                running_max = new_max

            out[h, i] = running_out / running_sum
            stats["hbm_writes"] += head_dim

    return out, stats


# --- 3) Tiled attention (FlashAttention algorithm) ---


def tiled_attention(Q, K, V, seq_len, block_size_tile=4):
    """
    FlashAttention: tile the computation into blocks.

    Instead of processing one key at a time (online softmax) or the full
    matrix (standard), process BLOCKS of keys at once. Each block fits in
    fast on-chip memory (SRAM).
    """
    stats = {"hbm_reads": 0, "hbm_writes": 0}
    Bc = block_size_tile
    Br = block_size_tile
    out = np.zeros_like(Q)

    for h in range(n_head):
        for i_start in range(0, seq_len, Br):
            i_end = min(i_start + Br, seq_len)
            Qi = Q[h, i_start:i_end]
            block_rows = i_end - i_start
            stats["hbm_reads"] += block_rows * head_dim

            row_max = np.full(block_rows, -np.inf)
            row_sum = np.zeros(block_rows)
            row_out = np.zeros((block_rows, head_dim))

            max_j = i_end  # causal: only attend up to current position
            for j_start in range(0, max_j, Bc):
                j_end = min(j_start + Bc, max_j)
                Kj = K[h, j_start:j_end]
                Vj = V[h, j_start:j_end]
                block_cols = j_end - j_start
                stats["hbm_reads"] += 2 * block_cols * head_dim

                scores = Qi @ Kj.T / math.sqrt(head_dim)

                # Apply causal mask within this block
                for bi in range(block_rows):
                    for bj in range(block_cols):
                        if (j_start + bj) > (i_start + bi):
                            scores[bi, bj] = -1e9

                # Online softmax update across blocks
                block_max = scores.max(axis=-1)
                new_max = np.maximum(row_max, block_max)
                old_correction = np.exp(row_max - new_max)
                exp_scores = np.exp(scores - new_max[:, None])

                row_sum = row_sum * old_correction + exp_scores.sum(axis=-1)
                row_out = row_out * old_correction[:, None] + exp_scores @ Vj
                row_max = new_max

            out[h, i_start:i_end] = row_out / row_sum[:, None]
            stats["hbm_writes"] += block_rows * head_dim

    return out, stats


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Compare all three on the same input
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
sample_tokens = np.array([BOS] + [uchars.index(ch) for ch in docs[0]])[:block_size]
seq_len = len(sample_tokens)

logits_std, stats_std = inference_forward(sample_tokens, standard_attention)
logits_online, stats_online = inference_forward(sample_tokens, online_softmax_attention)
logits_tiled, stats_tiled = inference_forward(sample_tokens, tiled_attention)

diffs = [
    ("online softmax", np.max(np.abs(logits_std - logits_online)), 1e-10),
    ("tiled", np.max(np.abs(logits_std - logits_tiled)), 1e-6),
]
print(f"sequence length: {seq_len}")
for label, diff, tol in diffs:
    print(f"max diff (standard vs {label + '):':<16s} {diff:.2e}")
    assert diff < tol, f"{label} diverged: {diff}"
print("All three produce identical output (within floating-point tolerance).\n")

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Memory operation comparison
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
print("--- memory operation counts (HBM reads + writes) ---\n")

for name, stats, note in [
    ("Standard attention", stats_std, "writes N*N attention matrix to HBM, reads it back for softmax"),
    ("Online softmax", stats_online, "never stores N*N matrix (processes one key at a time)"),
    (
        "Tiled (FlashAttention, block_size=4)",
        stats_tiled,
        "reads K/V in blocks (amortizes HBM access), vectorized compute",
    ),
]:
    r, w = stats["hbm_reads"], stats["hbm_writes"]
    print(f"{name}:\n  HBM reads: {r:>6d}  writes: {w:>6d}  total: {r + w:>6d}")
    print(f"  Key insight: {note}\n")

N, d, nh = seq_len, head_dim, n_head
print(
    f"Summary:\n"
    f"  Standard writes {2 * nh * N * N:>5d} elements for the attention matrix (O(N^2))\n"
    f"  Tiled and online write only {nh * N * d:>5d} elements for the output (O(N*d))\n"
    f"  At GPT-3 scale (N=2048, d=128): that's 16M vs 1M elements per head"
)

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# GPU memory hierarchy context
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
print("""
--- GPU memory hierarchy (why this matters) ---

  Level         Size        Bandwidth       What lives here
  ──────────    ────────    ────────────    ───────────────────────────
  Registers     ~few KB     fastest         Current computation
  SRAM (L1)     ~20 MB      ~19 TB/s       FlashAttention keeps Q,K,V blocks here
  HBM (VRAM)    ~80 GB      ~2 TB/s        Standard attention writes N*N matrix here
  CPU DRAM      ~TBs        ~50 GB/s        Model doesn't fit in GPU? Swap here

Standard attention writes the full N*N attention matrix to HBM — 10x slower
memory. FlashAttention tiles the computation so it stays in SRAM, never
materializing the full matrix. Same math, ~2-4x faster on real hardware.""")

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Inference — generate names using tiled attention
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
temperature = 0.5 # in (0, 1], control the "creativity" of generated text, low to high
print("\n--- inference (tiled attention) ---")
for sample_idx in range(20):
    generated = [BOS]
    sample = []
    for _ in range(block_size):
        logits, _ = inference_forward(np.array(generated), tiled_attention)
        logits = logits[-1] / temperature
        probs = softmax_np(logits.reshape(1, -1))[0]
        token_id = rng.choice(vocab_size, p=probs)
        if token_id == BOS:
            break
        generated.append(token_id)
        sample.append(uchars[token_id])
    print(f"sample {sample_idx + 1:2d}: {''.join(sample)}")

12.text: Standard math for sqrt, exp, and inf used in the hand-written attention algorithms.

13.text: Filesystem utilities for locating the dataset relative to this script's directory.

14.text: Seed-based randomness for shuffling data and sampling during generation, ensuring reproducible results.

17.text: NumPy is the star here, attention algorithms are written in explicit NumPy so every memory access is visible.

18.text: PyTorch handles training only; the interesting attention code is all NumPy.

22.text: Fix Python's random seed for reproducible dataset shuffling.

23.text: NumPy's modern RNG, used for sampling during inference.

25.text: Float64 ensures the three attention implementations match to very high precision, we want to prove they compute the same thing.

30.text: Resolve dataset path relative to this script.

31.text: Download the names dataset if not already cached.

34.text: Download the names dataset from Karpathy's GitHub repo if it's not already cached locally.

38.text: Each line is one name, our training documents.

42.text: Collect every unique character across all names and sort them to create a deterministic character-to-ID mapping.

43.text: BOS token at the end of the character set; vocab = characters + BOS.

49.text: Model hyperparameters: intentionally small so the three attention implementations run quickly and we can compare their memory access counts meaningfully.

50.text: Each attention head works on head_dim = 4 dimensions.

58.text: Normalize by root-mean-square, no learned parameters.

62.text: Standard causal self-attention, used for training only. The NumPy versions below are for inference comparison.

65.text: Q, K, V, and output projections, the four weight matrices of attention.

70.text: Training forward pass uses PyTorch's optimized attention.

71.text: Extract batch size, sequence length, and embedding dimension from the input shape.

72.text: Reshape into multi-head format: (B, n_head, T, head_dim).

73.text: Project to keys and reshape into multi-head format, same as queries above.

74.text: Reshape value vectors into the same multi-head format. Unlike Q and K which compute scores, values carry the content attention retrieves.

75.text: Scaled dot-product scores between queries and keys.

76.text: Causal mask: prevent attending to future positions.

78.text: Softmax + value aggregation in one line, the "standard" way.

79.text: Recombine the independent head outputs into a single vector by projecting through the output weight matrix.

82.text: Transformer block with attention and MLP.

85.text: RMSNorm before attention stabilizes the input scale so the projection matrices see consistent activation magnitudes.

86.text: The causal self-attention sublayer, where each token gathers information from all previous positions via Q/K/V projections.

87.text: RMSNorm before the feed-forward network, ensuring the MLP receives well-scaled inputs regardless of what attention produced.

88.text: MLP up-projection: expand to 4x embedding dim.

89.text: MLP down-projection: back to embedding dim.

91.text: Block forward: attention + MLP with residual connections.

92.text: Add the attention output to the original input via a skip connection, so the layer can only refine, not overwrite.

93.text: Residual around MLP (inline: norm -> expand -> ReLU -> contract).

97.text: Full GPT model, identical architecture to Lab 03.

100.text: Learned dense vectors for each token in the vocabulary, mapping discrete IDs into continuous embedding space.

101.text: Learned dense vectors for each sequence position, telling the model where each token sits in the input.

102.text: RMSNorm applied to the combined embeddings before entering the transformer, stabilizing the first layer's input scale.

103.text: A sequence of transformer blocks stacked on top of each other, progressively refining the token representations.

105.text: Walk every submodule and apply the weight initialization function so all parameters start with well-scaled random values.

109.text: Only initialize Linear and Embedding layers with the small Gaussian. Other module types (like RMSNorm) have no learned weights.

112.text: Forward pass: embed, normalize, transform, predict.

114.text: Combine token and position embeddings, then normalize.

115.text: Feed the normalized embeddings through each transformer block in sequence, progressively building richer representations.

117.text: Final linear layer produces next-token logits.

123.text: Instantiate the PyTorch model for training.

125.text: Adam optimizer, same settings as other labs.

126.text: Train for 1000 gradient steps, which is enough for this small character-level model to learn basic name patterns.

129.text: Pick one training example per step, cycling through the shuffled dataset.

131.text: Truncate the token sequence to fit within the model's positional embedding table and context window.

134.text: Run the model on the input sequence to get next-token predictions at every position.

135.text: Compute the cross-entropy loss: how far the model's predicted probabilities are from the true next tokens.

136.text: Reset all parameter gradients to zero before computing the new backward pass.

137.text: Compute gradients of the loss with respect to every model parameter via backpropagation.

138.text: Reduce the learning rate linearly from its initial value toward zero over training, allowing fine-grained convergence at the end.

139.text: Write the decayed learning rate into every optimizer parameter group so the next step uses the updated value.

141.text: Apply the computed gradients to update all model parameters one step toward lower loss.

150.text: Extract all trained weights to NumPy, from here on, everything is explicit NumPy so we can count every memory access.

151.text: Gather embedding and head weights into a flat dictionary.

152.text: Pull each layer's attention weight matrices (Q, K, V, output) out of the PyTorch model and convert them to NumPy arrays.

153.text: Q, K, V, and output projection matrices.

154.text: Transpose to match NumPy's convention (input on rows, output on columns).

159.text: Numerically stable softmax in NumPy, subtract max to prevent overflow.

160.text: Raise e to each shifted logit value. The prior max-subtraction ensures the largest exponent is 0, preventing numerical overflow.

161.text: Divide each exponentiated value by the total so the output forms a proper probability distribution summing to 1.

164.text: RMSNorm in NumPy, matches the PyTorch version exactly.

174.text: The shared inference forward pass, identical computation, but the attention function is pluggable. This is how we compare the three algorithms with the same surrounding code.

177.text: Will hold memory operation counts from the last attention call.

178.text: Embed tokens + positions, then normalize, same as PyTorch forward pass.

179.text: Walk through each transformer layer in sequence, applying attention and MLP sublayers with residual connections.

180.text: Snapshot the current hidden state before attention modifies it. The residual connection adds this back, preserving the original signal.

181.text: Normalize activations before attention so the Q/K/V projections see inputs with consistent scale across layers.

182.text: Project to Q, K, V and reshape into multi-head format: (n_head, seq_len, head_dim). This is where the three attention implementations diverge.

185.text: Call the pluggable attention function, standard, online, or tiled. Returns output and memory stats.

186.text: Reshape attention output back to (seq_len, n_embd), apply output projection, add residual.

187.text: Snapshot the hidden state before the MLP modifies it. The residual path ensures the MLP can only add refinements.

188.text: Normalize before the feed-forward network so the MLP weights see well-scaled inputs regardless of what attention produced.

189.text: Multiply the normalized hidden state by the first MLP weight matrix, expanding to 4x width.

191.text: Final projection to vocabulary logits. Return logits and memory stats from the last layer's attention.

197.text: Algorithm 1: Standard attention. Materializes the full N*N attention matrix in HBM (slow GPU memory). This is what everyone learned first, and it's the memory bottleneck FlashAttention eliminates.

202.text: Count every read/write to "HBM" (slow memory) to make the cost explicit.

203.text: Read all Q and K vectors from HBM to compute scores.

204.text: The O(N^2) matrix: every query attends to every key. This is the expensive part to store.

205.text: Write the full N*N score matrix to HBM, this is the waste FlashAttention avoids.

206.text: Build the causal mask: upper triangle blocks future positions.

207.text: Replace future-position scores with negative infinity. After softmax, these become zero, enforcing the causal left-to-right constraint.

208.text: Read the N*N matrix back from HBM for softmax, a full round-trip through slow memory.

209.text: Row-wise softmax to get attention weights.

210.text: Write the N*N attention weights back to HBM.

211.text: Read back the weights AND values to compute the final weighted sum.

212.text: Matrix multiply: attention weights * values = output.

213.text: Write the output back to HBM. Total: 2 round-trips of the N*N matrix through slow memory.

220.text: Algorithm 2: Online softmax. Processes one key at a time, never materializing the N*N matrix. The foundation of FlashAttention, same output, far less memory traffic.

227.text: Track HBM operations, this algorithm has zero N*N writes.

228.text: Allocate output buffer, same shape as Q.

230.text: Iterate over each attention head independently, since heads share no state and can be processed in any order.

232.text: Read query vector from HBM once.

233.text: Count the HBM read for this query.

235.text: The online softmax trick: maintain a running max, sum, and weighted output. No need to store all scores first.

236.text: Running sum of exp(scores) for softmax denominator.

237.text: Running weighted sum of values, the attention output, accumulated incrementally.

239.text: Only attend to positions 0..i (causal). Process each key-value pair one at a time.

240.text: Read one K and one V vector from HBM.

242.text: Record the HBM read cost for loading this key and value vector pair.

244.text: Dot the query vector with this key vector, scaled by sqrt(head_dim) to prevent softmax saturation in high dimensions.

245.text: Track the running maximum for numerical stability.

246.text: Correction factor: when the max changes, previous exponentials need rescaling. This is the key insight of online softmax, you can update the normalization incrementally.

247.text: Rescale the running sum and add the new score's contribution.

248.text: Rescale the running output and add the new value's weighted contribution. Same rescaling trick as the sum.

249.text: Update the running max for the next iteration.

251.text: Divide by the final sum to complete the softmax, equivalent to standard softmax, but computed in a single pass without storing N scores.

252.text: Write only the final output to HBM, no N*N matrix ever touches slow memory.

260.text: Algorithm 3: Tiled attention (FlashAttention). The best of both worlds: processes BLOCKS of keys at once (vectorized like standard) but never writes the N*N matrix (memory-efficient like online). This is what runs on real GPUs.

268.text: Track HBM operations, expect fewer reads than online softmax (due to blocking) and same writes.

269.text: Column block size: how many keys to process at once.

270.text: Row block size: how many queries to process at once.

271.text: Allocate the output array with the same shape as Q to store attention results.

273.text: Iterate over each attention head independently, since heads do not interact during attention.

274.text: Tile the query dimension into blocks of size Br.

275.text: Handle the last block which may be smaller.

276.text: Read a block of query vectors from slow HBM memory into fast on-chip SRAM, where all the compute for this tile happens.

277.text: How many rows in this query block.

278.text: Count the HBM read, one block of queries, not one at a time.

280.text: Per-row running max for online softmax within this query block.

281.text: Running sum of exponentiated scores for each query row, used as the softmax denominator. Updated incrementally as new key blocks arrive.

282.text: Running weighted output for each query row, accumulated block by block. After all key blocks, dividing by the sum completes softmax.

284.text: Causal boundary: only attend to keys at or before the query positions.

285.text: Tile the key dimension into blocks of size Bc.

287.text: Read a block of key vectors from HBM into SRAM. Processing keys in blocks amortizes the memory transfer cost over many queries.

288.text: Read the corresponding block of value vectors alongside the keys, since both are needed to update the running output.

289.text: How many columns in this key block.

290.text: Count the K+V block read, amortized over block_cols positions.

292.text: Block matrix multiply: all queries against all keys in this block. This is where the parallelism comes from, much better than one-at-a-time.

295.text: Apply causal mask within this tile. Only positions where key <= query should contribute.

296.text: Walk through every query-key pair in the current tile to apply the causal mask where keys come after queries.

297.text: If the key position is after the query position, mask it out.

298.text: Replace causally invalid scores with a large negative value so softmax gives them zero weight, matching the standard attention mask.

301.text: Online softmax update across blocks, same math as Algorithm 2, but operating on entire blocks at once.

302.text: New running max: element-wise maximum of old max and this block's max.

303.text: Correction factor for previous blocks when the max increases.

304.text: Exponentiate scores relative to the new max.

306.text: Update running sum: rescale old sum, add new block's contribution.

307.text: Update running output: rescale old output, add new weighted values. This is the block version of the online softmax accumulation.

308.text: Update the running max for the next block.

310.text: Normalize by the final sum, completes the softmax computation for this query block.

311.text: Write only the final output block to HBM, the N*N matrix never existed in slow memory.

319.text: Pick one name from the dataset as test input.

320.text: How long is our test sequence.

322.text: Feed the same test input through standard, online, and tiled attention to verify all three produce identical results.

323.text: Online softmax should produce identical results, it's mathematically exact.

324.text: Tiled attention uses block-level operations, tiny floating-point differences possible.

327.text: Online softmax should match to machine precision (1e-10).

328.text: Tiled allows slightly more tolerance due to different summation order.

331.text: Print the actual numerical differences, should be negligibly small.

333.text: Hard assertion: if any algorithm diverges, the implementation is wrong.

334.text: The whole point: same math, different memory access patterns, identical results.

339.text: Now compare how many times each algorithm reads/writes slow HBM memory.

341.text: Print stats for all three algorithms with explanatory notes.

350.text: Pull the read count and write count out of each algorithm's stats dictionary so we can compare memory traffic side by side.

351.text: Total memory operations = reads + writes. Standard attention dominates because of the N*N matrix.

354.text: Calculate the theoretical HBM traffic for each algorithm using the sequence length and head dimensions for the summary table.

355.text: Standard attention writes 2*N*N elements (scores + probabilities) per head, the O(N^2) cost. Tiled and online write only N*d elements (just the output), a dramatic reduction that grows with sequence length.

382.text: Temperature 0.5 for more focused name generation.

384.text: Generate 20 sample names using the tiled (FlashAttention) attention algorithm.

387.text: Generate up to block_size tokens per name.

388.text: Full forward pass with tiled attention for each step.

389.text: Divide logits by temperature before softmax. Lower temperature sharpens the distribution toward the most likely token.

390.text: Convert the temperature-scaled logits into a probability distribution via softmax for sampling the next token.

391.text: Sample from the distribution using NumPy's RNG.

394.text: Extend the token sequence for the next step.

395.text: Convert token ID back to character.

396.text: Display each generated name on its own line so we can visually inspect the model's learned character patterns.

title: Paged KV Cache (PagedAttention)
tagline: Virtual memory paging applied to KV caches, reducing memory waste from 60-80% to near zero.
code:
"""
microGPT — PagedAttention edition.

Same architecture as the pure-Python version (01), but demonstrating two
KV cache strategies for inference: contiguous (wasteful) and paged (efficient).
PagedAttention is the core innovation in vLLM — it applies the operating
system's virtual memory paging concept to KV caches, achieving near-perfect
memory utilization.

Zero dependencies. Pure Python. The algorithms are pure data structures.
"""

import math
import os
import random

random.seed(42)

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Dataset & Tokenizer
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
input_path = os.path.join(os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__)), "..", "data", "input.txt")
if not os.path.exists(input_path):
    import urllib.request

    url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/karpathy/makemore/refs/heads/master/names.txt"
    urllib.request.urlretrieve(url, input_path)

docs = [l.strip() for l in open(input_path).read().strip().split("\n") if l.strip()]
random.shuffle(docs)
print(f"num docs: {len(docs)}")

uchars = sorted(set("".join(docs)))
BOS = len(uchars)
vocab_size = len(uchars) + 1
print(f"vocab size: {vocab_size}")

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Model — reuse Lab 01's Value autograd + GPT architecture
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
n_embd = 16     # embedding dimension
n_head = 4      # number of attention heads
n_layer = 1     # number of layers
block_size = 16 # maximum sequence length
head_dim = n_embd // n_head # dimension of each head


# fmt: off
class Value:
    __slots__ = ('data', 'grad', '_children', '_local_grads') # Python optimization for memory usage

    def __init__(self, data, children=(), local_grads=()):
        self.data = data                # scalar value of this node calculated during forward pass
        self.grad = 0                   # derivative of the loss w.r.t. this node, calculated in backward pass
        self._children = children       # children of this node in the computation graph
        self._local_grads = local_grads # local derivative of this node w.r.t. its children

    def __add__(self, other):
        other = other if isinstance(other, Value) else Value(other)
        return Value(self.data + other.data, (self, other), (1, 1))

    def __mul__(self, other):
        other = other if isinstance(other, Value) else Value(other)
        return Value(self.data * other.data, (self, other), (other.data, self.data))

    def __pow__(self, other): return Value(self.data**other, (self,), (other * self.data**(other-1),))
    def log(self): return Value(math.log(self.data), (self,), (1/self.data,))
    def exp(self): return Value(math.exp(self.data), (self,), (math.exp(self.data),))
    def relu(self): return Value(max(0, self.data), (self,), (float(self.data > 0),))
    def __neg__(self): return self * -1
    def __radd__(self, other): return self + other
    def __sub__(self, other): return self + (-other)
    def __rsub__(self, other): return other + (-self)
    def __rmul__(self, other): return self * other
    def __truediv__(self, other): return self * other**-1
    def __rtruediv__(self, other): return other * self**-1

    def backward(self):
        topo = []
        visited = set()
        def build_topo(v):
            if v not in visited:
                visited.add(v)
                for child in v._children:
                    build_topo(child)
                topo.append(v)
        build_topo(self)
        self.grad = 1
        for v in reversed(topo):
            for child, local_grad in zip(v._children, v._local_grads):
                child.grad += local_grad * v.grad


def matrix(nout, nin, std=0.08):
    return [[Value(random.gauss(0, std)) for _ in range(nin)] for _ in range(nout)]


state_dict = {
    "wte": matrix(vocab_size, n_embd),
    "wpe": matrix(block_size, n_embd),
    "lm_head": matrix(vocab_size, n_embd),
}
for i in range(n_layer):
    for name in ("attn_wq", "attn_wk", "attn_wv", "attn_wo"):
        state_dict[f"layer{i}.{name}"] = matrix(n_embd, n_embd)
    state_dict[f"layer{i}.mlp_fc1"] = matrix(4 * n_embd, n_embd)
    state_dict[f"layer{i}.mlp_fc2"] = matrix(n_embd, 4 * n_embd)
params = [p for mat in state_dict.values() for row in mat for p in row]
print(f"num params: {len(params)}")


def linear(x, w):
    return [sum(wi * xi for wi, xi in zip(wo, x)) for wo in w]


def softmax(logits):
    if isinstance(logits[0], Value):
        max_val = max(val.data for val in logits)
        exps = [(val - max_val).exp() for val in logits]
    else:
        max_val = max(logits)
        exps = [math.exp(l - max_val) for l in logits]
    total = sum(exps)
    return [e / total for e in exps]


def rmsnorm(x):
    scale = (sum(xi * xi for xi in x) / len(x) + 1e-5) ** -0.5
    return [xi * scale for xi in x]


def relu(x):
    if isinstance(x[0], Value):
        return [xi.relu() for xi in x]
    return [max(0.0, xi) for xi in x]


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# KV Cache Managers
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------


class ContiguousKVCache:
    """Standard KV cache: pre-allocate max_seq_len slots per layer."""

    def __init__(self, max_seq_len, n_layers, dims):
        self.max_seq_len = max_seq_len
        self.n_layers = n_layers
        self.dims = dims
        self.k_cache = [[[0.0] * dims for _ in range(max_seq_len)] for _ in range(n_layers)]
        self.v_cache = [[[0.0] * dims for _ in range(max_seq_len)] for _ in range(n_layers)]
        self.length = 0
        self.allocated = n_layers * max_seq_len * dims * 2
        self.label = "contiguous"

    def new_sequence(self, seq_id=0):
        self.length = 0

    def append(self, seq_id, layer, k, v):
        self.k_cache[layer][self.length] = k
        self.v_cache[layer][self.length] = v
        if layer == self.n_layers - 1:
            self.length += 1

    def get_kv(self, seq_id, layer):
        return self.k_cache[layer][: self.length], self.v_cache[layer][: self.length]

    def utilization(self):
        used = self.length * self.n_layers * self.dims * 2
        return used / self.allocated if self.allocated > 0 else 0


BLOCK_SIZE_TOKENS = 4


class PagedKVCache:
    """
    Paged KV cache (PagedAttention / vLLM concept).
    Allocate fixed-size blocks on demand. A block table maps logical positions
    to physical block IDs — just like page tables in an operating system.
    """

    def __init__(self, total_blocks, n_layers, dims, block_size_tokens=BLOCK_SIZE_TOKENS):
        self.block_size = block_size_tokens
        self.n_layers = n_layers
        self.dims = dims
        self.total_blocks = total_blocks
        self.k_blocks = [[[0.0] * dims for _ in range(block_size_tokens)] for _ in range(total_blocks)]
        self.v_blocks = [[[0.0] * dims for _ in range(block_size_tokens)] for _ in range(total_blocks)]
        self.free_blocks = list(range(total_blocks))
        self.block_tables = {}
        self.seq_lengths = {}
        self.alloc_events = []
        self.label = "paged"

    def _alloc_block(self):
        if not self.free_blocks:
            raise RuntimeError("Out of physical blocks!")
        return self.free_blocks.pop()

    def new_sequence(self, seq_id):
        self.block_tables[seq_id] = {li: [] for li in range(self.n_layers)}
        self.seq_lengths[seq_id] = 0

    def append(self, seq_id, layer, k, v):
        length = self.seq_lengths[seq_id]
        logical_block = length // self.block_size
        slot_in_block = length % self.block_size
        if logical_block >= len(self.block_tables[seq_id][layer]):
            phys_id = self._alloc_block()
            self.block_tables[seq_id][layer].append(phys_id)
            self.alloc_events.append(
                f"  seq {seq_id}, layer {layer}, logical block {logical_block} -> physical block {phys_id}"
            )
        phys_id = self.block_tables[seq_id][layer][logical_block]
        self.k_blocks[phys_id][slot_in_block] = k
        self.v_blocks[phys_id][slot_in_block] = v
        if layer == self.n_layers - 1:
            self.seq_lengths[seq_id] = length + 1

    def get_kv(self, seq_id, layer):
        length = self.seq_lengths[seq_id]
        ks, vs = [], []
        for pos in range(length):
            logical_block = pos // self.block_size
            slot = pos % self.block_size
            phys_id = self.block_tables[seq_id][layer][logical_block]
            ks.append(self.k_blocks[phys_id][slot])
            vs.append(self.v_blocks[phys_id][slot])
        return ks, vs

    def free_sequence(self, seq_id):
        for layer_blocks in self.block_tables[seq_id].values():
            self.free_blocks.extend(layer_blocks)
        del self.block_tables[seq_id]
        del self.seq_lengths[seq_id]

    def utilization(self):
        used = self.total_blocks - len(self.free_blocks)
        return used / self.total_blocks if self.total_blocks > 0 else 0

    def share_prefix(self, src_seq_id, dst_seq_id, prefix_len):
        """Copy-on-write prefix sharing: point dst's block table at src's physical blocks."""
        self.new_sequence(dst_seq_id)
        n_shared_blocks = -(-prefix_len // self.block_size)  # ceiling division
        shared = 0
        for layer in range(self.n_layers):
            for b in range(min(n_shared_blocks, len(self.block_tables[src_seq_id][layer]))):
                phys_id = self.block_tables[src_seq_id][layer][b]
                self.block_tables[dst_seq_id][layer].append(phys_id)
                shared += 1
        self.seq_lengths[dst_seq_id] = prefix_len
        return shared


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# GPT forward pass (unified for training and inference)
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------


def gpt(token_id, pos_id, cache, seq_id=0, W=None):
    if W is None:
        W = state_dict
    x = [t + p for t, p in zip(W["wte"][token_id], W["wpe"][pos_id])]
    x = rmsnorm(x)
    for li in range(n_layer):
        x_res = x
        x = rmsnorm(x)
        q, k, v = (linear(x, W[f"layer{li}.attn_w{n}"]) for n in ("q", "k", "v"))
        cache.append(seq_id, li, k, v)
        all_k, all_v = cache.get_kv(seq_id, li)
        x_attn = []
        for h in range(n_head):
            hs = h * head_dim
            q_h = q[hs : hs + head_dim]
            k_h = [ki[hs : hs + head_dim] for ki in all_k]
            v_h = [vi[hs : hs + head_dim] for vi in all_v]
            scores = [sum(q_h[j] * k_h[t][j] for j in range(head_dim)) / head_dim**0.5 for t in range(len(k_h))]
            weights = softmax(scores)
            head_out = [sum(weights[t] * v_h[t][j] for t in range(len(v_h))) for j in range(head_dim)]
            x_attn.extend(head_out)
        x = linear(x_attn, W[f"layer{li}.attn_wo"])
        x = [a + b for a, b in zip(x, x_res)]
        x_res = x
        x = rmsnorm(x)
        x = linear(x, W[f"layer{li}.mlp_fc1"])
        x = relu(x)
        x = linear(x, W[f"layer{li}.mlp_fc2"])
        x = [a + b for a, b in zip(x, x_res)]
    return linear(x, W["lm_head"])


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Training (identical to Lab 01, but using ContiguousKVCache)
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
learning_rate, beta1, beta2, eps_adam = 0.01, 0.85, 0.99, 1e-8
m = [0.0] * len(params)
v = [0.0] * len(params)
num_steps = 1000

for step in range(num_steps):
    doc = docs[step % len(docs)]
    tokens = [BOS] + [uchars.index(ch) for ch in doc] + [BOS]
    n = min(block_size, len(tokens) - 1)
    cache = ContiguousKVCache(block_size, n_layer, n_embd)
    cache.new_sequence()
    losses = []
    for pos_id in range(n):
        token_id, target_id = tokens[pos_id], tokens[pos_id + 1]
        logits = gpt(token_id, pos_id, cache)
        probs = softmax(logits)
        losses.append(-probs[target_id].log())
    loss = (1 / n) * sum(losses)
    loss.backward()
    lr_t = learning_rate * (1 - step / num_steps)
    for i, p in enumerate(params):
        m[i] = beta1 * m[i] + (1 - beta1) * p.grad
        v[i] = beta2 * v[i] + (1 - beta2) * p.grad**2
        m_hat = m[i] / (1 - beta1 ** (step + 1))
        v_hat = v[i] / (1 - beta2 ** (step + 1))
        p.data -= lr_t * m_hat / (v_hat**0.5 + eps_adam)
        p.grad = 0
    if (step + 1) % 10 == 0 or step == 0:
        print(f"step {step + 1:4d} / {num_steps:4d} | loss {loss.data:.4f}")

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Inference setup
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
W = {name: [[v.data for v in row] for row in mat] for name, mat in state_dict.items()}


def generate(cache, seq_id=0, max_len=block_size, temperature=0.5):
    cache.new_sequence(seq_id)
    token_id, sample = BOS, []
    for pos_id in range(max_len):
        logits = gpt(token_id, pos_id, cache, seq_id=seq_id, W=W)
        probs = softmax([l / temperature for l in logits])
        token_id = random.choices(range(vocab_size), weights=probs)[0]
        if token_id == BOS:
            break
        sample.append(token_id)
    return sample, cache


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Compare contiguous vs paged KV cache
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
print("\n--- contiguous vs paged KV cache ---\n")

cache_c = ContiguousKVCache(block_size, n_layer, n_embd)
random.seed(100)
sample_c, _ = generate(cache_c)
name_c = "".join(uchars[t] for t in sample_c)
used_c = cache_c.length * n_layer * n_embd * 2
print(f"contiguous: '{name_c}' ({cache_c.length} tokens)")
print(f"  allocated {cache_c.allocated} slots, used {used_c} ({cache_c.utilization():.0%} utilization)")

total_blocks = 32
paged = PagedKVCache(total_blocks, n_layer, n_embd)
random.seed(100)
sample_p, _ = generate(paged, seq_id=0)
name_p = "".join(uchars[t] for t in sample_p)
print(f"paged:      '{name_p}' ({paged.seq_lengths[0]} tokens)")
print(f"  {total_blocks - len(paged.free_blocks)} blocks allocated on demand ({paged.utilization():.0%} utilization)")
assert name_c == name_p, f"Outputs differ: {name_c} vs {name_p}"
print("  -> identical output, paged allocates only what's needed")

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Prefix sharing — shared system prompt
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
print("\n--- prefix sharing (shared system prompt) ---\n")

prefix_cache = PagedKVCache(total_blocks=64, n_layers=n_layer, dims=n_embd)
prefix_tokens = [BOS] + [uchars.index(ch) for ch in "an"]
prefix_cache.new_sequence("prefix")
for pos_id, token_id in enumerate(prefix_tokens):
    gpt(token_id, pos_id, prefix_cache, seq_id="prefix", W=W)

prefix_len = prefix_cache.seq_lengths["prefix"]
used_blk = prefix_cache.total_blocks - len(prefix_cache.free_blocks)
print(f"prefix 'an' cached: {prefix_len} tokens, {used_blk} blocks")

n_shared = 3
for i in range(n_shared):
    shared = prefix_cache.share_prefix("prefix", f"req_{i}", prefix_len)
    print(f"  req_{i}: shared {shared} blocks (zero-copy)")
print(f"blocks: {used_blk} total — sharing adds zero new allocations")
print(f"without sharing: {used_blk * (1 + n_shared)} blocks ({used_blk} x {1 + n_shared})")

print("""
At production scale (Llama 3 70B, 8K context, 100 requests):
contiguous needs ~200 GB, paged needs ~30-60 GB, prefix sharing saves
another 30-50%. This is why vLLM achieves 2-4x higher throughput.
""")

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Inference — generate names (using paged KV cache)
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
print("--- inference (paged KV cache) ---")
final_cache = PagedKVCache(total_blocks=128, n_layers=n_layer, dims=n_embd)
for sample_idx in range(20):
    random.seed(42 + sample_idx)
    sample, _ = generate(final_cache, seq_id=f"sample_{sample_idx}")
    name = "".join(uchars[t] for t in sample)
    print(f"sample {sample_idx + 1:2d}: {name}")
    final_cache.free_sequence(f"sample_{sample_idx}")

176.text: PagedKVCache: the core innovation behind vLLM. Instead of pre-allocating max_seq_len per sequence, allocate small fixed-size blocks on demand. A block table maps logical positions to physical block IDs, exactly like OS page tables map virtual addresses to physical frames.

315.text: Reduce the learning rate linearly toward zero as training progresses, allowing fine-grained convergence at the end.

371.text: Section header for prefix sharing demo.

376.text: Run each prefix token through the model to populate the KV cache.

title: Soft Thinking
tagline: Instead of collapsing to one token per step, pass a probability-weighted blend forward, preserving information.
code:
"""
microGPT — Soft thinking edition.

Same architecture as the PyTorch version (03), but with soft decoding at
inference time. Instead of collapsing to a single token at each step, soft
thinking passes a "concept token" — a probability-weighted blend of all
token embeddings — to the next step. The full distribution flows forward,
preserving information that hard decoding discards.

  Hard:  logits -> sample -> embed(token)            -> next input
  Soft:  logits -> softmax(logits/T) @ embed_table   -> next input
"""

import math
import os
import random

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F

random.seed(42)
torch.manual_seed(42)

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Dataset & Tokenizer
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
input_path = os.path.join(os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__)), "..", "data", "input.txt")
if not os.path.exists(input_path):
    import urllib.request

    url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/karpathy/makemore/refs/heads/master/names.txt"
    urllib.request.urlretrieve(url, input_path)

docs = [l.strip() for l in open(input_path).read().strip().split("\n") if l.strip()]
random.shuffle(docs)
print(f"num docs: {len(docs)}")

uchars = sorted(set("".join(docs)))
BOS = len(uchars)
vocab_size = len(uchars) + 1
print(f"vocab size: {vocab_size}")

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Model (same as Lab 03, with inputs_embeds support for concept tokens)
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
n_embd = 16     # embedding dimension
n_head = 4      # number of attention heads
n_layer = 1     # number of layers
block_size = 16 # maximum sequence length
head_dim = n_embd // n_head # dimension of each head
device = "cpu"


class RMSNorm(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, _dim, eps=1e-5):
        super().__init__()
        self.eps = eps

    def forward(self, x):
        return x * torch.rsqrt(x.pow(2).mean(-1, keepdim=True) + self.eps)


class CausalSelfAttention(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.wq = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)
        self.wk = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)
        self.wv = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)
        self.wo = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)

    def forward(self, x):
        B, T, C = x.shape
        q = self.wq(x).view(B, T, n_head, head_dim).transpose(1, 2)
        k = self.wk(x).view(B, T, n_head, head_dim).transpose(1, 2)
        v = self.wv(x).view(B, T, n_head, head_dim).transpose(1, 2)
        att = (q @ k.transpose(-2, -1)) / math.sqrt(head_dim)
        mask = torch.triu(torch.ones(T, T, device=x.device), diagonal=1).bool()
        att = att.masked_fill(mask, float("-inf"))
        att = F.softmax(att, dim=-1)
        out = (att @ v).transpose(1, 2).reshape(B, T, C)
        return self.wo(out)


class MLP(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.fc1 = nn.Linear(n_embd, 4 * n_embd, bias=False)
        self.fc2 = nn.Linear(4 * n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)

    def forward(self, x):
        return self.fc2(F.relu(self.fc1(x)))


class Block(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.norm1 = RMSNorm(n_embd)
        self.attn = CausalSelfAttention()
        self.norm2 = RMSNorm(n_embd)
        self.mlp = MLP()

    def forward(self, x):
        x = x + self.attn(self.norm1(x))
        x = x + self.mlp(self.norm2(x))
        return x


class MicroGPT(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.wte = nn.Embedding(vocab_size, n_embd)
        self.wpe = nn.Embedding(block_size, n_embd)
        self.norm_in = RMSNorm(n_embd)
        self.layers = nn.ModuleList([Block() for _ in range(n_layer)])
        self.lm_head = nn.Linear(n_embd, vocab_size, bias=False)
        self.apply(self._init_weights)

    @staticmethod
    def _init_weights(module):
        if isinstance(module, (nn.Linear, nn.Embedding)):
            nn.init.normal_(module.weight, mean=0.0, std=0.08)

    def forward(self, idx=None, inputs_embeds=None):
        tok_emb = self.wte(idx) if idx is not None else inputs_embeds
        assert tok_emb is not None, "provide idx or inputs_embeds"
        T = tok_emb.shape[1]
        pos_emb = self.wpe(torch.arange(T, device=tok_emb.device))
        x = self.norm_in(tok_emb + pos_emb)
        for layer in self.layers:
            x = layer(x)
        return self.lm_head(x)


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Shared utilities (also used by Lab 21)
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
num_steps = 1000


@torch.no_grad()
def generate(model, mode="hard", soft_temp=1.0, temperature=0.5):
    """Generate a name using hard (discrete) or soft (concept token) decoding."""
    model.eval()
    tokens, entropies = [], []
    embeds = model.wte(torch.tensor([[BOS]], device=device))

    for _ in range(block_size):
        logits = model(inputs_embeds=embeds)[0, -1]

        # Sample a discrete token (for output and stopping)
        probs = F.softmax(logits / temperature, dim=-1)
        token_id = torch.multinomial(probs, 1).item()
        if token_id == BOS:
            break
        tokens.append(token_id)

        # Track entropy: how spread is the distribution?
        entropies.append(-(probs * probs.clamp(min=1e-10).log()).sum().item())

        # Next input: discrete embedding or concept token
        if mode == "hard":
            next_emb = model.wte(torch.tensor([[token_id]], device=device))
        else:
            # Concept token: probability-weighted blend of ALL token embeddings
            soft_probs = F.softmax(logits / soft_temp, dim=-1)
            next_emb = (soft_probs @ model.wte.weight).view(1, 1, -1)

        embeds = torch.cat([embeds, next_emb], dim=1)[:, -block_size:]

    return tokens, entropies


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Training & Inference (runs only when executed directly)
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
if __name__ == "__main__":
    model = MicroGPT().to(device)
    print(f"num params: {sum(p.numel() for p in model.parameters())}")

    # Training — identical to Lab 03
    optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=1e-2, betas=(0.85, 0.99), eps=1e-8)

    for step in range(num_steps):
        doc = docs[step % len(docs)]
        tokens = [BOS] + [uchars.index(ch) for ch in doc] + [BOS]
        n = min(block_size, len(tokens) - 1)

        input_ids = torch.tensor([tokens[:n]], device=device)
        targets = torch.tensor([tokens[1 : n + 1]], device=device)

        logits = model(input_ids)
        loss = F.cross_entropy(logits.view(-1, vocab_size), targets.view(-1))

        optimizer.zero_grad()
        loss.backward()
        lr_t = 1e-2 * (1 - step / num_steps)
        for pg in optimizer.param_groups:
            pg["lr"] = lr_t
        optimizer.step()
        if (step + 1) % 10 == 0 or step == 0:
            print(f"step {step + 1:4d} / {num_steps:4d} | loss {loss.item():.4f}")

    # Inference — hard vs. soft decoding at different temperatures
    print("\n--- soft thinking comparison ---\n")
    n_samples = 20
    max_H = math.log(vocab_size)

    for mode, soft_temp, label in [
        ("hard", 1.0, "hard (standard decoding)"),
        ("soft", 0.5, "soft T=0.5 (mild blend)"),
        ("soft", 1.0, "soft T=1.0 (moderate blend)"),
        ("soft", 2.0, "soft T=2.0 (diffuse blend)"),
    ]:
        print(f"{label}:")
        all_H = []
        for i in range(n_samples):
            toks, ents = generate(model, mode, soft_temp)
            name = "".join(uchars[t] for t in toks)
            avg_H = sum(ents) / len(ents) if ents else 0
            all_H.append(avg_H)
            if i < 10:
                print(f"  {i + 1:2d}: {name:<15s} entropy {avg_H:.2f}/{max_H:.2f}")
        print(f"  -> mean entropy: {sum(all_H) / len(all_H):.2f}/{max_H:.2f}\n")

    print(f"""--- what's happening ---

Standard (hard) decoding collapses the model's rich output distribution to a
single token ID at every step. The next step sees only one embedding — all
information about what the model "almost said" is discarded.

Soft thinking preserves this information:

  concept_token = softmax(logits / T) @ embedding_table

The concept token is a {n_embd}-dimensional vector blending all {vocab_size} token
embeddings, weighted by their probability. It lives in the same embedding space
as regular tokens but encodes the model's full uncertainty.

Temperature (T) controls the softness:
  T -> 0:  concept token = argmax embedding (hard, no benefit)
  T = 1:   standard softmax (moderate blending)
  T -> inf: uniform weights (noise — all tokens equally blended)

The entropy column shows how "spread" each distribution is. Higher entropy
means more tokens contribute to the concept token — richer information, but
higher risk of out-of-distribution drift. Max entropy = ln({vocab_size}) = {max_H:.2f}.

This is training-free — no model weights change. The Soft Thinking paper
(Zhang et al., 2025) shows this improves reasoning in large models by
+2.5 pass@1 while using 22% fewer tokens.
""")

16.text: Seed-based randomness for shuffling training data and sampling during generation, ensuring reproducible runs.

19.text: Provides neural network building blocks like Linear and Embedding used to construct the transformer layers.

20.text: Softmax, cross-entropy, and crucially, the softmax that creates concept tokens.

22.text: Fix seeds for reproducible comparisons between hard and soft decoding.

29.text: If the names dataset doesn't exist locally yet, download it on the fly so the script is self-contained.

35.text: Read the dataset file where each line contains a single name for training.

36.text: Randomize the order of training names so the model doesn't memorize alphabetical patterns from the dataset.

39.text: Build a sorted list of unique characters in the dataset, mapping each to a deterministic token ID from 0 to n-1.

40.text: The BOS token marks the beginning and end of each name sequence during training and generation.

47.text: Small model, same architecture as Lab 03, but with one crucial addition: the forward pass accepts pre-computed embeddings, not just token IDs.

48.text: Split the embedding dimension across four heads for multi-head attention.

49.text: A single transformer layer keeps the model small and training fast for this demo.

50.text: Maximum sequence length of 16 tokens, matching the longest names in the dataset.

51.text: Each attention head operates on a 4-dimensional slice of the full embedding.

52.text: Run everything on CPU since this small model does not need GPU acceleration.

55.text: RMSNorm, same as all previous labs.

60.text: Scale the input by its inverse root-mean-square, bringing activations to roughly unit magnitude without learned parameters.

67.text: Q, K, V, and output projections.

72.text: Run the complete attention computation: project inputs to Q/K/V, score, mask future positions, blend values, and project output.

73.text: Extract batch size, sequence length, and embedding dimension from the input shape.

74.text: Reshape the query projection into separate heads so each head independently attends to its own slice of the embedding.

75.text: Project to keys and reshape into multi-head format, same as queries above.

76.text: Project to values and reshape into multi-head format, same as queries above.

77.text: Compute Q dot K transposed, scaled by sqrt(head_dim). The scaling prevents softmax from saturating as dimensionality grows.

78.text: Causal mask, no peeking at future tokens.

79.text: Set future positions to negative infinity so softmax gives them zero attention weight.

82.text: Mix information across heads by projecting the concatenated output back to embedding dimension.

85.text: The feed-forward network that processes each token independently after attention has mixed information.

89.text: Project the expanded representation back to the model's embedding dimension, compressing the MLP's "thinking space" output.

92.text: Apply ReLU activation between the two linear layers, introducing the needed nonlinearity.

95.text: One transformer block combining attention and MLP sublayers with residual connections.

98.text: RMSNorm before attention ensures the Q/K/V projections see inputs with consistent scale, stabilizing training.

99.text: The multi-head causal self-attention sublayer that lets tokens attend to prior positions.

100.text: RMSNorm before the MLP ensures the feed-forward weights see well-scaled inputs regardless of what attention produced.

101.text: The feed-forward network sublayer that processes each token independently after attention.

104.text: Add the attention output to the original input via a skip connection, so this layer can only refine, not overwrite.

105.text: Add the MLP output via a second skip connection, preserving gradient flow and ensuring the layer is an incremental refinement.

109.text: The model, now with a key difference from earlier labs.

112.text: Token embedding table, this does double duty in soft thinking: it maps token IDs to embeddings AND is the table we blend against to create concept tokens.

113.text: Learned position vectors that tell the model where each token sits in the sequence. Added to token embeddings before the transformer.

114.text: RMSNorm applied to the combined embeddings before the transformer stack, stabilizing the first layer's input scale.

115.text: The stack of transformer blocks that form the core of the model's processing pipeline.

117.text: Walk every submodule and apply the weight initialization function so all learned parameters start with well-scaled random values.

121.text: Small normal init for all weights.

124.text: The critical change: forward accepts either token IDs OR pre-computed embeddings. This is what enables soft thinking, we can feed in concept tokens (weighted blends) instead of discrete token embeddings.

126.text: Safety check: must provide one or the other.

132.text: Map the final hidden states to a score for every vocabulary token, producing the logits for next-token prediction.

141.text: No gradients during generation, pure inference.

144.text: Disable dropout and other training-specific behaviors. Not strictly needed here (no dropout), but good practice for generation.

162.text: Hard decoding: collapse to a single token, throw away the distribution. Standard approach.

165.text: Soft decoding: preserve the full distribution as a "concept token."

The concept token lives in the same embedding space as regular tokens, but encodes the full uncertainty of what the model "wanted to say."

169.text: Append the new embedding (hard or soft) and truncate to context window. The model now sees concept tokens as part of its history.

198.text: Write the decayed learning rate into the optimizer so the next parameter update uses the reduced step size.
